Daily Template 6/25

To:

staff@greenlightamerica.org

Subject

Daily Clean Energy Clips 06/25/2026

Northeast

nothing to see here, folks

Mid-Atlantic

nothing to see here, folks

OH/PA

PENNSYLVANIA

Residents voice concerns about Mora Solar permit in Carbon County “Residents in Carbon County voicing concerns about a proposed wastewater discharge permit tied to a solar energy project near Lake Harmony. A public hearing was held by the Department of Environmental Protection tonight and Jim Thorpe to discuss the pollutant discharge system permit submitted for Moura Solar LLC…Residents at the hearing raised concerns about pollution to creeks, wildlife habitats, and the destruction of forest for construction. ” [WNEP, 6/23/26, link to video]

Southeast

ALABAMA

Controversial 2,000-acre solar farm in Stockton may avoid new rules: Here’s why “A controversial Stockton solar farm project would likely receive grandfathered status if residents vote next week to adopt zoning regulations, according to Baldwin County officials. That’s because developers submitted a completed application in advance of the zoning referendum. With only six days remaining before the special zoning referendum in Stockton, Jessica Waters, a spokesperson for Baldwin County, confirmed that a proposed $350 million, 2,000-acre solar farm will be exempt from zoning laws that could restrict its development, should the referendum pass.” [AL.com, 6/24/26, link to article]

Fort Payne hosts public hearing on data centers “The Fort Payne Planning Commission held a public hearing to gather feedback from residents after the city approved a six-month moratorium on data centers and battery energy storage systems last week.According to the Planning Commission, no company has approached the city about building a data center. Brown said the possibility of a battery energy storage system has been introduced. However, officials say they want to establish rules now rather than be forced to react later. ” [Yahoo News, 6/24/26, link to article]

MISSISSIPPI

County continues to study ways to add teeth to solar, wind farm contracts “The action came amid more discussion on what authority the county has to regulate wind and solar projects and to limit data centers in the county. State law now requires county ordinances to follow state building standards, preventing a county from being more strict that state regulations…Following a public hearing, the amended county wind ordinance was approved, aligning it with state regulations. The board postponed action on the special use permit for two solar projects…The owners had canceled and then resubmitted their applications to be sure they met county regulations after the county amended its solar ordinance last month.” [Mississippi Valley, 6/24/26, link to article]

Midwest

MICHIGAN

Zoning amendment for proposed Wexford solar farm enters formal review stage “A proposed solar farm in Wexford Township is moving one step closer to consideration. The Wexford Joint Planning Commission has officially begun reviewing a zoning ordinance amendment tied to the project and is asking for more public input. The proposed shipyard solar project would span property in both Wexford and Grand Traverse counties. During Monday’s meeting, the commission voted to begin the formal review process and scheduled a public hearing after dozens of locals raised concerns.” [UpNorthLive, 6/23/26, link to article]

ILLINOIS

Illinois put community solar on a 150-year-old coal mine “An old coal mine in Illinois is now generating community solar power for hundreds of households and businesses. Nexamp and TurningPoint Energy have completed two community solar projects on a reclaimed coal mine site in Woodford County, Illinois. Together, the solar farms can generate 9.8 megawatts of power and use nearly 17,000 solar panels spread across roughly 40 acres. The electricity flows directly to ComEd’s grid, and Nexamp says all the solar modules were manufactured in the US. ” [Electrek, 6/17/26, link to article]

North-Central

MISSOURI

Jackson County Legislature approves 180-day data center moratorium “In an unanimous vote Monday, the Jackson County Legislature approved a 180-day moratorium on data centers and battery storage systems in unincorporated areas of the county…Six legislators’ terms will be up in December, meaning a future legislative body will decide what to do after the moratorium concludes.” [FOX 4, 6/22/26, link to article]

Central-Central

nothing to see here, folks

South-Central

OKLAHOMA

Persica urges judge to grant permit for solar farm near Porter – Oklahoma Energy Today “Nearly one year after solar farm developer Persica Solar, LLC filed suit following a vote of Wagoner County Commissioners to deny a conditional use permit to construct a $336 million solar array, the company has filed a trial brief asking the courts to issue the permit.“Persica has unquestionably satisfied the requirements for a conditional use permit to construct a solar energy farm on agricultural property in Wagoner County and the only question before this Court would be “whether the board of adjustment was possessed of discretionary authority to create additional conditions, and if imposed, whether they were reasonable,” argued Roth in the June 16 filing. ” [Solar Now, 6/24/26, link to article]

Daily Template 6/24

To:

staff@greenlightamerica.org

Subject

Daily Clean Energy Clips 06/24/2026

Northeast

Is New England’s new hydropower transmission line paying off? “When the New England Clean Energy Connect transmission line started carrying electricity from Canada into Maine in January, supporters hailed the project as a triumph for renewable power. Now, after nearly six months of operations, the early numbers raise questions about whether the project will be able to advance the region’s energy transition as much as advertised. Energy flow into New England is up just marginally, and there have been roughly 27 days when no power at all traveled along the new line, commonly called NECEC. If current trends hold, New England will receive less hydropower this year over two transmission lines than it did over just one line in 2023 and previous years.” [Canary Media, 6/16/26, link to article]

CONNECTICUT

Connecticut’s solar expansion is colliding with concerns over forests and farmland “As Connecticut pushes for more solar power, the state is increasingly forced to balance renewable energy goals against the protection of forests, wetlands and farmland. Connecticut has committed to a carbon-free electric grid by 2040. But the state’s clean-air ambitions force difficult land-use choices as developers search for places to build solar projects. Protecting forests often means placing greater development pressure on farmland, and vice versa. ” [CT Insider, 6/21/26, link to article]

Mid-Atlantic

DELAWARE

AG celebrates win for clean energy in Delaware “Delaware Attorney General Kathy Jennings this week applauded the Delaware Supreme Court’s recent decision to uphold state legislation allowing the construction of an electrical substation by Renewable Redevelopment LLC, in association with a proposed offshore wind project designed to bring cleaner and cheaper energy to Delawareans, she said. The Delaware Supreme Court’s ruling represents the elimination of a major obstacle in the path for the project, which continues to face federal hurdles, Jennings noted.” [Coastal Point, 6/19/26, link to article]

VIRGINIA

Dominion-NextEra merger raises questions for Virginia energy customers “A proposed merger between Dominion Energy and NextEra would create one of the largest utilities in the country, and some state lawmakers and regulators are already raising questions about what the deal could mean for Virginia customers.The company expects to file its formal merger application in the third quarter. After that, the State Corporation Commission, or SCC, will have up to 180 days to decide whether to approve the deal. One of the biggest questions raised during the hearing was whether the SCC has enough time to fully review a merger of this size.” [WTVR, 6/21/26, link to article]

OH/PA

OHIO

New Ohio bill could hamstring big wind and solar farms even more “[S]tate lawmakers are advancing a bill that would expand preferences for natural gas and nuclear generation while adding even more hurdles for solar and wind — energy sources that the state has already stymied over the last decade. On June 10, the Republican-dominated Ohio Senate voted along straight party lines to pass Senate Bill 294…and calls for electricity generation to ​“employ affordable, reliable, and clean energy sources.” Louisiana and Utah have passed similar laws, and bills are also under consideration in Arizona, New Hampshire, New Jersey, and West Virginia, according to an April report from ALEC.” [Canary Media, 6/23/26, link to article]

Southeast

nothing to see here, folks

Midwest

MICHIGAN

Michigan utility Consumers Energy wins nod for 1.3 GW of solar PPAs “Michigan energy provider Consumers Energy has secured regulatory approval to sign power off-take deals that will add 1,269 MW of new solar photovoltaic (PV) capacity to the Michigan electric grid. Specifically, Consumers Energy will buy the entire output of a 150-MW solar park in Illinois’ Clark County under a deal with Moonshine Solar Park LLC, while another contract with Hectate Energy will make it the power off-taker of the 499-MW Sunfish solar complex in Calhoun County, Michigan. The utility will also sign PPAs with Acceleration Solar LLC, Portside Solar LLC, Hawkeye Solar LLC and Hawkeye Solar II LLC that will enable it buy the entire output of the 90-MW Acceleration, 80-MW Portside, 200-MW Hawkeye and 250-MW Hawkeye Solar II projects in Iowa. ” [Renewables Now, 6/23/26, link to article]

North-Central

WISCONSIN

Kenosha County weighs battery storage moratorium next month “In July the Kenosha County Board will consider codifying a one-year moratorium on the approval of battery energy storage systems across all county jurisdictions.” [Kenosha News, 6/18/26, link to article]  [link to bypass paywall]

Advisory resolution related to local control of solar, wind projects approved “The Washington County Land Use & Planning Committee approved an advisory resolution related to the regulation of solar and wind projects during its meeting Thursday.The purpose of the advisory resolution is to request that the Wisconsin Legislature establish policies to give local elected bodies greater control in the regulation of large solar and wind projects so that they have a say in preserving farmland.” [GGMToday, 6/23/26, link to article] [link to bypass paywall]

Central-Central

nothing to see here, folks

South-Central

KANSAS

Two Kansas solar farms would convert nearly 3,000 acres of farmland near Wichita “Sedgwick County officials are considering approval for two massive solar farms just southwest of Wichita. The renewable energy projects would help power an expected surge in energy demand fueled largely by an impending hyperscale data-center boom to support the rapid expansion of artificial intelligence.” [The Witchita Eagle, 6/20/26, link to article]

Plains

nothing see here, folks

Pacific Northwest

WASHINGTON

Big battery farms encounter resistance across western Washington “Community opposition to big battery farms is spreading across western Washington just as the technology takes on growing importance for the state’s clean energy transition and to stabilize the electric grid. A multiplying number of cities and counties have enacted moratoriums on permitting for new utility-scale battery storage systems over the past year. Over a similar timeframe, project developers withdrew around a dozen early-stage battery storage proposals from the interconnection queue of Puget Sound Energy, Washington’s largest utility.” [Washington State Standard, 6/18/26, link to article]

Northern Rockies

nothing to see here, folks

CO/NM/NV

nothing to see here, folks

AZ/UT

UTAH

Utah’s Largest Solar and Storage Project Begins Commercial Operations at Green River Energy Center “Utah’s largest solar and storage project has begun commercial operations, as rPlus Energies brings the Green River Energy Center online in Emery County, marking a major milestone in the state’s clean energy expansion. The facility includes 400MW of solar generation and 400MW/1,600MWh of battery energy storage….The project comes as Utah accelerates efforts to expand electricity supply under Operation Gigawatt, a state-backed initiative launched by Governor Spencer Cox in 2024 to double the state’s energy production over the next decade.” [Construction Review, 6/23/26, link to article]

Southeast

ARKANSAS

Cypress Creek secures US$3.5 billion to fund 1.63GW/1.9GWh solar-plus-storage project “US independent power producer (IPP) Cypress Creek Energy has secured US$3.5 billion in financing to support the development of a 1.63GW/1.9GWh solar-plus-storage project in Arkansas. The financing will support the first two phases of the three-phase Steel River Energy Center project, which comprises 2.45GW of solar PV and 2.9GWh of battery storage, making it one of the largest solar-plus-storage projects in the US. The entire project is expected to be completed by 2029.” [PV Tech, 6/12/2026, link to article]

NORTH CAROLINA

MN8 commissions 260 MW of new solar capacity to supply clean electricity for Microsoft operations in Texas and North Carolina “MN8 Energy revealed to the public that the newly operational projects include the 120 MW Long Point Solar facility in Texas and the 140 MW Herbert Solar facility in North Carolina. Together, the installations deliver 260 MW of solar generation capacity across two major U.S. electricity markets. The projects have been developed under long-term power purchase agreements (PPAs) with Microsoft. Data centers are known to use massive amounts of electricity, so MN8 will assist the technology entity to secure additional renewable electricity for its ever-growing operations. ” [Energies Media, June 15, 2026, link to article]

South-Central

TEXAS

Sabanci Renewables Expands U.S. Growth Through Long-Term Power Purchase Agreement with Meta “ Sabanci Renewables, a wholly owned subsidiary of Sabanci Holding, one of Türkiye's leading holding companies with deep expertise in the energy sector, has entered into a long term Power Purchase Agreement (PPA) with Meta for 100% of the environmental attributes from its 100 MWac / 130 MWdc Lucky 7 Solar and 120 MWac / 156 MWdc Pepper Solar projects in Texas with a total capacity of 220 MWac / 286 MWdc. ” [NewsFile, 6/22/26, link to article]

Plains

nothing to see here, folks

Pacific Northwest

OREGON

Group forms to oppose proposed solar project in Keno “The Keno Wildlands Alliance, composed of local residents, has established a 501(c)(4) political advocacy group to oppose Ørsted’s proposed Klamath Falls Energy Center. “We’re not anti-solar; we just don’t think it should be on this land,” said Joyce Furlong, who represents the alliance… The Klamath Falls Energy Center is a proposed 400-megawatt solar farm with a 400-megawatt battery storage system. The project is currently under review by the Oregon Department of Energy’s State Solar Siting Commission.” [Herald and News, June 19, 2026, link to article]

Northern Rockies

nothing to see here, folks

CO/NM/NV

NEW MEXICO

After nearly two decades, this massive New Mexico wind project is now powering California “The largest wind energy project in U.S. history is now online, delivering power from a massive array in New Mexico to Arizona and California — and signaling a new era for sending clean electricity across the West. Nearly two decades in the making, the estimated $11-billion SunZia project from Pattern Energy is now fully operational, company officials said Thursday. It’s made up of 916 turbines that can produce up to 3.65 gigawatts of electricity, making it potentially more powerful than the Hoover Dam. ” [Los Angeles Times, June 18, 2026, link to article]

NEVADA

BLM advances massive Pahrump Valley solar project “Federal land managers are moving forward with the proposed Purple Sage Energy Center project, a massive solar-generation facility about 13 miles south of Pahrump, Nevada.The Bureau of Land Management issued a final environmental review and resource management plan last week for the proposed 400‑megawatt Purple Sage Energy Center project on more than 4,500 acres of public land near the Nevada-California border. It would be one of largest solar generation projects in the state.” [Nevada Current, June 22, 2026, link to article]

AZ/UT

ARIZONA

Apache County's renewable energy moratorium recommendation, part 1: The foundations “The Apache County Planning and Zoning Commission’s vote on June 3 to recommend that the Board of Supervisors consider a temporary moratorium on renewable energy conditional use permit applications followed months of public pressure ordinance concerns, commissioner debate, a field trip to an operating wind project and repeated questions about whether Apache County should continue accepting renewable energy conditional use permit applications while its renewable energy ordinance rewrite remains unfinished. The commission approved the recommendation by a 6-2 vote…” [WMI Central, June 15, 2026, link to article]

California

Solar generations surpasses natural gas in California after panel installs surge “Utility-scale solar generation in California exceeded power from gas during 82% of the days this year through May, according to a Tuesday report from the US Energy Information Association. While solar has supplied more power to the state’s grid for short periods in the past, this marks the first year when average generation in the first five months has outpaced the fossil fuel that’s the biggest source of US electricity.” [The Mercury News, June 18, 2026, link to article]

Daily Template 6/22

To:

staff@greenlightamerica.org

Subject

Daily Clean Energy Clips 06/22/2026

Northeast

NEW YORK

Ellery nears moratorium extensions for wind, solar, BESS: The Ellery Town Board is poised to extend its moratorium on wind, solar, and battery energy storage system projects for up to one year. Public hearings were held June 9. The moratoriums will last six months after passage and can be extended twice for three months each. The town is drafting a local law for wind energy and considering one for BESS. For solar, the board will require that components be recycled as part of decommissioning. Solar projects in sensitive areas near Chautauqua Lake, schools, Long Point State Park, the village of Bemus Point, and wildlife management areas will face higher standards and a 2,500-foot setback from non-participating property lines. Once a special use permit is granted, developers will have six months to obtain a building permit and one year to complete the project. Town attorney Neil Robinson reminded the board that the local law “can’t be so restrictive that it prevents development of solar farms.” [Post-Journal, June 2026, link to article] [Use Option 4 on PayWallRemover]

Mid-Atlantic

nothing to see here, folks

Southeast

FLORIDA

NextEra to pay $150M to settle charges related to Florida political misconduct allegations: NextEra Energy agreed to pay $150 million to settle a shareholder lawsuit in federal court in Florida. The case involved allegations of political misconduct, including funding “ghost” candidates to defeat state lawmakers and spying on a reporter, in the context of NextEra’s proposed $67 billion merger with Dominion Energy. The settlement is covered by insurance and is the largest securities class action settlement in the U.S. District Court for the Southern District of Florida since 1995. [Utility Dive, June 18, 2026, link to article])

Midwest

INDIANA

Community continues to voice concerns on expansive solar projects in Dubois County: Dubois County residents are raising ongoing concerns about large solar projects, including fire safety, road access, lack of independent county oversight, and inadequate financial protections. Commissioners have limited authority to stop projects due to state law but are developing new zoning ordinances and considering stricter setbacks. A moratorium remains in place on the second phase of the EDP Renewables Duff Solar Project while the county finalizes land-use rules. [Dubois County Free Press, June 16, 2026, link to article]

MICHIGAN

Unfounded health concerns in Michigan are powering a solar backlash: St. Clair County adopted a novel public health regulation last year that set limits on solar development and battery storage, encouraged by the county’s medical director who warned of noise, visual pollution, and potential contamination. County Commissioner (now Chair) Steven Simasko accepted the medical opinion “as a good standard for the protection of the public health of our citizens.” The policy included a $25,000 nonrefundable fee and violations punishable by up to six months in prison. In February, a circuit court judge ruled the health regulation “invalid, null, and void,” but county officials unanimously voted to appeal. [Deadline Detroit / ProPublica, June 16, 2026, link to article])

North-Central

IOWA

Plymouth County hits pause on data centers with 1-year moratorium: Plymouth County Supervisors approved a one-year “revocable moratorium” on the construction of data centers and industrial battery storage facilities. The vote was 4-0. The moratorium aims to give the county time to prepare rules on construction, water and power usage, and noise issues. Supervisor Chairman Don Kass said, “It’s become more of a local issue. I myself had citizens come up to me and express their concern regarding these kind of facilities. So, we’ve decided we really need to be proactive and get ahead of this issue.” [1011 Now, June 17, 2026, link to article]

WISCONSIN

Marathon County to hold listening session on proposed wind farm: Marathon County hosted a listening session on June 17 at the Lake View Conference Center at 6 p.m. to gather resident feedback on the proposed Hub City Wind Farm by Alliant Energy (28–38 turbines, ~150 MW). The project could power 45,000 homes and bring over $22 million in revenue. The county’s advisory committee is collecting input to inform negotiations with the developer. Committee chair John Robinson said, “Our (job) is not to pass judgment on the merits of the project, but rather collect the information” and “We’re trying to be inclusive and get as much input as we can.” [WPR, June 17 2026, link to article])

South-Central

KANSAS

Sedgwick County approves solar pause, considers proposals for two developments: Sedgwick County commissioners approved a six-month moratorium on new solar energy projects. The pause gives the county more time to review applications amid concerns over farmland conversion and infrastructure. Two large proposed projects southwest of Wichita are not affected by the new moratorium. [Yahoo News, June 17 2026, link to article])

Plains

nothing to see here, folks

Pacific Northwest

WASHINGTON

Big battery farms encounter resistance across western Washington: Community opposition to utility-scale battery energy storage systems has grown across western Washington, leading multiple counties and cities (including King, Skagit, Pierce, Kitsap, and Thurston counties, plus Snoqualmie, Carnation, North Bend, Puyallup, and Duvall) to enact or extend six-month to one-year moratoriums on new BESS permits. Concerns center on lithium-ion battery fire risks, toxic smoke, noise from cooling fans, and impacts on neighborhoods, schools, and farmland. Anacortes resident Elke Siller Macartney said, “There’s a dawning awareness of the awful effects of BESS installations such as thermal runaway fires and toxic smoke, and the horrible noise from cooling fans… It’s the height of arrogance for these companies to pretend to install these things for the supposed good of all.” [News from the States, June 18 2026, link to article])

ID/MT/WY

Daily Template 6/18

To:

staff@greenlightamerica.org

Subject

Daily Clean Energy Clips 06/18/2026

Northeast

NEW YORK

State goals, local impacts: Inside the fight over where large-scale solar should go in NY: New York’s push for large-scale solar is creating conflicts in rural upstate counties as projects like Flat Creek Solar (300 MW on 3,000 acres in Montgomery County) and Mill Point Solar move forward under the Office of Renewable Energy Siting (ORES). Residents and local officials in Montgomery and Washington counties are raising concerns about cumulative impacts on farmland, wildlife, drainage, property values, and the loss of local control. [CBS6 Albany, June 16, 2026, link to article])

Mid-Atlantic

nothing to see here, folks

Southeast

nothing to see here, folks

Midwest

INDIANA

Community continues to voice concerns on expansive solar projects in Dubois County: Dubois County residents are raising ongoing concerns about large solar projects, including fire safety, road access, lack of independent county oversight, and inadequate financial protections. Commissioners have limited authority to stop projects due to state law but are developing new zoning ordinances and considering stricter setbacks. A moratorium remains in place on the second phase of the EDP Renewables Duff Solar Project while the county finalizes land-use rules. [Dubois County Free Press, June 16, 2026, link to article])

MICHIGAN

Unfounded health concerns in Michigan are powering a solar backlash: St. Clair County adopted a novel public health regulation last year that set limits on solar development and battery storage, encouraged by the county’s medical director who warned of noise, visual pollution, and potential contamination. County Commissioner (now Chair) Steven Simasko accepted the medical opinion “as a good standard for the protection of the public health of our citizens.” The policy included a $25,000 nonrefundable fee and violations punishable by up to six months in prison. In February, a circuit court judge ruled the health regulation “invalid, null, and void,” but county officials unanimously voted to appeal. [Deadline Detroit / ProPublica, June 16, 2026, link to article])

North-Central

nothing to see here, folks

South-Central

KANSAS

Two Kansas solar farms would convert nearly 3,000 acres of farmland near Wichita: Sedgwick County is considering approval for two large solar projects southwest of Wichita — the 260 MW Galena Solar Project (≈1,490 acres) and the 130 MW Branch Line Solar Project (≈1,360 acres). Both include battery storage. On June 17, the Sedgwick County Commission voted unanimously to impose a six-month moratorium on new solar permits. The moratorium will not affect these two applications. [The Kansas City Star, June 17, 2026, link to article])

TEXAS

40+ Texas lawmakers back effort to pause major transmission lines: More than 40 Texas lawmakers filed an amicus brief asking the Public Utility Commission of Texas to pause decisions on proposed 765kV transmission lines, citing the need for more time for route studies, environmental reviews, and addressing landowner concerns. [Yahoo News, June 16, 2026, link to article])

Plains

nothing to see here, folks

Pacific Northwest

nothing to see here, folks

ID/MT/WY

WYOMING

Albany County extends permit for Rail Tie Wind Project despite opposition: Albany County commissioners voted 2-1 to grant a five-year extension of the permit for the 504 MW Rail Tie Wind Project (≈120 turbines on 26,000 acres south of Laramie). The project has faced significant delays from litigation and a stop-work order. Supporters highlighted economic benefits and tax revenue, while opponents argued little actual construction progress has been made. [Cowboy State Daily, June 16, 2026, link to article])

CO/NM/NV

nothing to see here, folks

AZ/UT

nothing to see here, folks

California

Supervisors extend battery energy storage moratorium, draft ordinance released: The San Benito County Board of Supervisors unanimously voted to extend a moratorium on battery energy storage systems for up to 10 months and 15 days while staff develops local regulations. A draft BESS ordinance was released shortly after the vote and includes fire hazard standards, flood protections, and emergency response requirements. [BenitoLink, June 16, 2026, link to article])


Daily Template 6/17

To:

staff@greenlightamerica.org

Subject

Daily Clean Energy Clips 06/17/2026

Northeast

NEW YORK & MASSACHUSETTS

States claim victory as Trump admin ends wind court fight: The Trump administration dropped its appeal of a court ruling that struck down its “wind directive” pausing new onshore and offshore wind projects. The 1st U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals granted the Interior Department’s request to dismiss the appeal. New York Attorney General Letitia James said the ruling protects projects that “will create jobs, strengthen our economy, and bring down New Yorkers’ electric bills.” Massachusetts Attorney General Andrea Joy Campbell said the court “correctly protected those critical investments from the Trump administration’s unlawful order.” [E&E News, June 16 2026, link to article])

NEW YORK

Huntington officials approve 6-month battery storage moratorium: The Huntington Town Board approved a six-month moratorium on new battery energy storage system permits. Town Supervisor Ed Smyth said recent incidents have shown that “more research needs to be done on the safety of these systems.” Board member Dave Bennardo noted the six months will be used to improve emergency response protocols with the firefighting community. Two applications are currently paused. [Newsday, June 16 2026, link to article])

Mid-Atlantic

Nothing to see here, folks!

Southeast

Nothing to see here, folks!

Midwest

MICHIGAN

Jupiter Power receives approval to build battery storage facility in Saginaw County: The Tittabawassee Township Planning Commission approved Jupiter Power’s special use permit for a battery energy storage facility on Hackett Road. The project is expected to generate $31.5 million in tax revenue over its 20-year operational life. Consulting planner Grant Murschel said the township focused on adhering to public health and safety standards. [WNEM, June 16 2026, link to article])

MICHIGAN

Zeeland Twp neighbors fight to stay in solar farm approval process: Neighbors in Zeeland and Jamestown townships are fighting to remain as intervenors in the Michigan Public Service Commission case for RWE Americas’ proposed 200-megawatt Silver Maple Solar Farm on 1,900 acres. RWE’s attorneys moved to dismiss some non-adjacent neighbors. Administrative Law Judge James Varchetti will rule on intervenor status June 25. Neighbor Valerie Driesenga said, “We’re an adjacent landowner, so we immediately have the right to be part of the case.” Neighbors are holding a pancake breakfast fundraiser on June 20 to raise money for legal representation. [FOX 17, June 12 2026, link to article])

North-Central

Nothing to see here, folks!

South-Central

TEXAS

No moratorium for Leon County, but residents’ fight continues: Leon County commissioners tabled discussions of a temporary moratorium on new data center, solar, and battery storage development. Residents expressed frustration, saying the commission’s actions felt like “political theater.” Multiple solar farms, data centers, and BESS facilities are already approved or in the pipeline. [KXXV, June 2026, link to article])

Plains

Nothing to see here, folks!

Pacific Northwest

OREGON

Oregon clean energy group sues Defense Department over wind project freeze: Renewable Northwest filed suit against the Department of Defense, alleging the Pentagon is improperly blocking wind energy approvals by refusing to complete routine reviews, effectively halting wind development nationwide. The group says the freeze puts over 100 land-based wind projects and 30 gigawatts of capacity at risk. [The Oregonian, June 12 2026, link to article])

AZ/UT

Nothing to see here, folks!

California

Solar-friendly bill would again cut Kern’s property tax receipts: Senate Bill 1329 would change how large-scale solar projects are assessed for property taxes by valuing only tangible assets and excluding power purchase agreements, tax credits, and subsidies. The change would reduce tax revenue for counties like Kern. Kern County Board Chairman Phillip Peters said, “The state continues to pass costs, unfunded mandates and other financial obligations down to the county.” State Sen. Shannon Grove (R-Bakersfield) strongly opposed the bill, saying she is “tired of providing the energy and using our energy and our resources, and now you won’t even let us have the tax revenue.” [The Bakersfield Californian, June 9 2026, link to article])

Daily Template 6/16

To:

staff@greenlightamerica.org

Subject

Daily Clean Energy Clips 06/16/2026

Northeast

MAINE

‘Different this time’: Wind farms and transmission lines poised to come to northern Maine: Maine’s Public Utilities Commission is moving forward with a new RFP (issued December 2025, bids closing June 12, 2026) to select wind and solar projects plus transmission infrastructure in Aroostook County to deliver at least 1,200 MW to the New England grid. The effort is a regional collaboration with Connecticut, Massachusetts, Rhode Island, and Vermont to share costs and benefits. Philip Bartlett, PUC chairman, stated, “This RFP builds on prior stakeholder input and regional transmission planning to move renewable energy projects in northern Maine from concept to reality.” Jack Shapiro of the Natural Resources Council of Maine noted, “This time they started working with all the other states in advance… It’s basically the biggest opportunity that we have for low-cost renewable energy in the medium term.” [The Maine Monitor, June 12 2026, link to article]

MASSACHUSETTS

Landlord Sues Vineyard Offshore for Missed Rent Payments: BP Hancock filed suit in Suffolk County Superior Court claiming Vineyard Offshore (an affiliate of Vineyard Wind backer Copenhagen Infrastructure Partners) owes more than $1.2 million in unpaid rent, fees, and security deposit replenishment for 28,370 square feet of office space on the 18th floor at 200 Clarendon Street in Boston. The tenant has been in default since March 1, 2026. Attorney Kate Moran Carter stated, “Tenant has been in default since March 1, 2026, after it failed to pay Rent and other charges due under the Lease and to replenish its security deposit.” [Vineyard Gazette, June 15 2026, link to article]

NEW YORK

Town of Franklin proposes one-year moratorium on battery storage projects: The Town of Franklin (Franklin County) is considering a one-year moratorium on battery energy storage systems due to concerns over lithium-ion battery fires and long-term pollution from decommissioned batteries. A public meeting is scheduled for Wednesday at 5 p.m. at town hall. This follows a trend of more than 30 New York municipalities enacting BESS bans or moratoriums. [MyNBC5, June 9 2026, link to article]

Mid-Atlantic

VIRGINIA

Rockingham supervisors reject solar farm proposal: The Rockingham County Board of Supervisors voted 4-0 to deny a special use permit for a solar farm near Timberville on agricultural land roughly a quarter-mile from Plains Elementary School. Concerns included stormwater runoff, rocky terrain making construction difficult, conflict with the county’s agriculture-support goals, high municipal water use for cooling, and potential negative impacts on property values. Resident David Mewellski said, “They’re not going to be able to just pound poles in the ground… The property they’re planning on putting it on is on nothing but rock.” [Northern Virginia Daily, June 14 2026, link to article]

nothing to see here, folks

Midwest

MICHIGAN

Michigan battery storage and local control updates advancing under PA 233: (Note: Full article content was limited in retrieval; based on context and recent Canary Media coverage, this appears to cover ongoing implementation of Michigan’s Public Act 233 for battery storage siting, local government roles, and the push for 2,500 MW of storage by 2029. One utility-scale project, the 100 MW Tibbits Energy Storage in Coldwater Township, is currently operating.) [FOX 17 Online, June 12 2026, link to article]

nothing to see here, folks

South-Central

KANSAS

Sedgwick County weighing second temporary ban on solar energy projects in 3 years: The Sedgwick County Commission is considering a six-month moratorium on new solar projects (with possible extension). Commissioners cited the need for more time after reviewing two recent applications, concerns over protest petitions, proximity to Clearwater’s urban growth area, and infrastructure issues (including Ark 95 and potential south connector roads). Commissioner Jeff Blubaugh stated, “We don’t want to rush this… I want to make sure that we’ve turned over every stone.” A decision is expected at the June 17 meeting. [KWCH, June 15 2026, link to article]

ARKANSAS

EDP Renewables and Meta advance major solar project in Arkansas: (Note: Full article content was limited in retrieval; this appears to cover progress on a large solar + storage project in Arkansas with Meta involvement, including financing milestones.) ARIZONA

Apache County P&Z recommends renewable energy moratorium: The Apache County Planning and Zoning Commission voted 6-2 on June 3 to recommend that the Board of Supervisors adopt a temporary moratorium on new renewable energy conditional use permit applications while the county rewrites its renewable energy ordinance. Public concerns raised include impacts on public safety, emergency communications, water sources, roads, cultural/scenic resources, noise, and proximity to homes. A separate Black Ridge wind project application was submitted around May 26, adding urgency. The recommendation now goes to the Board of Supervisors for further process including public hearing. [White Mountain Independent, June 15 2026, link to article]

VIRGINIA

Chesapeake Planning Commission recommends denial of proposed battery energy storage system: “The Chesapeake Planning Commission has recommended denial of a conditional use permit for the Mill Stone Energy Center, a proposed battery energy storage system (BESS) facility in the Hickory area of Chesapeake…The Chesapeake City Council is scheduled to take up the project at their council meeting on July 21. That meeting is open to the public for comment.” [WTKR News 3, 06/11/2026, link to article here].

[Opinion] Solar lawyer: Are bans on solar really banned?: “HB711, which amended VA Code 15.2-2288.8 (“Special exceptions for solar voltaic projects”), has been called the “solar standards” bill. It has also been called a pyrrhic victory because, for all that it does, it does not limit counties’ ability to turn down solar projects…The core point is not complicated. HB 711 gives covered solar projects a required path to local review. Virginia counties still control the ultimate vote. They can still consider local impacts. They can still impose lawful conditions. They can still deny projects that do not satisfy the applicable standards.” [Cardinal News, 06/12/2026, link to article here].

DELAWARE

Sussex County solar farm developments might be slowed due to connectivity challenges – Delaware Public Media: “Sussex County staff is tracking 59 conditional uses, or CUs, related to solar arrays or panels in the county, but only some have been issued building permits…Commissioner Matt Lloyd asks if that explains a recent surge of applications from solar developers. ‘Just understanding that a percentage of them will fall off, do you think they’re bringing us extra applications knowing that only a few of them might actually make it to the end?’ he said…Conditional use approvals also expire after three years in Sussex County, which could create the need for a developer to apply twice for the same project.” [Solar Now, 06/11/2026, link to article here].

OH/PA

Nothing to see here folks!

Southeast

NORTH CAROLINA

Regulators' pause in Duke Energy solar procurement raises concerns about bills: “However, state regulators this year halted Duke Energy’s procurement of new solar farms until the utility's new carbon plan is approved…However, state regulators this year halted Duke Energy’s procurement of new solar farms until the utility's new carbon plan is approved…He added that the delay could cause solar developers to miss key deadlines tied to federal tax credits that are set to expire, which could further increase costs for customers.“ [WFAE 90.7, 06/10/2026, link to article here].

ARKANSAS

Cypress Creek scores USD 3.5bn in funds for Arkansas solar-BESS giant: “US renewable energy developer and operator Cypress Creek Renewables has secured USD 3.5 billion (EUR 3.03bn) in financing for part of its gigawatt-scale Steel River Energy Center, a huge solar-and-storage project in Arkansas…When all phases are completed, the Steel River Energy Center will comprise 2.45 GW of photovoltaic (PV) farms and 2.9 GWh of battery energy storage systems (BESS)...The solar-storage complex will be located in Mississippi County in the northeastern part of Arkansas. Phases 1 and 2 will be equipped with domestically-made panels by First Solar and will be operated under a virtual power purchase agreement (PPA) with an investment-grade corporate counterparty.” [Renewables Now, 06/12/2026, link to article here].

Midwest

INDIANA

​​Clark County Plan Commission issues favorable recommendation on solar amendment: “The Clark County Plan Commission voted on Wednesday to recommend a change to the Unified Development Ordinance that would prohibit agricultural land from being used for solar energy facilities…In a 4-3 vote, the plan commission voted to recommend that the Clark County Commissioners amend the UDO to only permit solar energy systems to be built on property that is zoned light or heavy industry.” [News and Tribune, 06/11/2026, link to article here].

ILLINOIS

Grundy County intends to appeal appellate court’s ruling on solar panels: “The Grundy County Board approved a new solar guideline Tuesday that requires solar developers to have concrete plans in place before receiving permits from the county…The Third District Appellate Court ruled against Grundy County on Tuesday, March 10, in the case of Equity Solar Illinois vs. County of Grundy. According to the court document, the dispute between Equity Solar and Grundy County arose when Grundy County rejected special use permit requests, and the plaintiffs filed a mandamus action, which was granted by the Grundy County Circuit Court…The county will require more intensive financial and legal protections through explicit fee formulas, higher insurance limits and irrevocable financial assurances for both decommissioning and reclamation…The circuit court ordered Grundy County to issue the permits, and the county appealed. It lost its appeal, but it plans to appeal further.” [Shaw Local, 06/11/2026, link to article here].

Sherman tightens fire code for lithium-ion battery storage systems: “The Sherman City Council approved new fire code regulations for battery energy storage systems as the facilities continue to pop up across Texoma…The city council passed the rule change unanimously. Hartsfield said the ordinance is a preventative measure, treating a fire as a matter of if, not when, it occurs.” [KXII 12, 06/11/2026, link to article here].

Nothing to see here folks!

Pacific Northwest

WASHINGTON

Massive clean energy project caught in legal crosswinds at WA Supreme Court: “Opponents of the Horse Heaven wind project contend that, in looking to act expeditiously, the state Energy Facility Site Evaluation Council and former Democratic Gov. Jay Inslee failed to properly account for the project’s negative impacts on the environment, surrounding communities and tribal cultural resources…Tri-Cities CARES, Benton County and the Yakama Nation filed separate petitions seeking to overturn the final decision issued in October 2024. Justices consolidated the petitions and will hear arguments at 10 a.m. Thursday. At stake is the future of a massive wind, solar and battery storage project envisioned on roughly 72,500 acres along Interstate 82 south of the Tri-Cities. More broadly, if the court sides with the project’s foes, it will seriously crimp state efforts to reduce Washington’s reliance on power generated from fossil fuels…Opponents contend the state’s Administrative Procedures Act was not followed and that the environment and wildlife are inadequately protected. Residents say nothing was done to address interference with their views of the Horse Heaven hills…The Yakama Nation contends the final decision resulted in a project that would have significantly greater harm to wildlife and their cultural resources compared to the council’s initial recommendation. They also said there was inadequate consultation between the tribe and the council.” [Washington State Standard, 06/10/2026, link to article here].

Northeast

NEW YORK

NY power grid squeezed by aging plants, extreme weather, and data centers: NYISO report: “The New York Independent System Operator warned on Tuesday that extreme weather and new corporate users with high demands are shrinking electricity reserves, leaving the state’s grid close to a shortage on the hottest and coldest days of the year…Despite the warnings about electric grid reliability, New York achieved a renewable energy milestone on June 3. According to NYISO, the record for solar power generation was broken during the noon hour that day, with solar panels generating 5,661 megawatts—about 29% of the entire statewide demand. Everyday residential New Yorkers generated most of that through small, private rooftop systems called “behind-the-meter” solar. Those systems created 5,131 megawatts, while large commercial solar farms generated just 530 megawatts.” [Localsyr.com, 06/10/2026, link to article here]

Residents express frustration with county regarding solar ordinance: “Concerned residents spoke up regarding McKean County's lack of a solar energy facility ordinance during commissioners' regular meeting Tuesday at the McKean County Courthouse in Smethport. Residents hoped commissioners would adopt their proposed measure, but the board tabled the ordinance again.” [The Bradford Era, 06/09/2026, link to article here].

KANSAS

Cherokee County planners advance comprehensive zoning plan: “After hearing from residents and Confluence, the firm hired to help create the plan, the Cherokee County Planning Commission unanimously approved a resolution recommending commissioners adopt the comprehensive plan for the county. This comes as Clearway Energy has proposed a solar energy project. Currently, a moratorium is in place prohibiting large-scale projects like solar and nuclear, from moving forward. The process of creating a comprehensive zoning plan began as an attempt to stop new energy projects. The plan will now go before the Cherokee County Commission at its next scheduled meeting.” [Yahoo! News, 06/10/2026, link to article here].

TEXAS

Waller County Judge Posts, Comal Commissioner Shares, Explanation Why Counties Can’t Stop Developers, Data Centers: “Waller County Judge Trey Duhon explained on Facebook yesterday the reasons why counties can’t regulate the explosive growth reshaping Texas communities like Canyon Lake and New Braunfels… ‘I recently received a DM on Facebook from a resident inquiring about starting a petition against a new development because they are concerned that it’s going to affect electricity, roads, and flooding. This raises a very good topic for discussion, especially in light of current hot-button topics such as data centers, battery-energy storage facilities, solar farms, etc…Counties can only regulate what the Texas legislature has given us the ability to regulate, which honestly is not much. ‘For example, we do not have ‘zoning authority.’ We can not arbitrarily dictate where certain types of developments can be built. We do not have noise-ordinance authority…We can not impose arbitrary rules on any developer without running the risk of exposing the county to liability….As I frequently tell people, if you want our commissioners court to have more authority in terms of regulating these types of things, then your time and energy is probably better spent on communicating with state representatives, state senators, and state officials to give counties more authority to regulate these types of things (like zoning authority, for example).’” [MyCanyonLake.com, 06/10/2026, link to article here].

County of Winnebago held that Illinois courts cannot compel counties to issue permits when solar projects meet statewide standards for solar siting under 55 ILCS 5/5-12020.” [JDSupra, 06/09/2026, link to article here].

Texas' grid accounted for nearly half of all of the power generation growth as data centres flood into the southern U.S. state.” [Yahoo! Finance, 06/04/2026, link to article here].

MICHIGAN

Neighbors rally against battery energy facility in Oshtemo Township: “A rally against a potential battery energy storage system, or BESS, facility that could be built on rural land near homes and agriculture drew a group of more than 100 neighbors to Little Pistol Farms in Oshtemo Township Monday night…Residents formed a group called ‘Say No to BESS,’ urging their local leaders to oppose BESS development, because of a variety of environmental and health concerns related to the technology…The land up against Little Pistol Farms and seven other residential property lines, on Van Kal Avenue, has been eyed by an out of state company, NewEdge Renewable Power. NewEdge Renewable Power has not submitted a formal application, but has previously stated plans to submit an application for the site during the second half of 2026, which is around the same time as the township's moratorium is expected to expire…Oshtemo Township’s one-year moratorium on commercial battery energy storage development applications was approved in November of last year. According to the township attorney, it could be extended. The next Oshtemo Township Planning Commission meeting is scheduled for Thursday, June 25th at 6 pm.” [News Channel 3, 06/09/2026, link to article here].

Rep. Wortz: Residents 'spoke loud and clear' against solar expansion: “State Rep. Jennifer Wortz is praising Fayette Township's decision to deny an expansion for a utility-scale solar project, which could still be appealed for override by state regulators. The Fayette Township Planning Commission voted last week to deny a special land use permit sought by Ranger Power for its proposed Heartwood Solar II and III projects…Wortz added that federal incentives are likely contributing to the continued push for solar projects across Michigan. ‘The federal Solar Tax credit is expiring on December 31, 2027,’ she said. ‘For commercial solar projects to receive this 30% tax credit the project must be fully installed and functioning by this date. I believe that is why there is such a push to get these projects through.’ If Ranger Power appeals, Wortz said she hopes state regulators defer to the township's decision.” [The Center Square, 06/08/2026, link to article here].

California

2026 Election Results: A close race in Covina has voters waiting on results: “As of the latest results from the Los Angeles County registrar, released at 4:48 p.m. Monday, June 8, Susan Zermeno, 59, a retired city planner and land use consultant, appears to be holding on to a steady lead, with 52.7% of the vote compared with 47.3% for her opponent, Rosie Richardson, 37, a community outreach manager…Zermeno said Monday that she is not comfortable enough to declare victory. ‘One thing that I'd like to bring up now is the announcement of the Planning Commission's meeting on the BESS (battery energy storage system) scheduled for June 23,’…The Planning Commission meeting and public hearing on the battery energy storage project is set for 7 p.m. Tuesday, June 23, at City Hall council chambers, 125 E. College St., Covina. For more information, call 626-384-5450 or email planning@covinaca.gov.” [The Tribune, 06/08/2026, link to article here]

Allium Solar: County could lose local control over battery energy storage project: “In August 2025, Boston-based Longroad Energy filed an application to build a 945-acre solar and battery facility north of the Hollister airport…The project, called Allium Solar, would sit north of Hollister on agricultural land bordering Highway 156, across from the Amazon warehouse, and stretching up to Hwy 25. Both the property owner and Longroad describe the site as low-productivity farmland. It would generate 110 megawatts of solar power and store an equivalent capacity in batteries…The county’s current moratorium remains in effect until the ordinance is presented to the board, which was supposed to occur on June 9.  The draft ordinance, County Counsel Gregory Priamos said, was expected to be posted June 4 or June 5. However, on June 5, the county released a June 9 board meeting agenda without a draft energy storage ordinance that includes a scheduled public hearing with a staff recommendation to adopt an urgency ordinance to extend the moratorium for more than 10 additional months while staff continues work on an energy storage ordinance.” [Benito Link, 06/08/2026, link to article here]

Floating solar panels on North Country waterways?: “A bill passed the state Senate this week, calling for a floating solar incentive and education program. It would allow solar panels to float on bodies of water, but it doesn’t have language that excludes any body of water, natural or not…The bill will move on to the state Assembly during the next legislative session in January.” [WWNY 7, 06/05/2026, link to article here].

Caroline County Board to Review Solar Zoning Updates June 9: “The Caroline County Board of Supervisors will meet on Tuesday, June 9, 2026, at 6:00 p.m. at the Community Services Center in Milford…A key discussion item is TXT-03-2026, a proposed amendment to the county’s zoning ordinance that would update rules for solar energy and energy storage facilities to align with new state legislation effective July 1, 2026. Residents interested in local energy policy or zoning changes can attend to listen or share comments during the meeting.” [Caroline County VA Daily Brief, 06/06/2026, link to article here].

GEORGIA

Wilcox Commissioners approve $2.7 million for roads, advance jail site review: “Under old business, commissioners discussed an intergovernmental agreement with Abbeville regarding fire suppression automatic mutual aid and held the second reading of the county’s solar ordinance amendment….The Wilcox County Board of Commissioners meets on the first Tuesday of each month at 6:30 p.m. in the commissioner’s office at the courthouse in Abbeville.” [Cordele Dispatch, 06/07/2026, link to article here]

Midwest

MICHIGAN

Rural Showdown: Fayette Township Slams Brakes On Massive Heartwood Solar Farm: “On Monday night, Fayette Township’s planning commission slammed the brakes on Ranger Power’s Heartwood II solar proposal, voting to deny a special land‑use permit for a 140‑megawatt array that would have stretched across roughly 1,350 acres. The decision capped months of heated public meetings where neighbors, local officials and Hillsdale College warned the project would threaten water resources, wetlands and the township’s agricultural character…Zoning consultants from Carlisle/Wortman formally recommended denial, citing deficient wetland protection and the inappropriate conversion of productive farmland. The commission’s motion to reject the permit echoed those findings and, as reported by WTVB, also pointed to gaps in the applicant’s emergency‑response planning, the planned removal of dozens of acres of trees and other impacts to the township’s rural landscape.” [Hoodline, 06/05/2026, link to article here].

Huron County will continue review of Pine Sap Solar despite moratorium: “Despite Huron County's new moratorium on solar and battery energy storage projects, Ranger Power's application for the 150-megawatt Pine Sap Solar project in Rubicon Township will continue through the review process…The decision was based on a recommendation from the county’s corporation counsel. The Huron County Board of Commissioners unanimously approved a three-month moratorium May 12, nearly a week after Ranger Power submitted its application…During Wednesday evening's planning commission meeting, Building and Zoning Director Jeff Smith said the review process should continue as normal…Outlining the path forward, Smith said the application will remain under review, with a public hearing possible later in the process.” [Huron Daily Tribune, 06/05/2026, link to article here].

ILLINOIS

Trump administration blocks wind farm development in Illinois and across the nation: “Billions of dollars in wind farm projects all over Illinois are on hold, including two projects in McLean County, two in Peoria County, and one in Tazewell County…Until last August, getting these [FAA (Federal Aviation Administration) compliance] certificates was routine. The FAA automatically kicks applications to the Department of Defense to see if there is a risk to military readiness, said a source knowledgeable about the process…Mitigation cases stopped getting approved starting last August, said a background source. In late April, even the easy cases stopped receiving certificates. Now, no wind farm project in the nation that didn’t already have permit approval is moving forward, said multiple sources…The American Clean Power Association estimated not allowing the U.S. to construct new wind projects would cost the national electricity grid an extra $361 billion over the next 25 years because of higher prices based on demand.” [NPR, 06/05/2026, link to article here]
