TEXAS

[Tweet]: “Hill County passed a temporary moratorium on new data centers, power generation, and large battery storage projects in unincorporated areas. After being sued by a developer for approximately $100 million in damages — a federal lawsuit that remains active even after the moratorium was rescinded — they replaced it with a practical developer checklist…Hood County and Hays County both attempted moratoriums but ultimately voted them down under legal pressure from state officials, including a letter from a Houston area state senator to the Texas Attorney General arguing counties lack authority to impose such pauses.” [@Steve_Soman, 06/07/2026, link to tweet here].

Plains

NORTH DAKOTA

Commission advances battery storage ordinance: “Ward County commissioners forged ahead with an ordinance governing siting of battery energy storage systems on Tuesday, June 2, even though the industry’s proposed language tweaks remain to be dealt with…The commission debated sending the proposed ordinance back to the planning commission and considered imposing a moratorium on applications until the ordinance is completed. Instead, the majority voted to proceed with first reading of the planning commission’s recommended ordinance, revisiting industry proposals at the public hearing and second reading in July.” [Minot Daily News, 06/03/2026, link to article here].

Pacific Northwest

OREGON

Crook County moves toward halting new solar farms: “Crook County leaders are moving forward with plans for a potential moratorium on new solar farms…The Crook County Board of Commissioners voted this week to hold public meetings and prepare documents for a potential moratorium. The measure passed 2-1. County leaders say the ordinance could begin to take shape this summer.” [Central Oregon Daily News, 06/05/2026, link to article here].

A proposed Willamette Valley solar park is back. Neighbors still oppose it: “Developers of the Muddy Creek Energy Park have now submitted their preliminary application to the Oregon Energy Facility Siting Council – a volunteer advisory board appointed by the governor that’s charged with reviewing and approving large energy projects…But a group of local residents has long argued against the project. They worry Muddy Creek could harm wetland habitats for waterfowl that surround the site and replace productive farmland with expansive solar panels if the agricultural part of the project falls through…In a preliminary application hundreds of pages long, Hanwha developers say the site is ideal due to its proximity to a PacificCorp substation and power lines, which will allow solar energy to reach the electrical grid. Hanwha also said roughly 732 acres of the 873-acre solar farm will continue to be farmed…Once the application is complete, the state Department of Energy will hold a public informational meeting.” [OPB, 06/04/2026, link to article here]

Northern Rockies

WYOMING

Wind energy critics rally at Capitol, warn of a ‘Wyoming Wind Wall’: “Rather than take aim at any one particular wind energy project, rally organizers called on decisionmakers to consider the bigger picture: the cumulative impact of converting mostly undeveloped, agricultural landscapes into semi-industrialized zones with wind towers that are regarded by many as a blight on Wyoming’s open spaces…Cheyenne area resident Wendy Volk, who organized the event, has implored state officials to consider individual wind energy projects as part of a larger industrial infrastructure that’s morphing into a corridor of towers, power lines and electrical substations from Medicine Bow to Cheyenne to Douglas.” [Wyofile, 06/05/2026, link to article here]

CO/NM/NV

Nothing to see here folks!

AZ/UT

ARIZONA

[Tweet]: “Goodyear’s Planning & Zoning Commission is set to discuss proposed updates to the city’s zoning ordinance, including potential standards for Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS), data centers, public noticing requirements, and additional modernization efforts. While no project is scheduled for approval, the discussion could help shape how future development proposals move through the process. Wednesday, June 10th • 6:00 p.m. • Goodyear City Hall” [@Jen’sTwoCents, 06/05/2026, link to tweet here]

California

Nothing to see here folks!

Daily Template 06/08

To:

staff@greenlightamerica.org

Subject

Daily Clean Energy Clips 06/08/2026


Northeast

NEW HAMPSHIRE

[Op-Ed] Rob Werner: Stormy winters, rising bills: We must embrace offshore wind: “Granite State energy bills are as much as 39% higher than the national average. The biggest driver behind this is our over reliance on liquified natural gas (LNG), a highly volatile energy source whose cost is at the mercy of global markets, geopolitics, and disruptions to global trade…As our region’s demand for energy grows, we need to diversify our portfolio with renewable energy sources to drive down cost and increase our grid’s stability during peak demand. Our best opportunity to meet increased energy demands is offshore wind…The addition of 3,500 MW of offshore wind is achievable given recent capacity expansions — Vineyard Wind in Massachusetts and Revolution Wind in Rhode Island taken together are on course to generate 1,500 MW of electricity.” [New Hampshire Union Leader, 06/05/2026, linkt to article here].

MASSACHUSET

US court dismisses GE Vineyard appeal: A Massachusetts judge has rejected GE Vernova’s request to throw out a previous order requiring the turbine supplier to continue working on CIP-Iberdrola’s 806MW Vineyard Wind 1 array off the coast of Massachusetts…Justice Peter Krupp of the Massachusetts Superior Court ruled in a court order late last week that the activation of Vineyard Wind’s power purchase agreement with the state of Massachusetts and declaration of COD does not mean that GE Vernova can now walk away from the project…[Krupp] added: “The fact that VW declared a particular milestone under the PPAs does not mean that the (turbines) are functioning at capacity, as designed, or without substantial need for repair and improvement; or that (GE Vernova) does not have specialised expertise needed to bring about timely repair and improvement to VW’s operational capacity.” [reNews, 06/02/2026, link to article here].

Mid-Atlantic

VIRGINIA

Accomack County BZA upholds stop-work order on battery storage facility: “The Accomack County Board of Zoning Appeals affirmed an earlier stop-work order from the Zoning Administrator on the construction of a battery storage facility on the 32400 block of Chincoteague Road after public comment in opposition during a hearing Wednesday morning…Accomack County officials approved a conditional use permit to operate a battery storage system there, with a three-year expiration in July 2025. The Accomack County Zoning Administrator later approved a site plan and land disruption permit in March 2026, after the expiration date. They issued a stop-work order on April 1, which Scout LLC appealed…While Scout Storage, LLC (“Scout”) respects the Board of Zoning Appeals’ decision today, it is disappointed with the outcome. Scout intends to file an appeal in the coming days.” [WBOC, 06/03/2026, link to article here].

OH/PA

OHIO

Trustees gather for informational meeting on wind, solar power: “Township trustees from around the county gathered Monday evening for an informational meeting regarding solar and wind energy hosted by the Ashtabula County Board of Commissioners…The commissioners had asked the trustees to decide whether they want to allow wind or solar projects in a given township with no restrictions, allow them only in certain areas, or prohibit them outright…The commissioners asked the trustees to pass one of the three resolutions by Sept. 1, so the county can then start its process which includes a variety of deadlines and public meetings.” [Yahoo! News, 06/04/2026, link to article here].

Supreme Court of Ohio reverses permit for Oak Run Solar site: “The Supreme Court of Ohio has cut down a previously approved permit for Madison County’s Oak Run Solar project, which would have been the largest solar installation in the history of the state. The court’s majority claim that the project’s permit application did not meet the Ohio Power Siting Board’s (OPSB) submission requirements in full…In the court’s decision, the justices order the siting board to “more thoroughly address” some of the purely visual impacts of the project. However, it is crucial to note that this decision does not outright kill the Oak Run Solar project, and that the opportunity is still open to solar developers around Ohio and the surrounding area.” [Solar Builder, 06/04/2026, link to article here].

PENNSYLVANIA

Battery storage would ease stress on Pa.’s power grid, panel hears: “As higher electricity rates took effect Monday in Pennsylvania, state lawmakers heard broader deployment of battery storage technology could help improve the stability of the region’s power grid and lower the price of power…House Bill 2380, introduced by Rep. Nikki Rivera (D-Lancaster) would put the Pennsylvania Public Utility Commission (PUC) in charge of assigning shares of the 3,000 megawatts based on the service load and annual peak demand of each electric company with more than 600,000 customers…” [Pennsylvania Capital Star, 06/01/2026, link to article here].  

Southeast

NORTH CAROLINA

Why North Carolina’s electric co-ops are turning to grid batteries: “s of April 2025, rural co-ops across North Carolina had 43 battery projects operating or in development, according to the National Rural Electric Cooperative Association. Co-ops here were spearheading more grid batteries than those in any other state; Alaska was a distant second with 13 projects…The battery investments are partly a response to challenges now sweeping the country: Skyrocketing demand from data centers and other factors are constraining supplies and triggering expensive grid upgrades, driving up the costs of electricity.”[Canary Media, 06/04/2026, link to article here].

Midwest

MICHIGAN

Fayette Township Planning Commission Denies Solar Permit: “The Fayette Township Planning Commission voted unanimously Monday night to deny a special land use permit for a massive solar energy project, drawing a standing ovation from local residents…Ranger Power spokesperson Brady Friss told WCSR News that the company remains committed to the project and the property rights of those who have agreed to lease land for the installation. Under Michigan’s Public Act 233, the developer can appeal the decision to the Michigan Public Service Commission…Opponents have noted that the completed project would cover more than 20% of the township’s agricultural land, a factor that could weigh heavily in future state-level reviews.” [Radio Hillsdale, 06/02/2026, link to article  here, more reading here & here].

Tittabawassee Township considers battery storage facility permit: “ittabawassee Township is considering a request from Jupiter Power for a special use permit for a proposed battery storage facility on Tittabawassee Road near Hackett. A meeting is scheduled at the township administrative office building on June 15 at 5 p.m. A vote on the special use permit could take place at that meeting. Jupiter Power shared the following information about the project: Hackett is a 200-megawatt energy storage system.” [WNEM 5, 06/04/2026, link to article here].  

North-Central

WISCONSIN

Wind farm proposal stirs debate in Marathon County — and the county wants to hear from you: “The Marathon County Board of Supervisors has established an Alternative Energy Project Local Impact Advisory Committee to help guide the county’s response. The committee is hosting a public forum on June 17, from 6 to 8 p.m…A community survey is also open through June 19 at marathoncounty.gov/energy….The focus is Alliant Energy’s Hub City Wind Farm…The company plans to submit a formal permit application to the Wisconsin Public Service Commission in late 2026…” [Wausau & Pilot Review, 06/01/2026, link to article here].  

Central-Central

IOWA

Wind turbine project moving forward despite restrictive county ordinance: “Despite the strictest ordinance the company has operated under, a wind turbine project under development by AES Wind Energy will move forward…One of the concerns is that the height of the turbines at 650 feet is too tall. Sorrell said the height produces more electricity per turbine than lower heights and translates to the need for fewer turbines in one concentrated area…An application for a permit hasn’t been made yet, but Sorrell said one could come before the county board sometime in early 2027.” [The Hawk Eye, 06/04/2026, link to article here, more reading here].  

Taylor County Supervisors to discuss energy moratorium and FY27 budget June 4: "The Taylor County Board of Supervisors will meet on Thursday, June 4, 2026, at 9:00 a.m….The agenda includes updates on county roads, a proposed temporary moratorium on new data centers, solar, wind, and mineral development…” [Taylor County IA Daily, 06/04/2026, link to article here].  

South-Central

KANSAS

Finney County advances major solar and battery storage proposal: ‘The Finney County Commission is taking the next step on a planned solar and battery energy system project. Commissioners voted Monday to approve two special‑use permit requests tied to projects proposed by Sherlock Solar: a commercial solar energy conversion system and a battery energy storage system.” [Yahoo! News, 06/02/2026, link to article here].

Kansas county approves permit for 1,200-acre solar farm: “The Ellis County Commission on Tuesday approved a permit for Tallgrass Solar Development to build a solar energy farm 7 miles southwest of Hays and 9 miles southeast of Ellis. The property is about 1,800 acres, but the area of operations will be limited to about 1,200 acres, under the permit…The commission voted unanimously to approve the conditional use permit and contribution agreement with iBV.” [KSNW, 06/04/2026, link to article here].

Plains

NORTH DAKOTA

Ward County Commissioners Pass Ordinance For Battery Energy Storage Systems: “Ward County commissioners are moving a battery energy storage system ordinance forward…A push for a moratorium to look into how much distance would be there should be between a residence and the battery storage systems failed in a one-to-four vote before the commission passed the ordinance.” [Dakota News Network, 06/05/2026, link to article here]

Pacific Northwest

WASHINGTON

Valley cities place moratoriums on battery storage site development: “Snoqualmie City Council unanimously passed a moratorium on battery energy storage system sites at its meeting May 26…A BESS that is proposed for a 45-acre lot behind Fisher Creek Park near Snoqualmie Ridge has been a hot topic in the Valley for almost three months, with residents forming a nonprofit to fight the project. If built, the BESS would connect to the existing nearby Mt. Si substation and have a power capacity of 130 megawatts.” [Snoqualmie Valley Record, 06/02/2026, link to article here, more reading here & here & here]

Skagit County attempts to plan for clean energy future: “As proposed energy facilities cause conflict throughout the state, Skagit County is working on more specific development regulations for the projects that will inevitably come. Earlier this year, Skagit County received a $770,000 grant from the state Department of Commerce, funded through the Climate Commitment Act, to develop local clean energy development regulations and prepare an environmental impact statement, which requires significant public outreach. Similar grants were awarded to Spokane, King and Kittitas counties and the City of Richland…In Skagit County especially, where restrictive zoning to protect local agriculture means many parcels are relatively affordable, energy developers have been making overtures in the area for several years. The county has already seen two large battery facility proposals: one west of Mount Vernon that went through the county permitting process, and another outside of Sedro-Woolley that is still working its way through the state siting process, which supersedes county control...The project is expected to wrap up by May 2027, with opportunities for public comment throughout the process.” [Cascadia Daily News, 06/03/2026, link to article here].  

Solar moratorium hearing scheduled: “The hearing on extending the county solar energy and battery storage moratorium and interim controls is set for June 15. The county adopted the moratorium while officials review regulations for utility-scale solar and battery storage projects.” [Whitman County, Gazette, 06/04/2026, link to article here].

Northern Rockies

Nothing to see here folks!

CO/NM/NV

NEW MEXICO

PNM seeks approval for 240MW solar and 610MW storage in New Mexico resource plant: “US utility PNM has filed a resource plan with the New Mexico Public Regulation Commission (NMPRC) seeking approval for 1.69GW of new generation and energy storage capacity to support growing electricity demand and the state’s clean energy targets…The resources are intended to replace capacity from the Four Corners Power Plant ahead of PNM’s planned coal exit in 2031, while supporting projected demand growth of 40% by 2032…” [PV Tech, 06/02/2026, link to article here].

AZ/UT

ARIZONA

[Tweet]: “4:30 p.m. Work Session. Presentation, discussion and public comment, pertaining to establishing a new Battery overlay zone related to Battery Energy Storage Systems” [@AZSurprise, 06/01/2026, link to tweet]

California

Major battery storage project proposed in San Jose’s Coyote Valley: “A Virginia energy company is proposing to build one of the largest battery storage plants in California in Coyote Valley, a rural expanse of farmland and scenic open space on San Jose’s southern edges…the facility would include thousands of lithium-ion batteries in 312 shipping containers, company officials say, spread across 40 acres…Under state law, AES still must complete an environmental impact report and hold public hearings. [Dave Cortese, D-San Jose] said opponents should sue if the project moves forward…Families at the Charter School of Morgan Hill, located along Monterey Road about 100 yards from the site, have collected more than 2,900 signatures on a petition opposing the project.” [The Mercury News, 06/02/2026, link to article here].

Daily Template 06/05

To:

staff@greenlightamerica.org

Subject

Daily Clean Energy Clips Month/Date/Year


Surprise, we couldn’t bear a whole week without Clips emails so we’re popping in to say the research team is meeting up in Chicago today and will be out today, tomorrow and Thursday. Prepare for Friday a.m. for a giant clip email summarizing clips for the rest of the week.

~ Rikki

Northeast

NEW YORK

Wawarsing mulls moratorium on data centers and battery storage facilities: “WAWARSING, N.Y. — Spurred by a letter from a company expressing interest in putting an AI data center on the banks of the West Branch Beer Kill, town officials will consider this week placing a six-month moratorium on both data centers and battery energy storage systems. Supervisor Joseph Stoeckeler said he didn’t consider the inquiry serious given the terrain of the land the firm indicated interest in is in a former town park on Old Greenfield Park Road…Stoeckeler said that he expects board members to enact the moratorium at its meeting on Thursday, June 4…Although the Wawarsing inquiry is the first known inquiry by a company looking to locate a data center in Ulster County, there are currently several proposals for battery energy storage systems in the county.” [Daily Freeman, 06/01/2026, link to article here]

Angelica residents concerned over impacts of possible battery storage facility: “In April, Angelica Town Supervisor Girard Kelly told fellow board members about a request by SeaTac Solar looking to build a battery energy storage system (BESS) facility between State Route 19 and the former I86 Repair…The Village of Angelica does have a planning board; however, the proposed site for the facility falls under the Town of Angelica, which has not had a planning board since 2006.” [WKBW, 05/30/2026, link to article here]

Mid-Atlantic

Nothing to see here, folks!

OH/PA

OHIO

[Tweet]: “The Ohio Supreme Court overturned a permit that state officials previously granted to a massive, 6,000-acre industrial-scale solar farm and battery operation in Madison County.” [KDKA Twitter, 06/01/2026, link to tweet here].

Southeast

ALABAMA

Poarch Creek Indians weigh in on large solar projects near tribal lands in Alabama: “Alabama’s only federally recognized tribe has weighed in publicly for the first time on the industrial-scale solar development happening near its tribal and historic lands in south Alabama…In a press release this week, the Poarch Creek Indians Tribal Council said it was gathering information on three industrial-scale solar projects in north Baldwin and Escambia counties: the Silicon Ranch solar facility in Stockton, a planned solar farm in the Tensaw region of Baldwin County, and a potential solar project in Escambia County, adjacent to the tribe’s current property…The tribe said its historic preservation office reached out to the developers of all three projects to proactively address any issues that may be of cultural and historical significance.” [Yahoo! News, 05/31/2026, link to article here]

SOUTH CAROLINA

South Carolina county lifts moratorium for 1,500-acre solar farm as divided residents largely boo vote: “A narrow county council vote led to shouts and boos from the crowd and a massive solar project moving one step closer to reality in rural South Carolina…At a tense Berkeley County Council meeting, members voted 3-2 to lift the large-scale development moratorium…The network reported that the vote removes a major obstacle for the planned 198-megawatt Sandy Run Solar facility, a project slated to span roughly 1,500 acres…The proposal next goes to the planning commission and could face several more public readings…” [Yahoo! News, 05/30/2026, link to article here].

Midwest

Nothing to see here, folks!

North-Central

Nothing to see here, folks!

Central-Central

IOWA

Union County refuses to budge on solar project: “Alliant Energy’s proposed amendment to their battery energy storage system likely reached a final roadblock as Union County officials refuse to budge on their standing moratorium on solar fields…The original permit allowed a 25 MW BESS storage system to be installed. Over the past few months, representatives from Alliant have requested a change in the permit to increase the storage system from 25 MW to 40. During a meeting on April 29, the county pushed back against the amendment. County supervisors approved a moratorium in 2025 which limited the construction of new solar fields in the county. They wished to see more information on taxation before proceeding with additional projects.” [Creston News Advertiser, 06/01/2026, link to article here]

MISSOURI

Winners of the 2026 Legislative Session: “At the beginning of session, Missouri’s solar industry faced what many viewed as a serious threat amid discussions of a potential moratorium that could have hindered the state’s competitiveness in the growing solar market. By the end of session, however, those proposals had largely stalled out. Instead, the debate appeared to strengthen support for solar development in parts of rural Missouri, where many landowners framed the issue through the lens of private property rights. As session concluded, Missouri’s solar industry appeared to be in a far stronger position than many observers initially anticipated.” [Missouri Times, 05/29/2026, link to article here].  

South-Central

Nothing to see here, folks!

Plains

WASHINGTON

Mount Vernon council extends moratorium on BESS facilities: “The Mount Vernon City Council voted Wednesday to extend the city's moratorium on battery energy storage systems, or BESS, for six months while the city continues to develop permanent regulations.” [Tri-City Herald, 05/29/2026, link to article here]

Midwest

MICHIGAN

West Michigan township weighs one-year moratorium on data centers, solar farms “Allendale Township leaders are considering putting a one-year moratorium in place preventing the development of any data centers, battery storage facilities, large-scale solar farms and wind farms. The moratorium would mean the township wouldn’t accept any applications from developers to build these kinds of projects for one year. The township’s board of trustees will vote on the proposed measure at its June 8 meeting.” [MLive, 5/28/26, link to article]

Richland Township residents pack third solar project hearing as decision still months away “At least 100 Richland Township residents were at Gull Lake Middle School Wednesday evening for a third continuation of a public hearing about a proposed large-scale solar farm project that could reshape more than 2,200 acres of Kalamazoo County farmland. The Liberty Farms Solar Energy Center, proposed by Consumers Energy, would generate 220 megawatts of electricity and power roughly 52,000 homes. The project has been working through Richland Township’s special exception use and site plan approval process since Consumers filed its application earlier this year.” [News Channel 3, 5/28/26, link to article]

Pacific Northwest

WASHINGTON

Mount Vernon council extends moratorium on BESS facilities “The Mount Vernon City Council voted Wednesday to extend the city’s moratorium on battery energy storage systems, or BESS, for six months while the city continues to develop permanent regulations. The moratorium restricts the city from accepting applications for the siting, developing and constructing of BESS facilities. The moratorium has been in place since May 2025. Mount Vernon Principal Planner Rebecca Lowell told the council Wednesday that the city placed a moratorium on BESS facilities because the city code didn’t have an allowance for them.” [Go Skagit, 5/29/26, link to article]

California

New Leaf Energy withdraws permit application for Watsonville battery storage facility “New Leaf Energy has withdrawn its permit application from Santa Cruz County to build a battery storage facility just outside of Watsonville. The company said it will instead seek approval through the California Energy Commission, bypassing the county. New Leaf Energy said amendments added by Santa Cruz County supervisors in January will create delays and financial risks that make the local permitting path "non-viable."” [KSBW, 5/28/26, link to article]

Fiscal Court considers wind energy moratorium “Members of the McLean County Fiscal Court held the first reading of an ordinance requesting a temporary moratorium on wind energy conversion system applications during its recent regular meeting. The McLean County Planning and Zoning Committee approved 11 findings of fact on the ordinance request submitted by the Fiscal Court previously, following the court's addition of an amendment during a prior meeting.” [Messenger Inquirer, 5/28/26, link to article]

Finney County Commissioners to consider Special Use Permit requests for Sherlock Solar & Sherlock Energy Storage projects “The Finney County Board of County Commissioners will consider Special Use Permit requests for the proposed Sherlock Solar and Sherlock Energy Storage projects at the June 1, 2026, meeting. The meeting will be held at 8:30 a.m. at the Finney County Administration Building, located at 311 North 9th Street, Garden City, Kansas. If approved, the projects would include a 400 MW solar energy system and a 400 MW battery energy storage system, both located in Finney County and reviewed under the County’s Special Use Permit process.” [Western Kansas News, 5/28/26, link to article]

Northeast

NEW YORK

Fort Edward solar project approved by New York State, siting permit issued “A 100-megawatt solar project in Fort Edward has received state approval. The New York State Department of Public Service announced Tuesday that the Office of Renewable Energy Siting and Electric Transmission issued a final siting permit to Boralex LLC for the Fort Edward Solar Project, a large-scale facility planned in the Town of Fort Edward.” [CBS6, 5/26/26, link to article]

Southeast

SOUTH CAROLINA

Berkeley County Council lifts moratorium for solar farm project in 3-2 vote amid outcry “Tense moments returned to the Berkeley County Council chambers as council members voted to lift a large-scale development moratorium, clearing a key hurdle for a proposed solar farm in the Cross community despite loud opposition from residents. The motion passed 3-2, with three council members recusing themselves from voting… Lifting the moratorium is a step forward for energy company RWE, which is seeking to build the Sandy Run Solar project, a 198-megawatt solar facility that would cover about 1,500 acres in the rural community.” [ABC4, 5/27/26, link to article]

ARKANSAS

Wind turbine plan moves forward after Crittenden County vote “Wind turbines are one step closer to coming to rural Crittenden County. Tuesday, the Crittenden County Quorum Court unanimously approved an ordinance allowing Triple Oak to submit its application to build a wind turbine. It’s a big step, but far from the final one.” [WREG Memphis, 5/26/26, link to article]

Plains

NEBRASKA

Lincoln County approves permits for major power line project “Lincoln County commissioners voted to approve conditional use permits for Nebraska Public Power District’s R-Project, a transmission line stretching from Sutherland to Holt County. The permits cover five temporary construction yards, helicopter landing pads and smaller staging areas known as “fly yards.” The sites will be set up at multiple leased locations across the county. NPPD says the R-Project will improve reliability and reduce electrical congestion across its power system.” [KNOP News 2, 5/26/26, link to article]

AZ/UT

Nothing to see here, folks!

California

CEC Approves Third Major Clean Energy Project Under Opt-In Certification Program “The California Energy Commission (CEC) on Tuesday approved the Potentia-Viridi Battery Energy Storage System through its Opt-In Certification program, marking the third major clean energy project approved under the accelerated permitting pathway in the past year. Levy Alameda, LLC (Levy Alameda), a subsidiary of Clearway Energy Group, will construct and operate a 400 MW battery energy storage system (BESS) in eastern Alameda County. Once built, the project will charge and store energy when there is excess solar or low-cost electricity on the grid and discharge up to 3,200 megawatt-hours (MWh) of stored energy when needed.” [CEC, 5/26/26, link to press release]

Defying Trump, California continues to bet big on offshore wind “While the Trump administration takes extraordinary measures to halt the development of offshore wind power in the United States, Southern California is advancing a $4.7-billion plan to deploy hundreds of towering wind turbines in waters off the state’s coast. The proposed Pier Wind project at the Port of Long Beach is a 400-acre terminal for the positioning, storage and assembly of some of the world’s largest offshore wind turbines, which would be towed north to federal wind lease areas some 20 miles off Morro and Humboldt bays… California’s approach is to push forward with offshore wind preparations that fall within its jurisdiction, readying the ports and the power grid to eventually take on electricity from 1,000 turbines in federal waters. The aim is to wait out the current administration, which is notoriously hostile toward a form of renewable energy that is booming elsewhere in the world.” [LA Times, 5/24/26, link to article]

Daily Clips 5/27

To:

staff@greenlightamerica.org

Subject

Daily Clean Energy Clips 5/27/26


Northeast

NEW YORK

Blanket BESS Prohibition May Not Work In Ellicott “In August 2024 the county IDA sold about 12 acres of vacant land on Dow Street, south of Allen Street, in Ellicott to Granite Source Power. Granite Source Power officials told IDA officials the company wants to install a 100-megawatt battery storage project on the site. Essentially, the project is described as a shipping container one would see on a freightliner filled with racks of modules made up of batteries… The local law Ellicott is proposing prohibits the construction, maintenance and placement of Battery Storage Systems and Equipment in the town of Ellicott. A public hearing on the local law will be held on Tuesday, June 2, in the Town Council Chambers at 5:30 p.m.” [Post-Journal, 5/26/26, link to article]

Mid-Atlantic

KENTUCKY

Planning commission hears recommendations to mitigate noise, vibration and shadow flicker coming from wind turbines “The nearly three-hour public hearing was the second of seven scheduled meetings used to gather information and hear public testimony before the planning commission writes an ordinance regarding wind energy conversion systems. That ordinance will then be sent to the Henderson Fiscal Court, which can approve it, decline it or send it back to the planning commission for further work. Holding the meetings, gathering the information and then writing an ordinance comes to the planning commission as a directive from the fiscal court. The next WECS public hearing will be 6 p.m. Wednesday, July 15, in the Fiscal Court courtroom. The topic of that hearing will be environmental and ecological impacts.” [The Hendersonian, 5/23/26, link to article]

OH/PA

Nothing to see here, folks!

Southeast

SOUTH CAROLINA

Solar project looks to set up in Cross. Residents worry risks outweigh benefits. “A German energy company is looking to turn 1,500 acres of forest and farmland in northwestern Berkeley County into a large solar farm. Residents in the surrounding area, however, believe the costs of the project outweigh the benefits it could provide. RWE Solar Development has asked Berkeley County Council to lift a large-scale development moratorium in order to proceed with the zoning process for a planned solar farm, Sandy Run Solar. In a request submitted to County Council, the company says it will build solar arrays, or panels, across 1,500 acres as part of a 7,500-acre planned development.” [Post and Courier, 5/24/26, link to article]

Midwest

MICHIGAN

Fayette Twp Planning Commission Schedules June Meeting On Solar Site Plan “The Fayette Township Planning Commission has scheduled a June meeting to continue an ongoing public hearing on a special use permit for the Heartwood II Solar project. The meeting, which is set for Monday, June 1st, will be a continuation of a session that was cut short for time on April 16th and again on May 7th.” [Radio Hillsdale, 5/22/26, link to article]

North-Central

WISCONSIN

Kenosha County votes to pause battery facility, project may still move forward “On Tuesday night — the Kenosha County Board of Supervisors voted to adopt a one-year moratorium on the approval of new battery energy storage systems in the county. County Executive Samantha Kerkman specifically requested the moratorium — citing mounting public health and safety concerns from residents. The resolution specifically cites potential health and safety issues including toxic gases, fire hazards, and impacts on air and water quality. But Robin Energy Storage is not backing down. The company sent a letter to the county board the night of the vote — saying they were not notified of the resolution until that same day — and asking the board to decline action. The board voted anyway. Director of Project Development Jan Porvaznik told TMJ4 News the company is evaluating other options — including going directly to the Wisconsin Public Service Commission — a state level body whose approval could potentially override the county moratorium entirely..” [Newsbreak, 5/22/26, link to article]

Central-Central

Nothing to see here, folks!

South-Central

Nothing to see here, folks!

Plains

Nothing to see here, folks!

Pacific Northwest

WASHINGTON

City of North Bend Unanimously Passes BESS Moratorium “A packed house turned out on May 19th as North Bend council members voted unanimously to adopt a 12-month moratorium on battery energy storage systems amid growing opposition to the proposed Cascadia Ridge project.” [Living Snoqualmie, 5/22/26, link to article]

Northern Rockies

Nothing to see here, folks!

5/25 Daily Clips

To:

staff@greenlightamerica.org

Subject

Daily Clean Energy Clips 5/25/26

Hope everyone enjoyed the long weekend! Cheers!

  • Jasir Rahman

Northeast

NEW YORK

BESS ban proposed in St. Armand “The St. Armand town council is proposing a local law to ban utility-scale battery electric storage systems townwide, and a public hearing on the potential law has been set for June 22 at 5:30 p.m. in the town hall.The town is also considering a solar farm moratorium, as an array has been proposed here. The council is also asking residents if they want the town to implement a zoning code.” [Gazette News Group, Inc., 05/22/2026, link to article]

North Elba to possibly enact temporary moratorium “Town officials are considering imposing a temporary moratorium on major solar and battery energy storage systems (BESS)... The supervisor anticipates that a final decision on whether a temporary moratorium is necessary will be made at the next town board meeting on June 9 at 5:30 p.m.” [suncommunitynews.com, 05/22/2026, link to article]

Bill to ban battery storage in parts of Staten Island stalls in state legislature “A bill proposed by Assemblymember Michael Tannousis that would prohibited the siting and construction of battery energy storage systems in his district has stalled… The legislation, which Tannousis sponsored in March, calls for a ban on battery energy storage systems, commonly referred to as BESS, in his district, which covers the East Shore of Staten Island and parts of South Brooklyn.” [silive, 05/22/2026, link to article]

Cortlandville extends moratorium on solar energy systems “A temporary ban in Cortlandville is still in effect. On May 6, the Cortlandville Town Board extended a moratorium on approving new solar energy systems. The ban began in October. All board members voted in favor of the extension, which will stretch into August.” [607 News Now - Cortland & Ithaca, NY News, Weather & Events, 05/22/2026, link to article]

Town board in Franklin County tables battery discussion “The Tupper Lake Town Board tabled any action in regards to a potential moratorium on battery storage facilities last week until its members have had more time to review arguments in both directions. At its May 11 meeting, the board heard public comment from area resident Andy Foster. He didn’t expressly speak against a moratorium, but encouraged the elected officials — if they do ultimately pass one — to create an accompanying community task force and to limit its duration.” [NNY360, 05/23/2026, link to article]

Guilderland adopts BESS moratorium “The Guilderland Town Board this week voted unanimously to place a short-term halt on anything having to do with the installation of commercial battery energy storage systems as it looks to draft regulations for governing large-scale energy storage facilities in town. The four-month moratorium adopted by the board on Tuesday allows for the installation of residential systems, and also comes with the almost certainty of needing to be extended by the time September rolls around.” [The Altamont Enterprise, 5/21/26, link to article]

MASSACHUSETTS

Fitchburg City Council Sets Solar Moratorium Hearing Date for June 3rd (See meeting video here.) [YouTube, 05/19/2026, link to article]

Mid-Atlantic

VIRGINIA

Concerns pause [Buckingham County] rezoning request [and solar ordinance hearing] “[June 8 at 6 PM ]will be the time for a public hearing concerning updating solar policy standards in the Buckingham County’s zoning ordinance and comprehensive plans to comply with legislation approved during the General Assembly session. Information on the changes was first introduced to the planning commission March 23 led to a public hearing April 27 and its unanimous recommendation for approval.” [NewsBreak, 05/21/2026, link to article]

OH/PA

OHIO

[Hillclimber Solar Withdraws Application] “Hillclimber Solar, a RWE-backed utility solar and storage project, this week withdrew its application to the Ohio Power Siting Board. Days earlier, project opponents packed a key OPSB hearing in the county, which has a solar moratorium. RWE was able to avoid the temporary ban because its agreements with PJM happened before it was enacted. But OPSB has tended to frown on tense situations like these. Ohio used to be a fine place for a solar farm but no more, thanks to changes in state law and regulators sympathetic to local opposition. Solar developer Open Road Renewables pledged in March to never develop another project in the state, and I wouldn’t be surprised to see more join them.” [NewsBreak (via Jael Hozman), 05/21/2026, link to article]

Southeast

Nothing to see here, folks!

Midwest

INDIANA

Solar Moratorium Denied by Commissioners “The Pulaski County Advisory Plan Commission (APC) had recently recommended a 12 month moratorium on commercial solar energy systems (CSES) with a 6-0 vote. However, on Monday, May 4, the commissioners voted down the moratorium request with a 2-1 vote. The county currently has moratoriums on data centers and Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS)... cRedweik clarified that the proposed moratorium would only pause the filing and the acceptance of any new applications for any commercial solar energy systems in Pulaski County for a period of 12 months.” [pulaskijournal.com, 5/21/26, link to article]

ILLINOIS

Madison County Board Does Not Grant Special Use Permit to Proposed Glen Carbon Solar Farm “The Madison County Board did not vote on a motion to grant a special use permit to Trentino Solar, LLC, to develop a commercial solar energy facility in Glen Carbon. During the Board’s regular meeting on Wednesday, May 20, 2026, several representatives with Trentino Solar and a volunteer with the Illinois Solar Education Association spoke in favor of the project during the public comments section of the meeting [, Mayor Bob Marcus submitted a letter against the project]... When the Building and Zoning Committee reviewed this project, there was no motion or second, so the committee ultimately did not vote on whether to recommend the project’s approval or denial… The Board then was asked to vote on whether to approve or deny the special use permit to Trentino Solar. Alison Lamothe made a motion to vote. There was no second, so the motion failed.” [EdGlenToday.com, 05/21/2026, link to article]

North-Central

WISCONSIN

Kenosha County votes to pause battery storage facility, but the project may still move forward “ On Tuesday night — the Kenosha County Board of Supervisors voted to adopt a one-year moratorium on the approval of new battery energy storage systems in the county. County Executive Samantha Kerkman specifically requested the moratorium — citing mounting public health and safety concerns from residents. The resolution specifically cites potential health and safety issues including toxic gases, fire hazards, and impacts on air and water quality. But Robin Energy Storage is not backing down. The company sent a letter to the county board the night of the vote — saying they were not notified of the resolution until that same day — and asking the board to decline action. The board voted anyway. Director of Project Development Jan Porvaznik told TMJ4 News the company is evaluating other options — including going directly to the Wisconsin Public Service Commission — a state level body whose approval could potentially override the county moratorium entirely.” [WTMJ-TV, 5/22/26, link to article]

Central-Central

IOWA

Supervisors again fail to schedule ordinance hearing “Wapello County supervisors Darren Batterson and Bryan Ziegler once again were unable to schedule a public hearing for a renewable energy ordinance during Tuesday's meeting at the courthouse. It's the second time the pair has been unable to agree to set a hearing for an ordinance that was crafted through a one-sided mediation session in February between Batterson and attorney Eric Updegraff, with little input from Ziegler.” [Yahoo News, 05/20/2026, link to article]

South-Central

TEXAS

[Opinion] Defense Department rides with Trump’s mendacious assault on wind energy “The Defense Department, undoubtedly at Trump’s insistence, is working to achieve through stonewalling what the administration could not through other shenanigans: to stop or significantly slow the development of wind energy in the U.S. In Texas, 54 such projects are among 165 nationwide in limbo as the Defense Department claims to still be reviewing them to ensure they don’t impair national security. But those reviews, triggered by proposed structures 200 feet and taller and which federal law requires to be completed within 60 days, have ground to a halt with no reasonable explanation. Not only has the department not cleared a wind project since August, in April it canceled all meetings with wind developers waiting for action, the American Clean Power Association reported. Jason Grumet, CEO of the ACP, told the Associated Press that the backlog is unprecedented, calling it a “de facto moratorium” on new land-based wind energy.” [San Antonio Express-News, 05/22/2026, link to article]

Plains

SOUTH DAKOTA

Pennington County approves [renewables] zoning change, will return for vote on June 16  “Pennington County Commission voted 3-2 to add amendments to the county's zoning ordinance that ban utility-scale wind and solar on agricultural land. [The Commissioners will return for a final vote on June 16.]” [Rapid City Journal, 05/25/2026, link to article]

Company Seeks Permit for $174 Million Battery Energy Storage Facility in Codington County “A Florida-based company is seeking approval from South Dakota regulators to build a $174 million battery energy storage system... The project would be located on 53 acres within the existing Crowned Ridge wind energy project, 13 miles northeast of Watertown. The permit application to the Public Utilities Commission was filed this month… The Public Utilities Commission will hold a public input meeting about the project at 6 p.m. Central time on July 1 at the Waverly South Shore School gymnasium, at 319 Mary Place in Waverly. The company hopes to have the project in operation by May 2028.” [mykxlg.com, 05/22/2026, link to article]

Pacific Northwest

OREGON

Crook County weighs moratorium on new solar farms – Central Oregon Daily “Crook County leaders are considering putting a pause on new solar farms. Members of the public have raised concerns about the pace and scale of solar development. Key concerns are on the impact on agriculture and wildlife. The Crook County Board of Commissioners has asked staff to conduct research on the topic. A moratorium would pause applications for all new large-scale solar development. No formal decision has been made, but staff are in the process of coming up with recommendations.” [Solar Now, 05/21/2026, link to article]

WASHINGTON

Avangrid signs power purchase agreement with Puget Sound Energy for wind facility “Avangrid signed a long-term power purchase agreement with Puget Sound Energy (PSE) for the Big Horn I wind energy project in Klickitat County, Washington. Big Horn I is a wind energy project that was first built by Avangrid in 2006. The 199.5 megawatts (MW) Big Horn I facility can generate enough electricity for about 70,000 homes annually.” [Daily Energy Insider, 05/21/2026, link to article]

OH/PA

PENNSYLVANIA

West Rockhill weighs battery storage project amid data center concerns “As West Rockhill Township officials work to address concerns from the community about data centers moving into the area, a landowner is now seeking permission to develop a battery storage system on Branch Road. Energy Mountain LLC, also known as Heritage Meadow, is seeking a special zoning exception to permit an F1— Utility known as a Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) at 25 Branch Road and on the parcel directly across the street… The West Rockhill Township Zoning Hearing Board will conduct a public hearing on this request at the Tylersport Fire House at 125 Ridge Road in Sellersville on June 3 at 7 p.m.” [Newsbreak, 5/20/26, link to article]

Southeast

Nothing to see here, folks!

Midwest

MICHIGAN

"Quite a quick change for local governments" as MI looks to boost battery energy storage “Michigan has ambitious clean energy laws on the books, while also facing a surging demand for power. What’s less known is that it also has an ambitious law for utility-scale battery energy storage, which experts say is needed to make the grid state’s grid work as it undergoes a major power shift. That means there are lots of battery projects in the pipeline across the state, and that local governments have to make some quick decisions about how to handle them — often without much guidance.” [Michigan Public, 5/21/26, link to article]

INDIANA

Solar amendments approved pending further ordinance review “The Knox County Commissioners on Tuesday approved amendments to an existing ordinance they say will better protect county residents from future solar development. But more changes may be on the horizon. “This is something that will give us more protection for right now,” Commission President T.J. Brink said. “We’ll be coming back to the table at a later date with additional things to add to this.” “This is simply the first step in that.” Brink called the newly amended county solar ordinance a “stopgap” measure intended to buy the county time to take a deeper look at the legislation — originally passed in 2020 to establish guidelines such as setbacks and screening requirements for solar development projects — to determine whether additional changes are needed.” [Vincennes Sun-Commercial, 5/21/26, link to article]

North-Central

MINNESOTA

Environmental concerns drive recommendation to deny Shell Rock River BESS  “After more than a year of public opposition, environmental review and contentious hearings, a Minnesota judge has recommended denying permits for a battery energy storage system, or BESS, near the Shell Rock River, marking a major development in a debate that has drawn concern from residents and officials on both sides of the Iowa-Minnesota border.” [Waverly Newspapers, 5/20/26, link to article]

Central-Central

Nothing to see here, folks!

South-Central

KANSAS

Commissioners push back vote on solar project near Clearwater “Hours of public comment hold back a solar project proposal in Sedgwick County. The Branch Line Project, submitted by the energy system developer TED Renewables, is one of several projects being considered in Sedgwick County… After hearing from the public, the commission voted to push back a decision, but tasked staff with researching several things concerning the project. These include water usage, fire protection and emergency response, noise from inverters, financial impact on the county, and a possible alternate property. They will take up the discussion again on June 25.” [KSNW, 5/20/26, link to article]

Plains

SOUTH DAKOTA

Pennington County weighs revised alternative energy ordinance as residents raise concerns “A newly revised alternative energy proposal in Pennington County would separate small-scale solar and wind systems meant to power a home or farm from utility-scale projects designed to feed electricity to the grid. Under the proposal, homeowners could still install smaller systems, but with added limits, such as prohibiting solar panels or wind systems in residential front yards. For larger projects, the rules would add new requirements. Developers would need a Conditional Use Permit and would be required to meet a noise limit near homes, along with other standards tied to siting and operations.” [KOTA, 5/20/26, link to article]

To:

staff@greenlightamerica.org

Subject

Daily Clean Energy Clips 5/21/26


Northeast

NEW YORK

150+ Local Governments Have Moved to Block Battery Storage Development, New Database Reveals “At least 150 local governments across 17 US states have enacted moratoriums, bans, or restrictive ordinances targeting battery energy storage systems (BESS), according to the first publicly available nationwide database of such restrictions. New York leads the country with 98 moratoriums… States including Indiana, Michigan, Iowa, and Maine are seeing a rising wave of new restrictions as utility-scale battery projects expand beyond traditional renewable energy markets.”  [Newsnet Jacksonville, 5/20/26, link to article]

Mortarium enacted for the Town of Clay on battery energy storage facility proposals “The Town of Clay will not accept or approve zoning requests for special permit requests related to battery energy storage systems, due to a six month-long moratorium enacted in April. It comes as site proposals been been under review by the town board since February, from companies Carson Power and Nexamp.”  [CNY Central, 5/20/26, link to article]

Mid-Atlantic

VIRGINIA

Preservation, environmental groups say Valley Link transmission line risks Va.’s natural resources “Ten historic preservation and environmental groups are cautioning against the potential impacts of the proposed high-powered Valley Link transmission line that will span 115-miles across Virginia, casting it as a danger to the state’s natural resources. The high-voltage line that will stretch from just outside Lynchburg to Culpeper County would be the largest transmission project in Dominion Energy’s territory.” [Virginia Mercury, 5/20/26, link to article, more on this here]

OH/PA

OHIO

Ohio utilities report subpar grid reliability as they seek a lower bar “Looming large over the debate is the fact that Ohio leaders and utilities have blocked measures that could have improved reliability over the years. With 2019’s scandal-plagued House Bill 6, legislators gutted the state’s clean energy standards, which could have spurred the development of more solar and wind power that may relieve long-distance transmission congestion or provide alternate generation when fossil-fuel or nuclear power plants are offline.” [Canary Media, 5/18/26, link to article]

PENNSYLVANIA

West Rockhill weighs battery storage project amid data center concerns “Energy Mountain LLC, also known as Heritage Meadow, is seeking a special zoning exception to permit an F1— Utility known as a Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) at 25 Branch Road and on the parcel directly across the street. The West Rockhill Township Zoning Hearing Board will conduct a public hearing on this request at the Tylersport Fire House at 125 Ridge Road in Sellersville on June 3 at 7 p.m.”  [Phillyburbs, 5/20/26, link to article]

Southeast

Nothing to see here, folks!

Midwest

MICHIGAN

DTE Energy seeks 1GW of solar, wind capacity in Michigan “Michigan power utility DTE Energy has issued a tender for 1GW of new solar PV and wind power projects across the state. The request for proposals (RfP) calls for projects that can be operational by the end of 2029, connected to the Midcontinent Independent System Operator (MISO) transmission network or the DTE distribution network.” [PV Tech, 5/19/2026, link to article]

WISCONSIN

“We're not backing down': Wheatland residents confront officials at heated forum for proposed battery facility “A community forum held by Robin Energy Storage turned heated Tuesday night — as Wheatland residents came face to face with the company behind a proposed 200-megawatt lithium-ion battery energy storage facility that would be built on farmland near homes and a grade school.” [TMJ4, 5/20/26, link to article, more on this here]

North-Central

KANSAS

Commissioners push back vote on solar project near Clearwater “WICHITA, Kan. (KSNW) — Hours of public comment hold back a solar project proposal in Sedgwick County. The Branch Line Project, submitted by the energy system developer TED Renewables, is one of several projects being considered in Sedgwick County. It would take over nearly 1,400 acres of land north and northeast of Clearwater for solar farms.”  [KSNW Wichita, 5/20/26, link to article]

Ellis County Commission discusses solar project permit, contribution agreement “The Ellis County Commission discussed project documents relating to the proposed Tallgrass Solar Development during its Tuesday meeting. The discussion included a conditional-use permit and contribution agreement… Members of the public were given the opportunity to provide input, but nobody chose to do so.”  [Hays Post, 5/19/2026, link to article]

Central-Central

IOWA

Iowa Supreme Court overturns decision in Worth County wind turbine moratorium battle “The Iowa Supreme Court on a six-to-one vote has reversed a Worth County district court judge’s decision that allowed a company to proceed with their wind energy project despite a moratorium on development in the county.”  [KGLO News, 5/18/20, link to article]

South-Central

OKLAHOMA

Oklahoma bill ending solar, battery storage tax exemption heads to governor’s desk “A bill eliminating a tax exemption for certain solar and battery storage projects is headed to the governor's desk. Senate Bill 237 phases out Oklahoma's five year manufacturer ad valorem tax exemption for solar generation and battery storage facilities beginning in 2028. Supporters say large energy companies should not receive special tax breaks at the expense of Oklahoma taxpayers.” [Fox 25, 5/19/2026, link to article]

Plains

SOUTH DAKOTA

Pennington County weighs revised alternative energy ordinance as residents raise concerns “For larger projects…Developers would need a Conditional Use Permit and would be required to meet a noise limit near homes, along with other standards tied to siting and operations. Some residents say shifting large-scale projects toward industrial-zoned areas does not automatically address neighborhood impacts—because many industrial zones sit close to Rapid City.”  [Kota Territory News, 5/20/26, link to article]

CO/NM/NV

COLORADO

Rio Grande County takes up solar regulations “The first public hearing is before the Rio Grande Planning and Zoning Commission beginning at 2:30 p.m. at the Rio Grande County Courthouse on May 19. The county commissioners are then scheduled to take up the solar regulations during a public hearing at 1:30 p.m. in the county commissioner chambers on May 20. The regulations will govern any major solar energy facilities or community energy solar facilities proposed for Rio Grande County.”  [Alamosa Citizen, 5/15/26, link to article]

AZ/UT

UTAH

‘The landowners’ right’: As costs rise, some Utah ranchers are leasing fields out for solar projects ‘rather than alfalfa’ “Greg Kesler’s sprawling ranch in Millard County has more than doubled in size over the past decade, stretching across a large swath of high desert land… About 1,100 acres of his Double Dollar Ranch are covered in a patchwork of solar panels. He leases the land to solar developers that built and own the panels, and the passive income is a welcome windfall… In rural Utah, solar is also reshaping school budgets.”  [Salt Lake Tribune, 5/15/26, link to article]

Northeast

NEW HAMPSHIRE

Eversource misclassified $385M transmission project to avoid scrutiny: ratepayer complaint “The dispute comes amid growing concerns about electric affordability across the United States and the role transmission construction can play in utility bills, including in New England. At the same time, ratepayer advocates and others have been pressing for stricter reviews of local projects that are planned by utilities with relatively little regulatory oversight.” [UtilityDive, 5/14/26, link to article]

Commentary: Offshore wind is meeting the moment “Offshore wind is clean, affordable, and reliable energy operating just off our coast. It provides thousands of jobs for New England residents, powers our residences and businesses with home-grown electrons, and lowers harmful carbon emissions. Our region is now an offshore wind leader with two projects delivering hundreds of megawatts of energy to the grid, the Vineyard Wind project in Massachusetts waters and the Revolution Wind Project off the coast of Rhode Island.” [New Hampshire Bulletin, 5/15/26, link to article]

Mid-Atlantic

Data centers drive 76% surge in PJM power prices “The findings highlight inflationary pressures on electricity costs in mid-Atlantic and Midwestern states. ‘The price impacts on customers have been very large and are not reversible,’ the grid market monitor said.” [E&E News, 5/15/26, link to article]

PENNSYLVANIA

Advocates push for renewable energy growth in Pennsylvania “New data from PennEnvironment shows Pennsylvania ranks 47th in the nation for renewable energy growth over the past decade. Advocates gathered at the state Capitol Rotunda to highlight the findings and push for change.” [WGAL, 5/13/26, link to article]

OH/PA

Nothing to see here, folks!

NextEra Energy to Acquire Dominion, Creating a Utility Giant “NextEra Energy, one of the largest power companies in the United States, on Monday announced plans to acquire Dominion Energy, in an all-stock deal that could transform the power industry as it races to supply electricity needed to fuel the booming growth of artificial intelligence.” [New York Times, 5/17/26,  link to article]

NORTH CAROLINA

North Carolina groups fight regulator’s order to cancel solar for 2026 “Last month, North Carolina’s top utility regulator abruptly halted Duke Energy’s solar farm investments for the year — an unforeseen blow to an industry already reeling from tariffs and a hostile federal government. Now, clean energy businesses and advocates have filed a motion to cancel the order, which they argue is not just ​“arbitrary and capricious,” but also at odds with a 2021 state climate law requiring Duke, North Carolina’s predominant utility, to zero out its carbon pollution by midcentury.”  [Canary Media, 5/14/26, link to article]

Defense Department delays 54 wind projects in Texas, citing national security concerns “According to data collected by the American Clean Power Association, 54 Texas wind projects are waiting for the department to review development plans to ensure that turbines don’t interfere with military operations. Northeast

NEW YORK

One by one, Adirondack towns put brakes on battery storage facilities “From the southern park town of Northampton, to northeastern Crown Point, residents are attending public hearings in droves, crowding into the halls and out the door of town halls, community centers and firehouses, to show their opposition to battery energy storage facilities. Town boards are listening and passing moratoriums, adding to more than 100 municipalities across the state, which have passed similar delays.” [Times Union, 5/18/26, link to article]

Tupper Lake Town Board tables battery discussion “The board agreed to table any action on a potential battery storage facility moratorium and a potential community task force on the matter until its next meeting.” [The Daily Gazette, 5/17/26, link to article]

Ticonderoga Reschedules Hearing on Battery Storage Moratorium “The Town of Ticonderoga has moved the public hearing on a proposed Local Law that would temporarily stop new Battery Energy Storage Systems from being located within the town. The hearing will now take place on Monday, May 18, 2026, at 6:00 p.m. in the Ticonderoga High School Auditorium.” [Newsbreak, 5/16/26, link to article]

Henry County places moratorium on AI data centers, data mines and battery storage facilities from locating in county “The Henry County Board of Supervisors placed a moratorium on siting applications to the county to construct AI data centers, data mines and battery energy storage systems here at is regular weekly meeting Thursday.The moratorium went into effect as of noon Thursday and will continue until December 31. Discussion on the facilities will be included with further discussion on solar and wind energy ordinances later this year.” [The Hawk Eye, 5/16/26, link to article]

Clark County Plan Commission tables solar facility amendment “The Clark County Plan Commission voted to table a change to the unified development ordinance preventing solar energy facilities from building on farms. Want to pack a public meeting in Kansas? Just say it's about a data center “The first part of the Finney County proposal was a February application for a special use permit to rezone some agricultural land for industrial use, to allow a solar panel array. The application was submitted by Lone Bison Solar — a subsidiary of Tyr Energy Development Renewables based in Overland Park and owned by Itochu Corp. of Japan — with the intent to potentially build a 3,853-acre solar field (roughly six square miles) that would generate 330 megawatts of electricity.”  [KCUR, 5/18/26, link to article]

Ellis Co. Commission to discuss law enforcement center project, solar farm “Commissioners also will discuss the proposed Tallgrass Solar Development and related project documents, including a conditional-use permit and contribution agreement. No action will be taken…Members of the public wishing to speak will be able to sign up before the meeting begins.”  [Hays Post, 5/18/26, link to article]

AZ/UT

ARIZONA

AC P&Z considers path to moratorium as residents press wind concerns “A standing-room crowd returned to St. Johns on May 6, one day after a packed Apache County Board of Supervisors meeting, as the Apache County Planning and Zoning Commission’s combined working session and public meeting focused heavily on wind projects, the county’s renewable energy ordinance rewrite and growing calls for a moratorium on new applications. The moratorium issue was discussed by commissioners during the working session and returned repeatedly during public comment.” [White Mountain Independent, 5/17/26 , link to article]

5/18/28

To:

staff@greenlightamerica.org

Subject

Daily Clean Energy Clips 5/18/2026

Northeast

NEW YORK

Ticonderoga residents raise concerns over potential lithium battery plant: Residents in Ticonderoga voiced strong opposition to a proposed lithium battery energy storage facility, citing environmental impacts, water usage for cooling, potential runoff into local water systems, and long-term cleanup responsibilities. Town leaders are considering a six-month moratorium to review local laws and scientific data on battery storage. Town Supervisor James O’Bryan stated, “Moratorium obviously gets a chance to look at not only our local laws, but also any other facts, any other sciences that revolve around battery energy storage.” Resident Carol Ramundo said, “My stance is definitely no,” and added concerns about water use: “These plants also use a tremendous amount of water to cool their batteries. So where is that going to go?” A public hearing will be held to gather input before a vote. [MyNBC5, May 14 2026, link to article]

NEW YORK

Chateaugay officials talk Wind Repowering Project: Town officials in Chateaugay (Franklin and Clinton counties) held a public hearing on the proposed repowering of the existing Chateaugay Wind Park from 106.5 MW to 116.5 MW by Valcour Chateaugay Newco LLC (AES subsidiary). The project involves replacing older turbines with larger, more efficient models on ~803 acres and is under review by the Office of Renewable Energy Siting (ORES). The project is expected to power 21,009 homes and continue economic contributions (over $14 million since 2009). Town Supervisor Donald Bilow noted the ORES process has been challenging. Fire Chief Gerry Blow praised AES as “great neighbors.” Landowner Bruce Bennett expressed strong support, saying AES helped fund his children’s college education. Written comments were due May 15, 2026. [Yahoo News, May 15 2026, link to article]

Mid-Atlantic

OHIO

Solar power hearing in Champaign County draws large crowd: A public hearing for RWE Clean Energy’s proposed Hillclimber Solar & Storage project (116.5 MW solar + 40 MW battery storage on 628 acres in Urbana Township) drew about 40 speakers. Opponents raised concerns about farmland loss, wildlife, and community character. James Cropper stated, “Taking thousands of acres of farmland to companies… they’re going to be making money off our backs.” Supporters emphasized energy needs and landowner rights. The Ohio Power Siting Board staff recommended denial; an evidentiary hearing is scheduled for May 26. [Journal-News, May 14 2026, link to article]

Southeast

SOUTH CAROLINA

Berkeley citizens speak out against large solar farm: Residents in the Cross community of Berkeley County strongly opposed RWE’s proposed 198-megawatt Sandy Run Solar project on 1,500 acres. The Berkeley County Council voted to lift the large-scale development moratorium in the area, with a final vote scheduled for May 26. Resident Robert Barrett said, “This is not a good thing for the community of Cross… There will be big and little promises made, but the bottom line is it will destroy 7,500 acres for wildlife for no reason.” Supervisor Johnny Cribb stated he would vote no if it came to a tie. [Post and Courier / Berkeley Independent, May 15 2026, link to article]

Midwest

INDIANA

Huntingburg water rate increases and Crossvine Solar settlement approved: The Huntingburg Common Council and Utility Board approved a settlement with Crossvine Solar (in partnership with AES) after the developer missed the original construction window. The agreement includes increased setbacks, screening, neighbor protections, incident monitoring, regulation of battery energy storage system locations, and investment in emergency services. The council also rescinded the prior moratorium on commercial solar and directed the Planning Commission to develop new standards. [WITZ AM/FM, May 2026, link to article]

ARIZONA

AC P&Z considers path to moratorium as residents press wind concerns: The Apache County Planning and Zoning Commission is considering options for a moratorium on wind development as residents raised strong concerns about a proposed wind project. The commission is exploring paths forward while weighing public input on potential impacts. [WMICentral, May 2026, link to article]

Daily Clean Energy Clips 5/15/2026


Northeast

NEW YORK

Rome Common Council to investigate battery energy storage systems “The Rome Common Council started the ball rolling on a moratorium on battery energy storage systems in the city to learn more about them before any official proposals come in. The council proposed a 6-month moratorium at its Wednesday night meeting at City Hall, then tabled that proposal so that the legislation could go before the Rome Planning Board for a public hearing. The council has already met with the mayor’s office and the code enforcement department to discuss the issue.” [Daily Sentinel, 05/14/2026, link to article]

Mid-Atlantic

Nothing to see here, folks!

OH/PA

OHIO

Large crowd turns out for Hillclimber Solar hearing in Champaign County “The Ohio Power Siting Board held a public hearing on Wednesday evening on the proposed Hillclimber Solar Project, allowing residents, elected officials, and other interested parties to provide sworn testimony for or against the project as the state determines whether to approve construction. Wednesday’s hearing drew a large crowd of residents, local officials, landowners, and industry representatives, highlighting the continued divide over utility-scale solar development in Champaign County. Hillclimber Solar… is seeking approval to develop a 156.5-megawatt solar and battery storage facility in Urbana Township in Champaign County… Despite the application advancing to the hearing stage, Ohio Power Siting Board staff has recommended the project be denied… The staff recommendation is considered a significant development in the case, as OPSB staff reviews applications for compliance with state standards before making recommendations to the board.” [Peak of Ohio, 05/13/2026, link to article]

PENNSYLVANIA

COMMISSIONERS START PROCESS FOR SALDO AMENDMENT FOR SOLAR, WIND FARMS “Earlier today, the Indiana County Commissioners approved starting the process to add a solar and wind development amendment to the county’s Subdivision and Land Development Ordinance. Josh Krug with the office of planning and development said that the amendments will deal with large-scale commercial solar and wind farms. The amendment will soon be made available online, at libraries throughout the county, municipal offices and at the Planning and Development and Commissioners offices, and there will be a 45-day period for the public to examine it.  There will also be a public hearing on the matter, but that has not been scheduled yet.” [WDAD Classic Hits, 05/13/2026, link to article]

Southeast

GEORGIA

Rockdale County extends ban on data centers “The Rockdale County Board of Commissioners on Tuesday night extended a moratorium on data centers and battery energy storage systems. The move follows an initial 180-day moratorium enacted in March 2026 to evaluate the cumulative impacts of high-water-use facilities. The county is reviewing regulations regarding noise, water, and power usage while updating its Unified Development Ordinance… Rockdale County’s new moratorium is good through Sept. 8 as the county continues its review process to determine future development rules.” [Atlanta News First, 05/13/2026, link to article]

Midwest

ILLINOIS

Knox County Wind Project Halted Over Permit Delays “The Knox County Wind Project in Illinois has been halted after a federal order delayed required permits. The project began in April 2026 and was expected to create about 300 construction jobs and generate new local energy capacity. More information: https://t.co/eJdXwdZHJE.” [NewsBreak, 05/13/2026, link to article]

Morgan board, residents talk data center regulations during meeting “[C]ommissioners began a review of the county's solar, data and battery storage ordinances. The discussion came two weeks after the board approved a six-month moratorium on data centers to give it time to review and establish guidelines for any projects that may seek to come to the area. Board Chair Michael Wankel read a report indicating a ‘commercial land energy conversion system ordinance update application’ and a ‘commercial solar energy facility ordinance update application’ would be ready for the county by Tuesday. Language surrounding battery storage systems for commercial wind and solar has been rewritten, he said, noting a separate ordinance and application for energy storage systems will be written by Oct. 1.” [msn.com, Unknown, link to article]

INDIANA

Clark County planning commission delays solar ordinance decision “The Clark County board of zoning appeals denied a special use exception to build a solar energy facility on private farmland. Now, the planning commission is considering a change to a land ordinance. The proposal comes after the Clark County Board of Zoning Appeals denied a special use exception to build a solar energy facility on private farmland. The commission heard about two and a half hours of public comment on the proposed amendment, but took no action during the meeting. The amendment was tabled until June 10.” [WLKY, 5/13/2026, link to article]

Huntingburg Council approves Crossvine Solar Project settlement “The Huntingburg City Council voted unanimously at the regular meeting held on Tuesday to approve a settlement agreement with Crossvine AES, despite strong opposition from residents concerned about safety risks and property values. The decision came after the city’s special counsel, Joshua Claybourn, warned that Huntingburg’s moratorium on solar development sits ‘on thin ice’ legally and that litigation against the multi-billion dollar corporation would likely fail while costing the city hundreds of thousands of dollars. The settlement includes several concessions from AES, including a six-foot berm or cmomparable vegetation screening along neighboring properties, increased setbacks of 100 feet from property lines and 250 feet from residences, and incident monitoring equipment for the battery energy storage systems.” [Dubois County Free Press, Inc., 05/13/2026, link to article]

MICHIGAN

Comstock Township Planning Commission Discusses Renewables [At their meeting 5/14/26 the Comstock Township Planning Commission heard a presentation regarding renewable energy from the University of Michigan and discussed a potential ordinance rework. In addition, one public commenter pressured public officials to vote no on a forthcoming BESS Project from Copenhagen Infrastructure Partners.] [YouTube, 05/14/2026, link to article]

Plains

Snoqualmie City Council Appoints New Member, Advances BESS Moratorium, Names Police Chief Finalist “At the meeting, Mayor Mayhew introduced a moratorium ordinance on battery energy storage facilities within Snoqualmie city limits. He noted that a Snoqualmie moratorium would have no effect on the proposed Jupiter Power project, which is located in unincorporated King County, but said it was appropriate for the city to move forward with its own legislative process regarding whether such facilities should be permitted within city limits. He also said the continued focus on whether Snoqualmie should adopt a moratorium had become a distraction from the city’s work in the King County process…The council went into a 20-minute executive session with legal counsel to discuss the ordinance before voting to waive the standard 72-hour notification requirement, allowing the ordinance to be read in for the first time… The council voted 5-1, with Councilmember Washington the lone dissenting vote, preferring to act on the ordinance that night rather than refer it to committee.” [Living Snoqualmie, 05/13/2026, link to article]
