staff@greenlightamerica.org

Subject

Daily Clean Energy Clips 5/14/2026



Northeast

NEW YORK

Attorney General James and Governor Hochul intervene to protect Sunrise Wind project: New York Attorney General Letitia James and Governor Kathy Hochul moved to intervene in a federal lawsuit seeking to vacate approvals for the 924-megawatt Sunrise Wind offshore wind project, which is expected to power 600,000 New York homes. The project is set to begin delivering power this year and become fully operational next year, providing more than $875 million in economic benefits and supporting over 3,500 jobs. Attorney General James stated, “Contrary to the president’s claims, wind energy is essential for our economy, our environment, and our electric grid… This administration has already tried to shut down Sunrise Wind once, and we will not leave the future of this critical project in its hands.” Governor Hochul added, “Offshore wind projects like Sunrise Wind will not only provide needed clean energy that will help our environment, but also create good-paying union jobs, benefit our local economies, and help secure our energy independence.” [EIN Presswire, May 2026, link to article]

Mid-Atlantic

OHIO

Confusing ballot wording may have tipped Ohio vote on renewables ban: Voters in Richland County, Ohio, upheld a ban on utility-scale solar and wind in 11 townships (53%–47%) on May 5, 2026, but exit polling suggests the result would have flipped to 54% favoring repeal if ballot wording had been clearer. The confusing language made a “yes” vote mean keeping the ban and a “no” vote mean repealing it. Campaign leader Morgan Carroll said, “The question of who gets to decide what happens on private land in Richland County did not end tonight.” [Canary Media, May 11 2026, link to article]

Southeast

SOUTH CAROLINA

Berkeley County panel advances plan to lift Cross moratorium for 1,500-acre solar farm: The Berkeley County Land Use Committee advanced a plan to lift a large-scale development moratorium covering nearly 7,500 acres in the Cross community to allow rezoning for RWE Clean Energy’s 198-megawatt Sandy Run Solar project on 1,500 acres. The full council will consider the proposal in two weeks. Resident Robert Barrett said, “Cross is not an industrial community. It’s a retirement and recreational community… We don’t want that big industry in there.” Developer Peter Stalter noted the site is at least a mile from the nearest public right-of-way. [ABC News 4, May 12 2026, link to article]

GEORGIA

Rockdale County extends data center and battery energy storage moratorium through Sept. 8: Rockdale County extended its moratorium on new battery energy storage system (BESS) applications through September 8, 2026, to allow time to review zoning, infrastructure, and community impacts as part of the Unified Development Ordinance update. [On Common Ground News, May 13 2026, link to article]

NORTH CAROLINA

Shunning the sunshine? NC order for Duke Energy to hit brakes on solar raises policy, legal questions: The North Carolina Utilities Commission (via Chairman Bill Brawley) ordered Duke Energy to pause its 2026 solar procurement (~770 MW) until the 2026 Carbon Plan is approved by December 31. Clean energy groups called the unilateral order “arbitrary and capricious” and warned it threatens reliability and affordability amid rising demand. [Carolina Public Press, May 12 2026, link to article]

Midwest

ILLINOIS

Workers left in limbo after permit delays stalls wind energy project: A major wind energy project in Knox County, Illinois, has come to a halt due to federal permitting delays involving the FAA and Department of Defense. Construction crews have begun dismantling a mobile concrete batch plant and lay-down yard. Labor leaders report that about 25 workers from the laborers union and another dozen from other trades were laid off immediately. Dane Simpson, Renewable Energy Director for Great Plains LECET, stated, “They were two to three weeks into the project, and they really felt like the rug got pulled out from under them.” The project was expected to create around 300 construction jobs and generate roughly $48 million in tax revenue for Knox County over its 35-year life cycle. NextEra Energy Resources has not commented on when construction might resume. [KWQC, May 12 2026, link to article]

MICHIGAN

No vote yet on large-scale Southern Michigan solar project after months of opposition: The Fayette Township Planning Commission postponed a decision on Ranger Power’s Heartwood Solar II project (~1,000 acres east of Jonesville). Resident Steve Oleszkowicz stated, “One out of every four acres of productive, agricultural land in this township gone, converted, industrialized.” Ranger Power’s Brady Friss noted, “Load is growing in Michigan and solar is currently the most affordable way to meet that demand.” [MLive, May 8 2026, link to article]

Foundation breaks with solar developer amid fervent opposition in Southern Michigan: The Hillsdale Community Foundation ended its charitable partnership with Ranger Power over the controversial Heartwood Solar II project in Fayette Township after months of heated public opposition. Foundation President Sharon Bisher said, “When it became polarizing and negative, it was time for us to step back… We don’t take stands on issues.” [MLive, May 12 2026, link to article]

Huron County commissioners freeze solar, battery permits while zoning standards are reviewed: Huron County commissioners approved a three-month moratorium on new special use permits for utility-scale solar and battery energy storage systems (including the pending 150 MW Pinesap Solar project in Rubicon Township) to allow time for updated zoning standards. Commissioner Kurt Geiger said, “I would just like to say this should have been addressed a long time ago.” [Michigan’s Thumb, May 13 2026, link to article]

North-Central

WISCONSIN

Stop 700 acre industrial scale solar farm in Town of Sumner: Residents of the Town of Sumner, Wisconsin (Trempealeau County), launched a Change.org petition opposing a proposed 700-acre industrial-scale solar and battery storage project, citing conflicts with township and county land-use plans focused on farmland preservation and rural character. The petition has gathered over 430 signatures. [Change.org, May 12 2026, link to article]

South-Central

ARKANSAS

EDP Renewables and Meta ink PPA for 250-MW solar project: EDP Renewables and Meta signed a long-term power purchase agreement for the 250 MW Cypress Knee Solar project in Chicot County, Arkansas. The project is expected to begin commercial operations in 2027 and be completed in 2028, delivering more than $25 million in additional funding to the county over 30 years. [Utility Dive, May 7 2026, link to article]

Daily Clean Energy Clips 5/13/2026

Northeast

NEW YORK

Budget and batteries: Tupper town and school boards poised for big meetings Monday night “The Tupper Lake Town Board is slated to discuss a moratorium on lithium ion battery energy storage systems… Supervisor Rickey Dattola said the town doesn’t yet have a formal document and isn’t expected to vote on the matter on Monday. Rather, it’s still in the discussion stage and is a chance for the town board to hear any feelings from the public on the matter, as well as how each of its five members feel before possibly drafting a resolution for vote at a future meeting… Dattola added that the town board had previously been approached by a company who floated a battery storage facility at its old landfill, but that never advanced beyond informal discussions and is not an active pursuit.” [Gazette News Group, Inc., 05/11/2026, link to article]

Clifton Park weighs short ban on solar-project applications “The Clifton Park Town Board is considering a six-month moratorium on solar-energy-system applications as developers continue to seek to cover more acreage with the technology. The moratorium would specifically prohibit Tier 2 and Tier 3 solar energy system applications for 180 days. The town classifies Tier 2 and Tier 3 systems as medium- to large-scale ground-mounted installations, including solar facilities that involve substantial land area, electrical interconnection infrastructure and long-term land conversion.” [Gazette News Group, Inc., 05/10/2026, link to article]

Amsterdam proposes extending moratorium on solar, battery systems “Amsterdam is considering extending its moratorium on industrial solar and battery-energy-storage systems through the end of the year to give officials more time to update related zoning laws. ’I've been observing this issue, how it's been handled in other communities, and I see controversy. I see some questions about it and some uncertainties,’ Mayor Michael Cinquanti said. [The hearing will be held on May 19, 2026 at 5:45 PM at the Amsterdam Common Council Chambers, Church Street, Amsterdam, NY].” [Gazette News Group, Inc., 05/10/2026, link to article]

Chazy Planning Board to Review Solar Project May 18 “The Town of Chazy Planning Board will meet on Monday, May 18, 2026, at 6:00 PM at Chazy Town Hall. The agenda includes a preliminary hearing on a proposed solar project and a discussion about a decommissioning plan for a previously approved application. No new applications are scheduled for review. The meeting is open to the public, and residents are welcome to attend and stay informed about local development plans.” [NewsBreak, 05/08/2026, link to article]

Mid-Atlantic

VIRGINIA

[Louisa County] ]Supes allocate $250k to fight VL transmission line“The Louisa County Board of Supervisors on Monday night stepped up its efforts to oppose a controversial ultra-high voltage power line that could slice through the center of the county, appropriating $250,000 to cover potential legal expenses related to the project… Valley Link Transmission Company, a partnership between Dominion Energy, First Energy Transmission and Transource, has proposed the 115-mile, 765-kilovolt (kV) transmission line that, if approved by state regulators, would cross as many as nine mostly rural counties as it travels from the Joshua Falls substation near Lynchburg to the proposed Yeat substation in southeastern Culpeper…The company has said it plans to submit a single preferred route to the State Corporation Commission (SCC) for approval by September. It hopes to energize the $1 billion project by 2029.” [Engage Louisa (Substack), 06/04/2025, link to article]

OH/PA

OHIO

Champaign County Public Hearing “Join us at the Hillclimber Solar public hearing and make your voice heard on the record. The Ohio Power Siting Board will be taking public testimony, and local voices matter enormously in these decisions. This is your chance to stand up for property rights, Ohio-made energy, and clean energy jobs in Champaign County. We'll have talking points ready for you, and first-time speakers are always welcome.” [Ohio Citizen Action (via Mobilize), 05/13/2026, link to article]

Southeast

SOUTH CAROLINA

Berkeley County residents clash with county over proposed 1,500-acre solar farm “Boos and pleas for county leaders to protect a rural community filled a public hearing in Berkeley County as residents in Cross pushed back against a proposed 1,500-acre solar farm and the decision to advance a request to lift a large-scale development moratorium. The Berkeley County Land Use Committee passed a motion to lift a large-scale development moratorium covering nearly 7,500 acres in the Cross community. The action moves forward a proposal tied to the Sandy Run Solar Project, which would require rezoning 1,500 acres to industrial planned development for a 198-megawatt solar farm that could power more than 37,000 homes… The matter will now go before the full Berkeley County Council at its next meeting in two weeks. If approved, RWE would still need county approval to rezone the 1,500 acres for the solar farm.” [WCIV, 05/11/2026, link to article]

ALABAMA

Baldwin County seeks legal clarity on solar moratorium amid Stockton project concerns

“The Baldwin County Commission is seeking guidance from the Alabama attorney general on whether it can impose a temporary moratorium on solar developments in unincorporated areas, as concerns grow over a proposed large-scale project near Stockton. Commissioners approved a resolution authorizing the county attorney to request a formal opinion on the legality of temporary moratoriums on certain land use and construction approvals in areas without zoning regulations. The request comes amid increasing public concern over a proposed utility-scale solar farm on roughly 4,500 acres near the unincorporated community of Stockton. The project, proposed by Silicon Ranch Corp., has prompted objections from residents and environmental groups who warn of potential impacts on flood-prone land and natural resources connected to the Tensaw River and its delta… No timeline has been announced for when the attorney general’s office may respond.” [WEAR2, 05/05/2026, link to article]

Midwest

MICHIGAN

Quincy Township Adopts Strict Battery Storage Ordinance “Quincy Township officials recently [unanimously] adopted a restrictive new ordinance for battery energy storage systems (BESS) to maintain local authority over utility-scale energy projects… The measure…restricts large-scale battery facilities exclusively to industrial zones as a special land use. This strategic move is designed to comply with Michigan’s Public Act 233, a 2023 law that allows the state to override local zoning for renewable energy projects unless a community has a ‘compatible’ local ordinance already in place. The new rules come in response to significant community opposition regarding the proposed 180-megawatt Santina Energy project on Boone Road, which residents feared could lead to fire hazards and groundwater contamination.” [The Voice of Branch County, 05/08/2026, link to article]

Oshtemo hosts discussions after battery storage prompts controversy “Oshtemo Township is launching Community Conversations to give residents more time to chat with elected officials… The first Community Conversation last week focused on battery energy storage systems. The township passed a moratorium to block the development of battery storage facilities after NewEdge Power approached the township about building a 260-megawatt battery storage facility in 2024. Multiple community members have spoken out against the proposed battery storage facility. The battery storage facility was among reasons why some residents supported a campaign to recall several board members. New Edge hasn’t made any formal development proposals.” [Kalamazoo Local News, 5/11/26, link to article]

INDIANA

Rules approved for data centers and battery storage – The Commercial Review “Jay County Commissioners approved two new ordinances regulating battery storage facilities and data centers and amended existing ordinances regarding commercial solar and wind farms Monday. They also repealed a one-year moratorium on new wind farm developments and retroactively ended one-year moratoriums on new solar farm and battery storage facility developments. Jay/Portland Building and Planning has been working with the county plan commission on drafting zoning regulations for battery storage and data center facilities, as well as updating zoning restrictions for solar and wind farms, for about a year… Moratoriums on new solar farm and battery storage developments in Jay County ended April 28. The moratorium on new wind farm developments was set to remain in effect through July 21… With the ordinance approved Monday, [BESS] facilities in Jay County must have 500-foot setbacks from property lines. Equipment also needs to be enclosed within a 7-foot security fence set back 50 feet from the property line. Permit fees are $2,500 per megawatt.” [Solar Now, 05/12/2026, link to article]

North-Central

MINNESOTA

County enacts one-year moratorium on solar, wind projects “Morrison County commissioners approved a one-year moratorium on certain solar and wind energy projects May 5, following a recommendation from the Morrison County Planning Commission. The interim ordinance applies to community solar energy systems, solar farms and wind energy conversion systems under county jurisdiction. It gives the county time to study how larger renewable energy projects fit into its comprehensive plan, zoning ordinance and land-use policies.” [Morrison County Record, 05/09/2026, link to article]

Central-Central

South-Central

Plains

Nothing to see here, folks!

Pacific Northwest

Watching Snoqualmie, North Bend Pursues Moratorium on Battery Storage “As the proposed Cascadia Ridge battery energy storage facility near Snoqualmie Ridge continues through King County’s permitting process, the City of North Bend is taking steps to ensure it doesn’t face a similar situation on its own doorstep. At an April 28 work study, North Bend city staff and council members discussed a proposed 12-month moratorium on commercial battery storage facilities, with a formal vote and public hearing scheduled for May 19. Community & Economic Development Director James Henderson presented the proposal, describing it as a response to growing interest in large-scale battery energy storage systems in the region… The May 19 North Bend City Council meeting is open to the public. A public hearing on the moratorium ordinance is expected the same night.” [Living Snoqualmie, 05/07/2026, link to article]

Northern Rockies

Nothing to see here, folks!

CO/NM/NV

Nothing to see here, folks!

AZ/UT

Desert Rainbow Mega Solar Farm Targets 5,000 Acres In Goodyear “A global energy company is looking to cover a big slice of southern Goodyear with panels and batteries, filing plans for a roughly 5,011-acre solar and storage complex that would put out about 550 megawatts of solar power and store another 550 megawatts. The project, called Desert Rainbow, is slated for Rainbow Valley and is pitched as producing enough carbon-free electricity to serve the equivalent of roughly 165,000 homes. At that scale, it would rank among the largest utility-scale solar footprints proposed in Arizona in recent years… City records show a special-use permit application, Case No. P25-00259, submitted by Desert Rainbow LLC for roughly 5,011 acres south of Queen Creek Road and east of Waterman Wash in Rainbow Valley… AES has penciled in development and permitting through 2029, with construction expected to run from 2029 to 2032 and operations following that window.” [Hoodline, 05/11/2026, link to article]

California

Planning Commission Approves Acreage Cap for Solar Panels – Noozhawk “The Santa Barbara County Planning Commission decided to cap the number of acres available for solar panel use to 16,000 acres and streamline the building process for new installations. The commission approved the countywide cap in a 3-1 vote on Wednesday during a discussion about where solar panels could be built, and which projects would be exempted from permitting… The 16,000-acre cap chosen by the commission will limit solar panels on agricultural land to 2% of the county’s available acreage.” [Solar Now, 05/11/2026, link to article]

staff@greenlightamerica.org

Subject

Daily Clean Energy Clips 5/12/2026

Northeast

MASSACHUSETTS

Offshore Wind Tracker: What’s new with wind projects off Massachusetts and beyond? “At The Light, we’re closely tracking these large-scale energy projects that stand to impact New Bedford, its workforce, its economy, and its storied fishing industry. Here’s a rundown of New England projects (plus two south of New York) and where they all stand. Many face legal challenges — and are being scrutinized by Interior Secretary Doug Burgum, who has been tasked with reviewing whether to amend or even terminate approvals to build these projects. ” [New Bedford Light, 5/4/2026, link to article]

Mid-Atlantic

As PJM Reopens Interconnection Queue, Experts Warn Damage to Maryland’s Clean Energy Plans Is Already Done “For the first time in four years, regional grid operator PJM Interconnection is adding new generation projects to its interconnection queue. PJM announced it received 811 new generation projects with 220 gigawatts total capacity. With gas and nuclear accounting for roughly 55 percent of total capacity, this reflects a dramatic shift from the renewable-dominated queue PJM spent four years clearing.” [Inside Climate News, 5/5/2026, link to article]

VIRGINIA

Move over, sheep. Cattle are grazing solar sites, too, and that’s good news for Virginia. “Farmers are finding that cattle also thrive among solar panels — and they will get their chance to prove it in Virginia. The market for beef is vastly bigger than the market for lamb, so persuading farmers they should diversify into sheep as well as solar is a tall order. But if cattle prove as compatible with solar as sheep are, there will be vastly more opportunities for both farmers and the solar industry. Given the dire economic situation facing small farms in Virginia today, “cattle-voltaics” could offer a lifeline for rural communities.” [Daily News Record, 5/11/2026, link to article]

OH/PA

OHIO

Confusing ballot wording may have tipped Ohio vote on renewables ban “Exit polling suggests Richland County would have elected to repeal its wind and solar ban, if not for residents who mistakenly voted in favor of the restriction. SB 52 states that for any referendum against a county’s ban, its commissioners’ resolution needs a majority vote in favor to go into effect So, a ​“yes” vote means someone opposes the referendum effort to overturn the ban, while a ​“no” vote means the person backs the campaign to get rid of the ban. “It’s confusing,” County Commissioner Cliff Mears told Canary Media.”  [Canary Media, 5/11/2026, link to article]

Southeast

ARKANSAS

Meta, EDP Renewables Sign 250 MW Arkansas Solar Deal to Expand U.S. Clean Power Supply “Arkansas will host a new 250 MW solar project as Meta moves to secure more clean electricity for its U.S. operations. Meta and EDP Renewables North America have signed a long-term power purchase agreement for Cypress Knee Solar, a project planned for Chicot County. The deal adds new renewable generation to the Arkansas grid and extends a multi-year energy partnership between the two companies. Cypress Knee Solar is expected to be completed by 2028. ” [ESGNews, 5/11/2026, link to article]

Midwest

MICHIGAN

Appeals court issues split ruling in Michigan solar permitting suit “The three-judge Michigan Court of Appeals panel ruled that state regulators followed proper legal processes when they set rules to carry out a controversial 2023 law that allows the Michigan Public Service Commission to approve large wind, solar and battery projects over local objections. But judges ruled the commission interpreted certain aspects of that law in ways that improperly limited local power.It wasn’t immediately clear how the split ruling could affect the multiple renewable energy development proposals currently awaiting state approval. However, state officials seemed to see it as a victory.” [Washington Post, 5/8/2026, link to article]

North-Central

nothing to see here, folks

Central-Central

nothing to see here, folks

South-Central

TEXAS

Gas power leapfrogs wind for first time in 10 years in Texas’ grid connection queue “For the last six months, the volume of gas generation in the Texas grid’s interconnection queue — the yearslong waiting list for electric generators wanting to connect to the grid — has surpassed wind.It’s the first time since January 2016 that gas has overtaken wind in the queue, a shift that reflects the policy and economic headwinds facing the wind industry and data centers favoring gas power as they seek to cash in on the artificial intelligence boom. ” [Texas Tribune, 5/7/2026, link to article]

Plains

nothing to see here, folks

Pacific Northwest

nothing to see here, folks

Northern Rockies

nothing to see here, folks

CO/NM/NV

NEVADA

Amazon Invests in 700 MW of New Carbon-Free Energy Projects to Power Future Data Centers in Nevada “Amazon announced that it is investing in 700 MW of new carbon-free energy projects in Nevada to power its future data center operations in the area. The company said that it has signed an agreement with Nevada-based NV Energy, a subsidiary of Berkshire Hathaway Energy, to add 100 MW of geothermal power from renewable energy company Zanskar, and 600 MW of solar plus 600 MW of battery storage from renewable energy developer Primergy to the Nevada power grid.” [ESGToday, 5/11/2026, link to article]

AZ/UT

Massive Utah data center complex ‘can’ be powered by renewables, O’Leary says — after state insisted ‘100%’ natural gas “The first time a hyperscale data center project in Utah was discussed in public, a state official was explicit: It would be powered “100% off the Ruby Pipeline,” the natural gas line that crosses the area. The first phase of the project is expected to consume 3 gigawatts of electricity. By comparison, the entire state of Utah currently uses about 4 gigawatts. At full build, the data complex is expected to use 9 gigawatts, project boosters have said. After two weeks of outcry about the fossil fuel plan, project developer and “Shark Tank” star Kevin O’Leary seemed to announce a potential pivot — or the possibility of one. “We can also put a percentage of the power generation through solar, wind and batteries, because the battery technology is 10x more efficient than it was just five years ago,” he shared on X this week. O’Leary didn’t commit to actually using any of the renewables.  ” [Salt Lake Tribune, 5/7/2026, link to article]

California

nothing to see here, folks

5/11/26

To:

staff@greenlightamerica.org

Subject

Daily Clean Energy Clips 5/11/2026



Northeast

CONNECTICUT

CT Senate passes major solar energy legislation: The Connecticut Senate passed House Bill 5340 on the last day of the 2026 legislative session. The bill extends solar incentive programs for residential, commercial, and community solar through 2035, expedites permitting for plug-in solar panels, creates a statewide agrivoltaics program, and establishes a pilot for low- or no-cost solar in 100 environmental justice homes. It also imposes a one-year moratorium on large-scale solar in East Windsor and Enfield. Democratic Rep. Jonathan Steinberg stated, “This bill modernizes Connecticut’s renewable energy programs by introducing measures meant to make them more reliable, consistent, and flexible to economic fluctuations.” The bill now heads to Gov. Ned Lamont. [CT Public, May 7 2026, link to article]

Mid-Atlantic

nothing to see here, folks

Southeast

NORTH CAROLINA

Warren County commissioners uphold current solar farm ordinance: Warren County commissioners unanimously voted to reject proposed text amendments to the solar farm ordinance that would have allowed larger projects beyond the current 150-acre cap. The decision upholds the 2022 ordinance and blocks amendments sought by TED Renewables for the Horizon Solar project (160 MW combined on ~900 acres across two sites). Planning Director Mark Bloomer warned the changes would “open the door to solar farms of any size.” The meeting drew more than 40 speakers and lasted over four hours. [Lake Gaston Gazette-Observer, May 7 2026, link to article]

NORTH CAROLINA

Clean energy groups push back on North Carolina regulators' solar energy pause: The Southern Environmental Law Center filed a motion asking the North Carolina Utilities Commission to reconsider its order pausing Duke Energy’s 2026 solar procurement (roughly 770 MW). The unusual order was issued solely by Commission Chair William Brawley without a public hearing. SELC called the pause “arbitrary and capricious” and warned that delaying the “missing megawatts” threatens reliability and affordability. [WFAE, May 6 2026, link to article]

Midwest

MICHIGAN

No vote yet on large-scale Southern Michigan solar project after months of opposition: The Fayette Township Planning Commission postponed any decision on a special use permit for Ranger Power’s Heartwood Solar II project (approximately 1,000 acres of panels on 1,400 acres east of Jonesville). The hearing on May 7 drew strong resident opposition. Resident Steve Oleszkowicz stated, “One out of every four acres of productive, agricultural land in this township gone, converted, industrialized. That is not incremental change. That is not balanced development. That is a transformation of this community.” Ranger Power’s Brady Friss noted, “Load is growing in Michigan and solar is currently the most affordable way to meet that demand.” No new meeting date was set. [MLive, May 8 2026, link to article]

Appeals court issues split ruling in Michigan solar permitting suit: A Michigan Court of Appeals panel upheld most of the Michigan Public Service Commission’s rules implementing Public Act 233 but sided with local governments on two key points: the 30-day local approval clock starts after a meeting with developers (not when offered), and any local unit touching project land qualifies as an “affected local unit” eligible for developer payments. The ruling largely affirms the state’s ability to approve large solar, wind, and battery projects over local objections when towns lack compatible ordinances. PSC spokesperson Matt Helms said the decision “largely affirms the Commission’s approach.” [Bridge Michigan, May 7 2026, link to article]

Farmers push back on proposed solar farm that could convert 1,500 acres of northern Michigan farmland: Farmers in Wexford and Grand Traverse counties are opposing Ranger Power’s (via subsidiary Shipyard Solar) early-stage proposal for a large solar array on nearly 1,500 acres of prime agricultural land. The project has not yet filed formal applications. Farmer James Cade stated, “You’re taking valuable farm ground, that is some of the best ground in the state of Michigan to raise vegetables that are feeding people.” Ranger Power’s Ryan Wardin said developments are built on “voluntary partnerships with local landowners who see solar as a benefit to their farming operations.” A public meeting is scheduled for May 18. [9&10 News, May 7 2026, link to article]

North-Central

nothing to see here, folks

South-Central

nothing to see here, folks

Plains

nothing to see here, folks

Pacific Northwest

WASHINGTON

More turbulence for Washington State’s giant wind farm: Rep. Dan Newhouse continues to push the FAA to revoke “no hazard” determinations for Scout Clean Energy’s Horse Heaven wind farm in Benton County. Scout has filed new airspace applications while the project remains under litigation at the Washington Supreme Court (arguments in June). The developer has already reduced turbine numbers and increased heights. No public record exists on whether prior FAA approvals were revoked. [NewsBreak / Heatmap News, May 2026, link to article]

ID/MT/WY

nothing to see here, folks

CO/NM/NV

NEW MEXICO

State Land Office awards lease to proposed wind farm near Corona: The New Mexico State Land Office awarded a lease to Southwestern Power Group for a 212 MW portion of the 1,100 MW Cedarvale Wind Project in Torrance County (8 miles northwest of Corona). The full project includes 243 turbines on 10,160 acres of state trust land and 51,149 acres of private land. It is projected to generate $98.9 million in school revenue over the lease lifetime. Construction is expected to begin in 2027 with operations in 2029. Land Commissioner Stephanie Garcia Richard stated, “Projects like this one are job creators in our rural communities and create long-lasting revenue streams for our public schools and other institutions.” [Yahoo News, May 8 2026, link to article]

AZ/UT

ARIZONA

Navajo County citizens petition for immediate moratorium on utility-scale renewables: Citizens of Navajo County, Arizona, launched a Change.org petition calling for an immediate moratorium on all utility-scale wind, solar, and battery projects. The petition targets West Camp Wind Farm II (500 MW, 144+ turbines) and criticizes the dissolved REAL AZ entity’s continued influence. It demands 2-mile setbacks, signed power purchase agreements, independent water and soil studies, and full decommissioning bonding. [Change.org, May 7 2026, link to article]

California

Daily Clean Energy Clips 5/8/2026

Northeast

NEW YORK

No decision yet on proposed battery storage sites in Huntington Town: The Huntington Town Board has not yet decided on two proposed battery energy storage facilities (one by Key Capture Energy) in East Northport and near the Greenlawn substation. A public hearing was held Tuesday with significant resident concerns over fire safety and emergency response for volunteer fire departments. Town Councilman Dave Bennardo stated, “The amount of false alarms that you have with a battery storage facility, the protocols of waiting outside the gates until you can get management to open the doors, that's impossible right now for a volunteer firefighting crew.” The board plans to meet with fire officials before voting and is considering a six-month moratorium to study risks. [News 12, May 2026, link to article]

Mid-Atlantic

OHIO

Resident-led campaign fails to reverse Ohio county’s ban on renewables: Voters in Richland County, Ohio, rejected a referendum (53%–47%) to overturn a ban on utility-scale solar and wind in 11 townships. The ban, passed in 2025, remains in place. Campaign leader Morgan Carroll said, “We made a commitment to stand up to our elected officials… That’s not nothing. That is everything.” [Canary Media, May 6 2026, link to article]

VIRGINIA

Dominion upbeat on offshore wind as cost estimate eases, sales rise: Dominion Energy reported that its Coastal Virginia Offshore Wind (CVOW) project (2.6 GW) is now 75% complete, with nine of 176 turbines installed and power generation underway since March 2026. The company lowered its capital cost estimate to $11.4 billion while maintaining a levelized cost of energy at $84/MWh. CEO Robert Blue said, “With no change to either LCOE or customer bill impacts, CVOW remains one of the most affordable sources of energy for our customers.” The project is expected to generate approximately $5 billion in fuel savings over its first 10 years. [Utility Dive, May 4 2026, link to article]

Southeast

nothing to see here, folks

Midwest

MICHIGAN

Oshtemo launches ‘Community Conversations’ after battery storage prompts controversy: Oshtemo Township (Kalamazoo County) launched monthly “Community Conversations” (first one Thursday, May 7) to give residents more informal input opportunities after controversy over a proposed 260 MW battery storage facility by NewEdge Power in 2024. The project was blocked by a township moratorium. Supervisor Cheri Bell said the sessions are “meant to be welcoming, unhurried and focused on listening… We want people to feel comfortable asking questions, sharing ideas and helping shape Oshtemo’s future.” [MLive, May 6 2026, link to article]

North-Central

IOWA

Taylor County Supervisors to discuss wind project and energy moratorium May 7: The Taylor County Board of Supervisors in Taylor County, Iowa, will meet on Thursday, May 7, 2026, at 9:00 a.m. to discuss the Black Maple Wind project and a proposed energy moratorium amendment covering data centers, solar, wind, and battery storage. [NewsBreak / Taylor County Daily Brief, May 2026, link to article]

South-Central

KANSAS

Douglas County residents ask commission to consider moratorium on battery systems, data centers: Douglas County commissioners held a work session on May 6, 2026, where residents requested a moratorium or ban on battery energy storage systems (BESS) and data centers, despite no local projects being proposed. Commissioners directed staff to draft regulations (starting with BESS tied to solar/utility uses) as a legal pause on new applications until regulations are adopted. [Lawrence KS Times, May 6 2026, link to article]

AZ/UT

UTAH

Emery County Commission begins drafting ordinance to prohibit future utility-scale solar development: The Emery County Commission voted 2-1 on May 6, 2026, to begin drafting an ordinance prohibiting new utility-scale solar projects in the county. The move follows strong public opposition to a proposed solar project at Poison Springs Bench/Drunkard’s Wash. Commissioner Keven Jensen stated, “I think the strongest tool that we have to stop this project is the power of the ordinance.” Commissioner Jordan Leonard added, “I think at this point no more utility-scale solar… the public has spoken on that.” [Castle Country Radio, May 6 2026, link to article]

California

California subpoenas Golden State Wind for records: The California Energy Commission issued a subpoena to Golden State Wind seeking documents about its agreement with the Trump administration to terminate its offshore wind lease off California in exchange for a payout. CEC Chair David Hochschild stated, “The Trump Administration is recklessly spending billions of taxpayer dollars on backroom deals that would turn back the clock on innovation.” [reNEWS, May 5 2026, link to article]

Daily Clean Energy Clips 5/7/2026

Northeast

NEW YORK

Cortlandville to hold public hearing on solar moratorium extension: Cortlandville residents can share their thoughts on the potential extension of a solar moratorium [May 6] at 5:30 p.m. at Cortlandville Town Hall. A temporary ban has been in place since October that prohibits the approval of new solar energy applications. [607 News Now, May 6 2026, link to article]

NEW YORK

Goshen adopts six-month solar energy moratorium: The Goshen Town Board passed a six-month moratorium on new solar farms at its April 23 meeting (4-0 vote). The moratorium does not affect projects already undergoing review. Councilman George Lyons stated, “We are not against solar farms. They are getting out of control. We want to take a closer look at it.” Councilman Richard Florio added, “We’re not looking to ban solar. We just want to make sure it’s done right.” Councilman Philip Canterino noted that solar farms were intended to be capped at five megawatts but projects were combining multiple parcels for higher output. [Chronicle Newspaper, May 5 2026, link to article]

Mid-Atlantic

nothing to see here, folks

Southeast

ALABAMA

Baldwin County seeks legal clarity on solar moratorium amid Stockton project concerns: The Baldwin County Commission approved a resolution asking the Alabama attorney general for a formal opinion on whether it can impose a temporary moratorium on utility-scale solar developments in un-zoned, unincorporated areas (especially flood-prone zones). The request is driven by concerns over a proposed utility-scale solar farm on roughly 4,500 acres near the unincorporated community of Stockton by Silicon Ranch Corp. The nonprofit Friends of the Tensaw River has called for a one-year pause on permits to study environmental risks tied to the Tensaw River and its delta. [NBC 15, May 5 2026, link to article]

Midwest

INDIANA

Marshall County approves ordinances restricting solar farms and prohibiting carbon capture: Marshall County commissioners gave final approval (2-0 vote) to the Farm Scale Solar Energy System ordinance, which strictly prohibits large-scale solar panel farms spanning multiple properties and acres across any zoning district. It allows private-use solar up to five acres per parcel. The board also approved a cleanup ordinance permanently prohibiting carbon capture projects. [Now Solar / giant.fm, May 6 2026, link to article]

INDIANA

West Lafayette City Council approves solar power restrictions: The West Lafayette City Council approved (5-1 vote) an ordinance limiting new commercial solar developments to 400 acres per project, with at least one mile between projects and 500 feet from neighboring property lines. Total commercial solar in the county is capped at 6,000 acres. District 5 Rep. Kathy Parker stated, “It’s better than nothing, right? … If nobody comes for a whole year, then maybe we reexamine … It’s a starting place.” [Now Solar / Journal & Courier, May 6 2026, link to article]

MICHIGAN

Public hearing process for Liberty Farms Solar continues in Richland Township: Consumers Energy’s proposed Liberty Farms Solar project (1,200 acres, 220 MW, enough to power over 50,000 homes) is moving through the public hearing process in Richland Township. A third meeting is scheduled for May 27 at Gull Lake Middle School at 6:30 p.m. Township Supervisor Bear Priest stated, “Those seven members who live here, work here in our community… They need to hear from our residents.” Resident Richard Jamieson (living 170 yards from the site) said, “There’s nothing good about it at all… It’s gonna kill the value of property around here. This is rich land.” [WWMT, May 5 2026, link to article]

North-Central

Plains

NORTH DAKOTA

Public hearing set for Longspur Wind Project in Morton County: The North Dakota Public Service Commission has scheduled a public hearing for 10 a.m. on May 18 at the City Auditorium in Glen Ullin on Minnesota Power’s proposed Longspur Wind Project (up to 45 turbines, 202.5 MW on ~26,100 acres in Morton County plus a 2.5-mile transmission line to Mercer County). Estimated cost: $791 million. ALLETE CEO Bethany Owen stated, “Longspur is part of the approximately $5 billion in investments we will be making over the next five years to serve our region.” [KFGQ / Dakota News Network, May 6 2026, link to article]

California

CALIFORNIA

Developer of Watsonville battery storage project pivots to state permitting route: New Leaf Energy is withdrawing its county application for the Seahawk Energy Storage project (160-unit BESS on 16 acres at 90 Minto Road in unincorporated Watsonville) and will instead use the California Energy Commission’s “Opt-in” process under AB 205 to meet CAISO deadlines. Project lead Max Christian stated that the county’s draft ordinance amendments “would likely prevent the necessary upstream procurement commitments.” The company still supports the core draft ordinance, calling it “one of the — if not the — strongest battery storage ordinances in the country.” [Mercury News, May 5 2026, link to article]

Northeast

NEW YORK

Clay Town Board to hold public hearing on new rules for battery storage projects: The Town of Clay held a public hearing (May 4, 2026) at 7:47 p.m. at Town Hall on proposed Local Law No. 4 of 2026 to establish guidelines for battery energy storage systems (BESS). The hearing follows resident concerns over potential projects on Long Branch Road, Wetzel Road and Goguen Drive. Proposed rules include tighter site-location limits, updated fire-safety and emergency-response requirements, noise and screening standards, clearer decommissioning rules, and alignment with updated state fire codes. Deputy Supervisor Joe Bick stated, “The hearing will stay open until we get the law right.” No vote will be taken; the hearing will remain open until the law is finalized. [Syracuse.com, May 4 2026, link to article]

MASSACHUSETTS

GE Vernova challenges Vineyard Wind’s claims of harm if it leaves project: GE Vernova filed an emergency motion to reconsider a preliminary injunction barring it from exiting its contract with Vineyard Wind 1, arguing that the project’s commercial operation date (achieved April 24, 2026) and activation of power purchase agreements undermine claims of irreparable harm. Vineyard Wind (806 MW, 62 turbines) is currently generating at less than half capacity. Attorney Michael Scheinkman for Vineyard Wind stated, “This is an 806-megawatt project that’s generating output at less than half of that, which is not sufficient to generate commercially viable levels of electricity.” The next court hearing is scheduled for May 12. [New Bedford Light, May 1 2026, link to article]

Mid-Atlantic

nothing to see here, folks

Southeast

nothing to see here, folks

Midwest

INDIANA

Tippecanoe County adopts revised solar ordinance, lifting 11-month moratorium: Tippecanoe County commissioners unanimously approved a new large-scale solar ordinance on May 4, 2026, ending an 11-month moratorium. The ordinance caps individual commercial solar developments at 400 acres, increases setbacks to 500 feet from adjacent property lines, and limits total commercial solar development in the county to 6,000 acres. Commissioner Dave Byers stated, “As the resident farmer up here, ... don’t think I don’t cringe every time we agree to a 40-acre development or an 80-acre development. I know that’s good ground disappearing. I was against this from the beginning. I see this as a compromise.” The changes were prompted by the proposed 1,700-acre Rainbow Trout Solar project. [AOL / StarCityTV, May 4 2026, link to article]

INDIANA

Pulaski County building inspector to discuss solar moratorium with commissioners: Pulaski County commissioners met (May 4, 2026) at 6 p.m. at the Pulaski County Courthouse in Winamac. The agenda includes discussion of a solar moratorium led by the county building inspector. No vote or specific project details were announced. [WKVI, May 4 2026, link to article]

MICHIGAN

Meridian Township Board considers creating data center and battery storage facility moratorium: The Meridian Township Board is considering a moratorium on data centers and battery storage facilities to allow time to develop protective guidelines. Supervisor Scott Hendrickson stated, “The reason why I was looking to chat about moratoriums, specifically, is because what I feel like we have seen across the state is a propensity to buy up property, eliminate the existing use and convert into a data center or a battery storage facility.” Community Planning Director Tim Schmitt noted no current proposals exist in the township. A decision could come at the May 7 meeting. [WKAR, May 4 2026, link to article]

CO/NM/NV

COLORADO

Two-year moratorium on commercial alternative energy projects in Grand County continues until June 2027: The Grand County Board of County Commissioners voted 3-0 to extend a two-year moratorium on commercial solar, wind, battery, and energy storage projects (those exceeding 25 kW) until June 24, 2027. The moratorium, which began June 24, 2025, gives the county time to update its master plan and draft regulations. Community Development Director Kristen Manguso stated, “We have less than 30 percent of developable area… The land is valuable here, and it is more valuable for development than it is for solar farms.” The measure does not affect residential solar. [Sky-Hi News, May 4 2026, link to article]

AZ/UT

nothing to see here, folks

California

CALIFORNIA

California investigates Trump administration’s deal to end an offshore wind project: The California Energy Commission issued an administrative subpoena to Golden State Wind seeking documents about its agreement with the Trump administration to terminate its offshore wind lease off California’s central coast in exchange for a payout (part of nearly $900 million in recent deals). CEC Chair David Hochschild stated, “The Trump administration is recklessly spending billions of taxpayer dollars on backroom deals that would turn back the clock on innovation. Californians deserve immediate answers about the nature of this payout.” The administration has committed nearly $2 billion total to wind down offshore wind leases. [AP News, May 2026, link to article]

staff@greenlightamerica.org

Subject

Daily Clean Energy Clips 5/5/2026

Northeast

NEW YORK

Clay Town Board to hold hearing on proposed battery storage law: The Town of Clay will hold a public hearing Monday at 7 p.m. at Town Hall on Local Law No. 4 of 2026, which would create guidelines for siting and installing battery energy storage systems. Two companies, Carson Power and Nexamp, are exploring potential sites in the town. Town officials noted the law aims to ensure responsible siting while supporting the transition to renewables. Local fire officials have begun discussions with developers on emergency response planning. [CNY Central, May 4 2026, link to article]

Southold Town Board extends BESS moratorium to 2027 amid safety and zoning debate: The Southold Town Board unanimously approved a 12-month extension of its moratorium on battery energy storage systems through April 2027. The moratorium, first enacted in 2023, gives the town time to update zoning, reconvene the BESS Task Force, and address fire safety concerns following a proposed 60 MW lithium-ion BESS by Key Capture Energy on Oregon Road in Cutchogue. Supervisor Al Krupski stated, “The recommended 12-month extension of the moratorium would be time to complete and accomplish the steps recommended by the state in their new BESS guidance document.” Kevin O’Mara of Friends of Oregon Road said, “The town has spent millions of dollars preserving the agricultural land around this site, which would become unusable if there were a fire because of the contamination of these heavy metals.” [Suffolk Times, April 24 2026, link to article]

MASSACHUSETTS

AG rules against Southwick’s temporary moratorium on solar arrays and BESS systems: The Massachusetts Attorney General’s office disapproved Southwick’s temporary moratorium on large-scale solar arrays and battery energy storage systems, ruling it lacked sufficient public health, safety, or welfare justification and violated state law prohibiting unreasonable regulation of solar installations. The moratorium had been approved at a Special Town Meeting in mid-January 2026 and was set to last until May 31, 2027. Town Planner Alvin Allen stated, “If a company puts in an application, we’d have to move forward through our current process and hear it within 60 days.” Planning Board Chair Jessica Thornton said the ruling “was not unexpected” and that the town is awaiting a state template for zoning bylaws. [Yahoo News / local reporting, May 2 2026, link to article]

CONNECTICUT

House OKs solar bill; GOP targets impact on public benefits charge: The Connecticut House passed House Bill 5340 on May 1, 2026, reauthorizing the state’s residential, commercial, and community solar programs through 2035 with an $85 million annual budget target. The bill includes expedited permitting for plug-in solar panels, a one-year moratorium on new grid-scale solar in East Windsor and Enfield, and exemptions for solar paired with battery storage. Lori Brown of the Connecticut League of Conservation Voters said, “If they don’t pass a solar bill this session to extend the programs, it would be an epic failure on the part of a legislature.” State Sen. Ryan Fazio, R-Greenwich, called it “unconscionable policy… extraordinarily expensive.” [CT Mirror / Now Solar, May 2 2026, link to article]

Mid-Atlantic

KENTUCKY

Fiscal Court hears first reading and preliminarily approves two-year BESS moratorium: The Henderson Fiscal Court gave first reading and preliminary approval to a two-year moratorium on battery energy storage systems. The moratorium, which will begin upon final passage (likely May 12), extends a prior one-year resolution passed in January. The Henderson City-County Planning Commission had recommended the two-year term after a public hearing. [The Hendersonian, May 1 2026, link to article]

VIRGINIA

Virginia’s new law blocks counties from banning solar: Gov. Abigail Spanberger signed legislation that voids community-wide bans on solar fields and requires localities to submit written reasons when denying permits. The law takes effect July 1. Two-thirds of Virginia counties currently ban or heavily restrict solar. Evan Vaughan of the Mid-Atlantic Renewable Energy Coalition said, “Localities still are in the driver’s seat here. They can still deny every project from now until the end of time if they want.” [Canary Media, April 28 2026, link to article]

Southeast

NORTH CAROLINA

Regulators hit pause on Duke Energy solar expansion: The North Carolina Utilities Commission ordered Duke Energy to pause its 2026 solar procurement process (approximately 770 MW) until the Commission completes review of the company’s updated Carbon Plan. Decisions on new solar could be delayed until late 2026 or later. Will Scott of the Environmental Defense Fund stated, “Delaying new solar procurements will only hurt households and the businesses that rely on North Carolina’s pro-business environment.” [WRAL, April 24 2026, link to article]

Midwest

MICHIGAN

Bills to repeal Michigan’s clean energy mandate advanced by GOP-controlled House committee: The House Energy Committee advanced HB 5710 and HB 5711 along party lines. HB 5711 would eliminate the 100% clean energy standard; HB 5710 would remove environmental justice and greenhouse gas considerations from utility planning. Samarth Medakkar of Advanced Energy United said, “With energy costs skyrocketing this winter and the expensive summer cooling season looming, it is alarmingly clear why it’s so important that we diversify our energy resource mix.” [Michigan Advance, April 28 2026, link to article]

OHIO

Emails reveal Richland County commissioner’s comments about residents who opposed ban on wind and solar farms: Commissioner Darrell Banks emailed a GOP consultant “Mission accomplished” after the commission banned wind and solar in 11 townships, adding he “would not be surprised if there is a cross burning in my future” regarding resident opposition. The ban faces a referendum vote on May 5. [Energy and Policy Institute, May 3 2026, link to article]

North-Central

nothing to see here, folks

South-Central

KANSAS

Decision on Sedgwick County solar project delayed: The Metropolitan Area Planning Commission deferred its decision on Mission Clean Energy’s proposed solar project (nearly 1,500 acres, 600,000 panels plus battery storage) southwest of Wichita until June 11. The project is projected to generate $79–129 million in tax revenue over 40+ years. Ethan Frazier with Mission Clean Energy stated, “We’re generating between $79-129 million of tax revenue.” [Now Solar / KAKE, May 3 2026, link to article]

KANSAS

KCK pushes forward $250M battery storage facility plans: Wyandotte County commissioners approved a special use permit (8-2 vote) for East Side Storage LLC’s $250 million battery storage facility on 64 acres in Kansas City, Kansas. The project is expected to deliver $27 million in property taxes over 20 years and $9 million in annual ratepayer savings. [Kansas City Star, May 2 2026, link to article]

Plains

NORTH DAKOTA

PSC schedules public hearing for proposed Longspur Wind Project: The North Dakota Public Service Commission will hold a public hearing May 18 on Minnesota Power’s proposed Longspur Wind Project — up to 202.5 MW with 45 turbines on 26,100 acres in Morton County — plus a 2.5-mile 230-kV transmission line to Mercer County. Total estimated cost: $791 million. [ND PSC News Release, May 4 2026, link to article]

NEW MEXICO

Judge temporarily denies Rancho Viejo Solar opponents’ request for records: A state district judge temporarily blocked the Clean Energy Coalition’s discovery requests in its appeal of Santa Fe County’s approval of the 96 MW Rancho Viejo Solar project (plus battery storage) on 680 acres south of Santa Fe. Coalition president Lee Zlotoff said the group is seeking information on whether “deals were made between the county and AES.” [Yahoo News / Santa Fe New Mexican, May 2 2026, link to article]

Daily Clean Energy Clips 5/4/2026

Northeast

NEW YORK

Two Rivers Solar Farm public hearing draws no comments “A 15-minute public hearing on Wednesday to take comments on the proposed Two Rivers Solar Farm in Brasher and Massena drew no comments.Two Rivers had filed a petition seeking a Certificate of Public Convenience and Necessity (CPCN) and approval of financing… A 15-minute public hearing on Wednesday to take comments on the proposed Two Rivers Solar Farm in Brasher and Massena drew no comments. Two Rivers had filed a petition seeking a Certificate of Public Convenience and Necessity (CPCN) and approval of financing.” [Yahoo News, 04/30/2026, link to article]

Wilson looking to pass battery energy storage law “Wilson is set to vote on a new local law limiting where certain battery energy storage systems can operate in the village. The law would limit Tier 2 battery storage systems in industrial-zoned areas, which for the village would be its southern parcels. Tier 1 storage systems, which can store up to 600 kWh, are allowed in all areas of the village, given their usage for rooftop solar systems. The language is based on the model law for battery storage that NYSERDA released, with Village Mayor Gregory Marin saying it is very similar to the Village of Lancaster’s law… The Niagara County Planning Board gave its support to this law at its April meeting, with the village board planning to vote on the law at its May 21 meeting. Wilson is among many Niagara County municipalities looking to establish such regulations, with others including Cambria, Newfane, Pendleton, and Porter.” [Yahoo News, 04/30/2026, link to article]

Southold Extends Moratorium on Controversial BESS Energy Storage Sites “The Town of Southold extended a moratorium on the construction of Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS) on the North Fork to give officials time to finalize regulation of the facilities. The Southold Town Board unanimously voted on April 21 in favor of extending the BESS moratorium for another year following a public hearing. The decision was made to give the town’s Battery Energy Task Force and town board to follow New York State guidance on setting BESS policies to help mitigate the potential for harder-to-extinguish fires that can break out at such facilities.” [Politics NY, 04/30/2026, link to article]

Southeast

Midwest

INDIANA

“The Pulaski County Advisory Plan Commission (APC) is recommending a 12 month moratorium  on Commercial Solar Energy Systems (CSES) to the Pulaski County Commissioners. A special meeting with a public hearing was held on Monday, April 20… Before hearing public comment, Building Inspector and Plan Administrator Karla Redweik read the proposed ordinance with the 12 month moratorium aloud. The purpose of the ordinance and the moratorium is to give them time to understand the prevalence and presence of CSES within the zoning jurisdiction of Pulaski County, in consideration of the public life, safety, health and general welfare of Pulaski County, the effects on residents and natural resources and to conduct appropriate studies and to gauge whether CSES is appropriate within Pulaski County, and if so, the extent to which regulation should be developed and enforced beyond current ordinance regulations.” [Pulaski County Journal, 4/30/26, link to article]

OHIO

Headline “ANDOVER — The Ashtabula County Board of Commissioners spoke to local residents Wednesday at the Andover Public Library… Commissioner J.P. Ducro spoke about potential wind and solar power projects in the county. ‘The position of the commissioners has really been wanting to defer to local control,’ he said… He said the commissioners are aware some people have been approached about land leases. ‘Nothing has really even come to our desk at this point and time for us to weigh in on officially,’ Ducro said. Under Ohio Senate Bill 52, county commissioners have the power to give a thumbs up or down to economically significant wind and solar projects, which generate between five and 50 megawatts, he said. Ducro said the commissioners are working with the county building department to map out where they would permit or not permit wind and solar projects. He said the plan is to detail and update the map with each township wants them, do not want them or wants to restrict them.‘That process is probably going to take some time,’ Ducro said. ‘We imagine that that’s going to probably take us through the year 2026.’” [Yahoo News, 04/30/2026, link to article]

North-Central

IOWA

Kossuth County Holds Public Hearing on Wind Farm Restart “The Kossuth County Board of Supervisors held a public hearing to potentially repower the Lakota Wind Farm. Iowa Lakes Electric Cooperative President and CEO Ryan Craig explained why the cooperative was applying for the permit. The project will work with the present wind generation units by upgrading them according to Craig. The hope is by using existing towers and systems, the cooperative can realize the full potential of the wind farm. The board approved the proposal.” [Mix 107.3 KIOW, 04/29/2026, link to article]

Solar debate spurs passionate comments from York County residents “This week’s solar zoning debate spurred many passionate comments from York County residents as the county commissioners were tasked with creating solar zoning regulations. A public hearing was held Monday night and more public comment was heard Tuesday morning before the county board took a vote, ultimately deciding (with a split vote) to create regulations that include half-mile setbacks for non-participating residences, churches and schools.” [YORK COUNTY, NEBRASKA, 4/30/26, link to article]

Daily Clean Energy Clips Month/Date/Year

Northeast

MASSACHUSETTS

[Opinion] No time for timidity on clean energy “Massachusetts has long been a leader in advancing smart, effective energy and environmental policies that reduce pollution, protect public health, create jobs, and lower costs for consumers over time. But lately, too many policymakers in Massachusetts are staying silent or even backtracking on proven strategies. Policymakers are failing to push back as fossil fuel interests try to distract from the harm they cause to our environment, public health, and runaway energy costs. As fuel prices surge from the war in the Middle East and residents demand relief from their monthly bills, now is the time to deliver clean energy and energy efficiency solutions for the people of Massachusetts.” [CommonWealth Beacon, 04/28/2026, link to article]

NEW YORK

NY PSC Virtual Public Hearing on North Seneca Solar “The New York State Public Service Commission (Commission) will hold a virtual public statement hearing and procedural conference on Wednesday, May 6, 2026, regarding a petition filed on December 18, 2025, by North Seneca Solar Project, LLC (North Seneca Solar) seeking a Certificate of Public Convenience and Necessity (CPCN) pursuant to Public Service Law (PSL) Section 68, lightened regulation as a wholesale electric generator. North Seneca Solar seeks authorization to construct and operate a solar-powered electric generating facility with a maximum capacity of 90 megawatts in the towns of Waterloo and Junius, Seneca County. The facility will interconnect to the New York power grid via a switching station that will connect to the existing National Grid Farmington – Hamilton 115 kilovolt transmission line.” [Department of Public Service, 4/29/26, link to article]

Public Hearing on Town of Walton 1-Year Solar Moratorium “PLEASE TAKE NOTICE that, for the purpose of adopting Local Law 3 of 2026, A Local Law to Establish a One (1) Year Moratorium on ‘Solar Energy Systems’ in the Town of Walton, a public hearing will be held by the Town Board at the Walton Town Hall in the Town of Walton, on the 11 day of May, 2026, at 6:00 p.m.” [The Reporter (Catskills Today), 04/30/2026, link to article]

Southeast

Nothing to see here, folks!

Midwest

This Ohio county put a ban on wind and solar. Will voters reverse it? “Last July, Richland County banned large-scale wind and solar projects in 11 of its 18 townships. The decision not only caught many locals by surprise; it also struck them as bad for economic development and as encroaching on individual property rights. Almost immediately after the county’s three commissioners made their decision, dozens of residents formed a group, called the Richland County Citizens for Property Rights and Job Development, to fight what they saw as an unjust restriction on renewable energy. Their initial goal was clear but daunting: Collect thousands of in-person signatures within 30 days in order to put the clean energy ban on the ballot during the 2026 primary election. They succeeded.” [Canary Media, 04/12/2026, link to article]

North-Central

Cerro Gordo County officials clash with state over solar development “The Iowa Utilities Commission has issued a final order advancing review of a proposed large-scale solar project in Cerro Gordo County, setting up a clash with local officials who enacted a moratorium and subsequent ban on such developments. In a decision filed Monday, April 27, the commission required additional information from River City Energy LLC as part of its application for a generating certificate for a 500-megawatt solar facility planned on roughly 2,250 acres in the county. The state’s action comes after Cerro Gordo County supervisors already moved to halt such projects locally. Following months of public meetings and debate, the board approved an ordinance amendment banning new utility-scale wind, solar and battery storage developments in unincorporated areas, replacing a temporary moratorium first adopted to study land-use impacts.” [Globe Gazette , 04/29/2026, link to article]

Central-Central

KANSAS

Decision delayed on proposed energy project in Sedgwick County “A proposed solar and battery storage project in Sedgwick County will not go to county commissioners just yet. During a special meeting on Thursday, the Wichita-Sedgwick County Metropolitan Area Planning Commission chose to put off a decision on the Galena Solar Project until June 11. The company in charge of the project, Mission Clean Energy, submitted its application at the end of January. If it’s approved, 1,490 acres of land southwest of Wichita would be used for solar panels and battery energy storage systems. Energy from the systems would be transmitted to an Evergy substation near the project area.” [Yahoo News, 04/29/2026, link to article]

[Opinion] Proposed Evergy electric transmission line would only hurt Kansas “As a Kansas rancher, I am writing to sound the alarm on Evergy’s proposed 133-mile, 345 kilovolt Buffalo Flats transmission line. This project, currently before the Kansas Corporation Commission, represents a profound threat to our state’s heritage, economy and environment. First, the Buffalo Flats project stinks of an insider deal. Unlike most major infrastructure, this $500 million project was not put out for public bid. On other Kansas projects, competitive bidding for transmission lines has realized savings of 40%. By bypassing this process, Kansas ratepayers are potentially left on the hook for hundreds of millions in unnecessary costs.” [Wichita Eagle, 04/29/2026, link to article]

South-Central

Nothing to see here, folks!

Plains

NEBRASKA

York County Commissioners approve zoning for solar farm, creates ‘tough environment’ for project “The York County Commissioners voted 3-2 to approve the zoning regulations surrounding a proposed 3,000-acre solar farm. Dozens of community members came out to make their voices heard one last time, filling every seat in the room and pleading with commissioners. ‘I moved out to York County to be surrounded by farm land, not an industrial solar park,’ said Kirkland Doht, a York County resident. The zoning regulations in place include a half-mile setback from any homeowner who is not a willing partner and from platted subdivisions. Other setbacks include 330 feet from cemeteries and 660 feet from state recreational areas. Commissioners decided to adjust the setback from churches and schools, which was previously set to 660 feet and is now a half-mile.” [KLKN-TV, 04/28/2026, link to article]

California

San Benito County keeps battery storage at bay, DMV data sharing could hurt undocumented drivers “The San Benito County Board of Supervisors has voted unanimously to adopt a new temporary moratorium on Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS) in unincorporated areas. The urgency ordinance passed on Tuesday cited public health and safety concerns. It extends a 45-day moratorium approved on March 10 for another 45 days. The county says they’ve used the moratorium to update parts of their countywide safety plans related to evacuations, emergency response, and hazardous materials.” [90.3 KAZU, 04/29/2026, link to article]

Northeast

NEW YORK

Horicon to hold public meeting on battery-storage facility moratorium “The Horican Town Board will hold a public meeting on Thursday, May 21, at 6 p.m. to discuss proposed Local Law No. 1 of 2026, which would impose a one-year moratorium on battery-storage system facilities in the Town of Horicon. Energy-storage systems are defined as one or more devices capable of storing energy derived from solar, wind or other sources to be released at a later time. The proposed moratorium would not apply to any storage facilities that have already received final approvals and permits from the Town. Copies of Local Law No. 1 are on file in the Town Clerk's Office and can also be found on the Town's website, https://www.horiconny.gov..” [suncommunitynews.com, 04/28/2026, link to article]

KENTUCKY

Several items approved at Henderson Fiscal Court “Several approvals were made at Tuesday’s Fiscal Court meeting in Henderson… Magistrates also approved the planning commission’s recommendation for a two year moratorium on a Battery Energy Storage System, or BESS.” [https://www.14news.com, 04/28/2026, link to article]

Midwest

OHIO

Public Hearing Set for Massive Vinton County Solar and Storage Project “State officials have officially scheduled a local public hearing to gather community feedback on a proposed 149-megawatt hybrid solar and energy storage facility in Vinton County. The project, spearheaded by Hamden Energy LLC, is slated for construction on approximately 945 acres of reclaimed mine land within Clinton Township. The site sits about two miles east of the village of Hamden. Hearing Details[:]The Ohio Power Siting Board (OPSB) will host the hearing to allow residents to provide sworn testimony regarding the development. Date: June 10, 2026[,] Time: 5:00 PM[,] Location: Vinton County High School Cafeteria (63910 US-50, McArthur, Ohio 45651)” [Scioto Post, 04/28/2026, link to article]

North-Central

MINNESOTA

[Opinion] What does it mean to execute renewable energy in Minnesota? “Renewable Energy has been marketed as the oasis of clean energy, but what if this wasn’t the whole truth?... In truth, clean energy, specifically, renewable energy, is very sustainable and efficient when it’s working; however, the process that goes into constructing them is less sustainable. The latest example of this is the Boundary Waters… last Thursday, the Senate overturned this law in a 50-49 vote, jumpstarting the long-delayed mine proposed for the Superior National Forest near the Boundary Waters… The mining will contaminate the groundwater and just disturb the nature that makes the Boundary Waters what it is… These events all come as Minnesota strives toward its 2040 goal: establishing carbon-free electricity and becoming 55% renewable energy… Even with all the risks and immediate problems the Minnesota Clean Energy Initiative imposes on the average Minnesotan, the state still needs to move forward with it.” [The Rubicon, 4/27/26, link to article]

KANSAS

Large-scale renewable energy project under consideration near Wichita “A renewable energy project is under consideration for the construction of a solar farm and battery storage facility on about 1,500 acres near Wichita. On Wednesday, the Wichita-Sedgwick County Metropolitan Area Planning Commission will consider a request from Galena Solar Project, LLC to rezone 1,490 acres from about 119th Street West to a little east of South Ridge Road and from West 47th Street South to West 63rd Street South. This is just southwest of Wichita.” [https://www.kwch.com, 04/28/2026, link to article]

Austin County Commissioners Court Hold Public Hearing on Data Center, BESS Project “If you were unable to attend the public hearing on April 27 at the Austin County Commissioners Court regarding the proposed BESS project in Wallis, you can still stay informed by watching the full hearing video. The meeting gave residents an opportunity to hear details, ask questions, and share concerns about the proposed battery energy storage system. If you could not attend in person, the full video is available to watch so you can review the discussion for yourself: https://t.co/IgeGSzm30O ” [X (formerly Twitter), 04/29/2026, link to article]

Daily Clean Energy Clips April/29/2026

Northeast

NEW YORK

NY PSC to Hold Public Statement Hearing to Discuss Two Rivers Solar Farm “The New York Public Service Commission will hold a virtual public statement hearing to discuss Two Rivers Solar Farm LLC’s proposed 200-megawatt solar electric generating facility in St. Lawrence County. The facility would interconnect to the power grid via the New York Power Authority’s Haverstock – Willis E 345 kilovolt transmission line. The company plans to build the project on land leased and/or purchased from owners of private property within an area of approximately 3,168 acres. The project has the potential to power approximately 56,000 homes. The company is seeking a lightened regulatory regime as it intends to operate the project as a competitive wholesale generator. Comments are due by May 6. 25-E-0616” [EnerKnol, 04/24/2026, link to article, more reading here]

Guilderland sets four hearings inspired by updated comprehensive plan “On April 21, the board agreed to set public hearings for four proposed local laws… A moratorium on battery energy storage systems, known as BESS… During the April 21 public comment period, Robyn Gray, who chairs the Guilderland Coalition for Responsible Growth, told the board that residents had been receiving solicitation letters from energy developers offering ‘anywhere between ten- and thirty-thousand dollars a year rent’ to install shipping-container-sized batteries on their properties… Supervisor Peter Barber told Gray the town was already moving on the issue, proposing to enact a temporary ban on BESS installations. Later in the meeting, the board set a May 19 public hearing on Barber’s proposed four-month moratorium.” [The Altamont Enterprise, link to article]

Town of Oyster Bay approves sixth battery energy system storage moratorium “The Town of Oyster Bay board voted unanimously to extend its moratorium on battery energy storage systems for another 12 months, citing ongoing safety concerns and continued public opposition during a recent meeting. The resolution passed with six votes in favor and none opposed as part of a broader action calendar approved by the town board during their regular April 14 meeting. It is another win for the anti-battery storage movement in the Glen Head community, which rallied around opposition to the Oyster Shore Energy Storage, a now-cancelled project by Jupiter Power in Glen Head which the moratorium was initially created to prevent.” [Herald Community Newspapers, 04/24/2026, link to article]

Dannemora Supervisor Varin engages discussion “In April, newly elected Town Supervisor Joey Varin held an open community meeting to learn about local concerns… A key development proposal the town is reviewing would place a battery storage system near the power substation just outside Lyon Mountain… The Leo Energy Storage project in Dannemora proposes at 75 megawatt (MW) - 300 MWh, four-hour standalone Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) would be built on approximately 16 acres of previously disturbed land, next to an existing 115-kilovolt transmission corridor… Varin said he will continue to hold open meetings with residents to keep conversations going. He is going to schedule another sesson near the end of May, drawing discussion toward residents at the southern side of town, below the mountain.” [suncommunitynews.com, 04/27/2026, link to article]

Midwest

INDIANA

Dearborn County plan commission votes against creating a solar farm advisory committee “The Dearborn County Plan Commission voted unanimously 8-0 against forming an advisory committee to amend commercial ordinances regarding solar, battery energy storage systems and data centers. The vote took place Monday evening, during the commission's first meeting since a 12-month moratorium on such developments was enacted in February. Elected officials said there will still be opportunities for public input throughout the ordinance process… It will now be up to the county commissioners to decide if a review committee is formed at that level, as recommended by the Plan Commission. The first in a series of informational workshops will be held May 20, where officials said there will be more opportunities for public input.” [WCPO 9 Cincinnati, 04/28/2026, link to article]

IOWA

Franklin County Supervisors Meet Tuesday “The Franklin County Board of Supervisors will meet Tuesday in regular session at the courthouse, with an agenda that includes budget action, infrastructure planning and policy discussions… Supervisors will also discuss a possible moratorium on future battery storage facilities and related road damage concerns…Drainage issues will be a focus later in the morning, including consideration of a utility permit application for Alliant Energy. Supervisors also are expected to acknowledge a revised engineer’s report for Drainage District 3 and set a hearing to accept the revised report… The meeting begins at 8:30 a.m. The full agenda is below; 20260428-agenda-bos” [RadioOnTheGo.com, 04/27/2026, link to article]

[Petition] Stop the Lava Run and Black Ridge Projects in Apache County “As a tax payer/resident of Apache County, I am OPPOSED to the proposed Lava Run and Black Ridge wind turbine and solar projects. These projects, primarily driven by foreign companies, threaten to strip away the unique beauty and tranquility of our pristine natural environment. Our land, which has been cherished and preserved for generations, now stands at the brink of irrevocable change.” [Change.org, 04/25/2026, link to article]
