Daily Clean Energy Clips April/28/2026


Northeast

CONNECTICUT

CT public benefits charge cut; electricity rates to fall by nearly 15% “Electricity rates in Connecticut will decline by nearly 15% in May, after state regulators on Wednesday approved changes that will all but remove the public benefits charge from customer bills. The Public Utilities Regulatory Authority voted unanimously in favor of the rate adjustments, which largely affect the costs borne by utilities for various state-mandated policy programs such as clean energy procurements and benefits for low-income customers.” [CT Mirror, 04/22/2026, link to article]

NEW HAMPSHIRE

House defeats advanced nuclear proposal from Senate “The House of Representatives voted Thursday against a bill that sought to allow utilities to build and own advanced nuclear reactors. Senate Bill 447 was defeated on the consent calendar by voice vote. From prime sponsor Sen. Kevin Avard, a Nashua Republican, the proposal would have allowed utilities to own ‘advanced’ nuclear reactors up to 300 megawatts in capacity, about one quarter the size of New Hampshire’s only active nuclear reactor in Seabrook.” [New Hampshire Bulletin, 04/23/2026, link to article]

NEW YORK

Southold Town Board extends BESS moratorium to 2027 amid safety, zoning debate “Southold Town Board unanimously approved a 12-month extension of its moratorium on Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) facilities on Tuesday — buying time to draft and vote on local legislation governing their use. The latest extension keeps the moratorium in place through April 2027. The law was first enacted by the Town Board on Jan. 31, 2023, with an effective date of April 11, 2023, following public scrutiny of a proposed BESS facility by Key Capture Energy on Oregon Road in Cutchogue the previous year… Town officials said the additional year will allow them to reconvene the town’s BESS Task Force, gather public input and craft regulations aligned with new state guidance.” [longisland-ny.com, 4/24/26, link to article]

Mid-Atlantic

KENTUCKY

Henderson Fiscal Court: BESS Moratorium, Housing Rules & Road Projects “The Henderson County Fiscal Court will meet on Tuesday, April 28, 2026, at 9:30 a.m. The session will begin with an invocation by Father Ben Newland of St. Paul’s Episcopal Church, followed by the Pledge of Allegiance and roll call. Minutes from the April 14 meeting will be reviewed… The court will consider several key items: an appointment to the City-County Air Board, recommendations from the Henderson City-County Planning Commission on Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS) and manufactured housing, and first readings of ordinances to create a BESS moratorium and new manufactured housing regulations. A resolution for a Memorandum of Understanding between the City of Henderson and HMG will also be reviewed.” [NewsBreak, 04/25/2026, link to article]

Headline “Quoted text from article with key summary information. Include quotes from key individuals where available.” [Publication, date, link to article]

Southeast

ALABAMA

Baldwin County residents push for solar moratorium as legal review continues “A group of North Baldwin County residents is asking commissioners to temporarily halt large-scale solar development, citing environmental concerns and a lack of independent oversight. Megan Fowler spoke today on behalf of Friends of the Tensaw River, a newly formed grassroots organization, who made the request during Tuesday’s Baldwin County Commission meeting. The group is calling for a temporary moratorium on new utility-scale solar projects - potentially lasting up to 180 days - to give the county time to study environmental impacts, hire an independent engineering firm, and consider new regulations.” [WEAR2, 04/21/2026, link to article]

Midwest

ILLINOIS

Illinois Court Orders County to Issue Permits for Six Solar Energy Projects “A Will County circuit court ordered the county board to issue permits for six solar energy projects in one of the first decisions applying last month’s landmark Equity Solar appellate ruling. Several lawsuits are pending throughout Illinois in which solar energy developers are challenging county boards that have denied siting permits for solar energy facilities… In Channahon McKinley Woods Solar, the Will County court ruled on six consolidated cases brought by solar energy developers against the county. The developers challenged the county’s refusal to issue special use permits for proposed solar facilities despite the proposed facilities meeting all statutory requirements. The court granted summary judgment in all cases, ordering the county to approve ordinances granting special use permits for all plaintiffs by April 17.” [JD Supra, 4/24/26, link to article]

MICHIGAN

Neighbors, Zeeland Township take steps to oppose proposed solar farm in Ottawa County “Neighbors in Zeeland and Jamestown townships are intensifying their opposition to a proposed solar farm, taking steps to become formal participants in the state review process. Earlier this month, project developer RWE Americas, a subsidiary of RWE, filed an application with the Michigan Public Service Commission (MPSC) to construct the 200-megawatt Silver Maple Solar Farm, bypassing local permitting efforts. The proposed facility would cover 1,900 acres in Zeeland and Jamestown townships and produce enough energy to power more than 34,000 homes. On Friday, nearly 100 residents gathered at the Ottawa Executive Airport in Zeeland Township for a workshop guiding them through how to petition to intervene in the state’s regulatory process — a step that allows certain parties to submit information and have a voice during hearings.” [FOX 17 West Michigan News (WXMI), 04/25/2026, link to article]

North-Central

IOWA

Iowa Supreme Court says Worth County can limit wind turbine project “A $300 million wind project in Worth County received a blow after the Iowa Supreme Court upheld a county zoning ordinance that would reduce the buildable sites for turbines by up to 98%. Worthwhile Wind and Invenergy, its parent company, spent several years and roughly $2.8 million, planning and preparing for the project. It was expected to generate 165 megawatts of electricity.” [The Des Moines Register, 04/27/2026, link to article]

Renewable ordinance headed to final stages “One way or the other, the months-long saga over a proposed renewable energy ordinance for Wapello County may be nearing its conclusion. The board of supervisors set May 5 as the public hearing for the first reading of a revised ordinance that was reworked following a mediation session two months ago between Darren Batterson, Bryan Ziegler and Des Moines attorney Eric Updegraff. The third-floor McGiverin Courtroom was filled just for the date-setting hearing, with a handful of county residents speaking against the Dusty Fox Wind project. Because of strict setbacks contained in the revised ordinance, the Dusty Fox project — despite nearly 20,000 acres of land signed up for wind turbine leases — may be in jeopardy… It takes only one supervisor to vote no for the ordinance to fail, since supervisor Carrie Teninty has abstained due to a potential conflict of interest after her family signed a lease three years ago.” [Yahoo News, 04/23/2026, link to article]

Plains

NEBRASKA

OPPD halts $610M York County solar project amid zoning delays “Omaha Public Power District (OPPD) has allowed its Generator Interconnection Agreement for the proposed K-Junction solar project near McCool Junction to expire, pausing the project for now. The utility said the decision was made because of ongoing uncertainty around York County’s proposed solar zoning regulations. The project, which could have delivered up to 310 megawatts of solar power and generated more than $610 million in economic development and $30 million in local tax revenue, cannot move forward until new interconnection milestones are approved. A public hearing on the county’s proposed utility-scale solar zoning regulations is scheduled for April 27 at the York County Courthouse. Residents can attend to learn more or share input on the future of solar development in the area.” [NewsBreak, 04/24/2026, link to article]

Pacific Northwest

WASHINGTON

Snoqualmie neighbors rally against battery storage facility as permit application looms “Hundreds of Snoqualmie residents marched through their neighborhood Sunday — one day before the developer behind a proposed lithium-ion battery storage facility is expected to file a permit application with King County… The proposed 45-acre facility would be located on parcels immediately south of the park. Residents carried signs reading ‘Don't BESS With Us’ — BESS standing for Battery Energy Storage System — and chanted as they marched. Many brought their children, and some of those children took to the podium to address the crowd, echoing their parents' concerns about the project. The protest marked a rapid escalation of community opposition. Residents say they only learned about the project roughly a month ago, despite planning being underway since at least 2023.” [Yahoo News, 04/26/2026, link to article]

Appeal of Bayview Ridge battery energy storage facility set for June “An appeal of a Skagit County special-use permit for a battery energy storage system on Bayview Ridge will be heard June 23 in Whatcom County Superior Court. The Stewards of Skagit have filed the appeal over the permit, which was issued in January 2025. In June 2025, the Skagit County Board of Commissioners denied an appeal of the permit by the Stewards of Skagit, setting up the appeal in court under the state's Land Use Petition Act. The Stewards of Skagit cite concerns regarding safety, and an improper approval process by the county hearing examiner. The battery energy storage system, or BESS, proposed by NextEra Energy would be on 22 acres of land on McFarland Road west of Burlington and connect to Puget Sound Energy's nearby Fredonia substation. The system would use lithium-ion batteries capable of storing up to 200 megawatt hours of electricity.” [Tri-City Herald, 04/25/2026, link to article]

Northern Rockies

Headline “Quoted text from article with key summary information. Include quotes from key individuals where available.” [Publication, date, link to article]

CO/NM/NV

Headline “Quoted text from article with key summary information. Include quotes from key individuals where available.” [Publication, date, link to article]

AZ/UT

UTAH

Public hearing regarding solar held By Emery County Commission “The Emery County Commission had several requests for new ordinances, resolutions, or at least new requirements for permitting solar companies, so they held a public hearing at 6 p.m. on Tuesday evening. The commission room and adjoining other rooms were full of citizens, and over thirty of them came to the mic to address the commissioners… Various speakers spoke about the environmental impact the solar projects have on the county, i.e., wind storms, dust, and a decrease in rain… Only a couple of speakers spoke about the advantages of solar; most just remarked, “No more solar!”” [NewsBreak, 04/23/2026, link to article]

Subject

Daily Clean Energy Clips 4/27/2026


Northeast

Nothing to see here, folks!

Mid-Atlantic

Nothing to see here, folks!

OH/PA

Nothing to see here, folks!

Southeast

NORTH CAROLINA

Duke Energy’s proactive grid upgrades under fire from electric co-ops: “Duke Energy, the state’s predominant utility, is moving to proactively upgrade poles and wires to create room for prospective solar farms. Rather than making improvements pegged to specific projects and then charging solar developers for the full cost, as it did in the past, the company is now building in anticipation of future grid needs and spreading the costs among all customers…But one set of Duke customers isn’t happy. North Carolina’s electric member cooperatives, which buy most of their power wholesale from the utility, filed a complaint with the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission in February over four grid projects. They argue that the cost of the upgrades — $57 million, in this case — should not be distributed evenly among all customers. Instead, they want solar developers to pay half the total cost. Many observers believe the protest is on shaky legal ground. Yet FERC is chaired by an appointee of President Donald Trump, who is known to attack renewable energy regardless of the law.” [Canary Media, 4/24/2026, link to article]

TENNESSEE

Tenaska, TVA sign agreement for 225 MW battery project in Tennessee: “Bobwhite Energy Storage, an affiliate of Tenaska, has signed a 20 year energy storage agreement with the Tennessee Valley Authority (TVA) for a 225 MW / 900 MWh project. Located in East Tennessee, the Bobwhite project is expected to begin construction in 2027 and reach commercial operation by late 2029.” [PV Magazine, 4/21/2026, link to article, more reading here]

Midwest

ILLINOIS

Shorewood, Channahon plan appeal of solar farm ruling by Will County judge: “The local legal battle over solar farms isn’t over despite a court order that forced the Will County Board last week to approve six projects…The villages of Channahon and Shorewood plan to appeal the decision by Will County Judge Ben Braun that ordered approval of the projects…The mayors of both villages look toward a Winnebago County case now in the state appellate court, hoping a decision that favors local control could eventually undo plans for three solar farms that would be built near their borders…A different decision in the Fourth District could move the legal fight over solar rules to the Illinois Supreme Court, where Channahon and Shorewood could eventually block the solar projects they oppose…” [Shaw Local, 4/23/2026, link to article]

INDIANA

Starke County Commissioners visit solar ordinances section-by-section: “At last week’s Starke County Commissioner’s meeting, the board meticulously went over two current ordinances in regards to solar projects, section by section…Commissioners discussed needing another visit of the ordinances before scheduling a public hearing for the final versions.” [WYMR FM, 4/23/2026, link to article]

North-Central

Nothing to see here, folks!

Central-Central

How Google’s plan to build a data center created a rift between local governments in eastern Iowa: “Most Iowans know Palo for the Duane Arnold Energy Center — a boiling water nuclear reactor just outside of town that shut down in 2020 after 45 years of operation. Its tall stack is Palo’s lone skyscraper…Mentioning the fields of solar panels strikes a chord for many Palo residents — and not a good one. Because they were installed on county land, the city has had limited control over the development and the impacts on its residents…Now, the city is involved in a similar power struggle with another major industrial project on the horizon. Palo and Linn County are at odds over a proposed Google data center” [Iowa Public Radio, 4/23/2026, link to article]

South-Central

LOUISIANA

State regulators approve $300M solar farm planned for former sugarcane fields in south Louisiana: “The Louisiana Public Service Commission greenlit plans last week for a near-$300 million solar farm that would be owned and operated by Entergy in Iberville Parish…If additional permitting, contracting and engineering procedures go according to plan, Entergy says it will break ground on the Cypress Harvest site in September of 2026, and plans to have it fully operational by September 2028. It would be the first and only solar farm in the parish…Entergy will hold a public meeting to educate and receive feedback from Iberville residents about the project on Tuesday, April 28 at the Carl F. Grant Civic Center in Plaquemine from 5 to 6:30 p.m.” [The Advocate, 4/23/2026, link to article]

Northeast

NEW YORK

Small Town New York Fights Solar Farm Takeover: “The moratorium is a temporary measure, lasting only six months. Parma Town Supervisor, David Ciufo, explained that the moratorium could be extended when it expires when residents asked for a longer, even permanent, moratorium on solar farms…” [Millstone Media Network LLC, 4/22/2026, link to article]

Virtual Public Statement Hearing and Procedural Conference Set Regarding St. Lawrence County Solar Farm: “The New York State Public Service Commission (Commission) will hold a virtual public statement hearing and procedural conference on Wednesday, April 29, 2026, regarding a petition filed on September 24, 2025, by Two Rivers Solar Farm, LLC (Two Rivers Solar) seeking a Certificate of Public Convenience and Necessity (CPCN)...Two Rivers Solar seeks authorization to construct and operate a solar-powered electric generating facility with a maximum capacity of 200 megawatts in the towns of Brasher and Massena in St. Lawrence County.” [New York State Department of Public Service, 4/22/2026, link to article]

Mid-Atlantic

Nothing to see here, folks!

OH/PA

OHIO

Ohio Is Where Wind and Solar Projects Go to Die, and Other Findings From New Research on State Permitting: “The Buckeye State had the most projects rejected by state regulators, with seven, and the most withdrawn by developers before a decision, with five, says a paper in the journal Frontiers in Sustainable Energy Policy…The Ohio Power Siting Board had approved every wind and solar project to come before it until 2021, when it began to give more weight to opposition from local governments and residents.” [Inside Climate News, 4/23/2026, link to article]

Southeast

NORTH CAROLINA

Public hearing slated on requested amendments to solar farm ordinance: “The Warren County Board of Commissioners will conduct a public hearing at 6 p.m. on Monday, May 4, regarding a petition for several changes to the county’s solar farm ordinance. The hearing will be held at the Warren County Armory Civic Center, located at 501 US Highway 158 Business East, Warrenton. The requested amendments would allow larger-scale solar farm projects with certain conditions that include strict regulations for buffers and decommissioning plans. The local debate on whether the maximum size of permitted solar farms should remain at 150 acres began last year as TED Renewables announced its Horizon Solar proposed to be made up of an east site and a west site.” [The Warren Record, 4/22/2026, link to article]

Midwest

INDIANA

Records show possible Lakeville solar farm expansion after initial proposal filed in 2024: “The Dumont Solar farm near Lakeville is looking to add more property to its project, which has yet to break ground…And there are questions as to whether the company will have to abide by the original St. Joseph County solar farm ordinance or an amended ordinance passed last year.” [WSBT22, 4/22/2026, link to article]

Earth Day Fair brings community together: “Purdue students and local environmental advocates gathered Wednesday on Memorial Mall for the Earth Day Fair, a tabling event held as part of Purdue’s 2026 Earth Week programming…One of the most immediate concerns Anderson said she had was a proposed Tippecanoe County solar ordinance that would sharply restrict large-scale solar development…[Megan Anderson, a senior campaign representative with Sierra Club Hoosier Chapter] said she encourages Purdue students, especially those from Indiana and Tippecanoe County, to pay attention to local hearings and speak up.” [The Exponent, 4/22/2026, link to article]

KANSAS

Proposed solar moratorium fails at county planning meeting: “A proposed two-year moratorium on commercial solar projects in Jackson County failed on a 4-4 vote during the Jackson County Planning Commission meeting last Thursday evening…Five people spoke in opposition to the moratorium, including participating landowners and Sherman Burnett of NextEra. Burnett said the Jeffrey Solar Project has been scaled back and would remain at 2,000 acres.” [The Holton Recorder, 4/22/2026, link to article]

UTAH

‘It’s not black and white’: Emery County’s solar debate intensifies: “Solar again took center stage as the Emery County Commissioners met on Tuesday, April 21, moving closer to new ordinances surrounding the placement and construction of utility-scale solar projects within the county after the topic dominated the regular meeting and a public-hearing…Phelps also presented the commission with a petition opposing the proposed construction of a solar field. Between the online and physical versions, over 625 residents have signed the petition…The public hearing did see residents speaking in favor of solar within the county…Commissioner Keven Jensen shared…‘I will say [this]: You have private property rights, but this is a command economy. This is not free markets. This is pushed by an ideology that hates who we are.’ [Castle Country Radio, 4/22/2026, link to article]

Northeast

NEW JERSEY

A New Jersey-based company that builds monopiles for offshore wind turbines files for bankruptcy as the state’s offshore wind ambitions fizzle due to political and financial obstacles: “Already facing eviction, the builder behind huge foundations for offshore wind turbines in New Jersey filed for bankruptcy protection this month…An American subsidiary of Germany’s EEW Group declared bankruptcy earlier this month, filing for Chapter 11 protection in U.S. Bankruptcy Court for the District of New Jersey, records show…Two companies planning offshore wind farms, Ørsted and Atlantic Shores, have cancelled their projects.” (NJ.com, 4/21/2026, link to article]

NEW YORK

Johnstown enacts battery moratorium 45 minutes after BESS proposed: “Precisely 45 minutes after consultant Elyse Shapiro finished her presentation on a two-megawatt battery energy storage system, Nexamp is proposing to be built in Johnstown, the common council declared a one-year moratorium on battery systems…[T]he vote was 4-0, with Council Member-at-large Scott Jeffers absent…” [the Leader-Herald, 4/22/2026, link to article]

OH/PA

Nothing to see here, folks!

Southeast

ALABAMA

Baldwin County residents push for solar moratorium as legal review continues: “A group of North Baldwin County residents is asking commissioners to temporarily halt large-scale solar development, citing environmental concerns and a lack of independent oversight…The group is calling for a temporary moratorium on new utility-scale solar projects - potentially lasting up to 180 days - to give the county time to study environmental impacts, hire an independent engineering firm, and consider new regulations…He said one option could be asking the Alabama Attorney General for an official opinion. Commissioners may consider that step at their May 5 meeting.” [NBC 15 News, 4/21/2026, link to article]

Midwest

ILLINOIS:

Rivian, Redwood Materials announce energy storage partnership: “In June 2025, Redwood Materials announced a new business unit named Redwood Energy that’s focused on assembling and deploying low-cost stationary energy-storage systems to help meet the growing power demands of AI data centers and other commercial applications…With lower-than-anticipated demand after billions of dollars in EV investments, other automakers, including General Motors and Ford Motor Co., are turning to stationary energy storage systems for new revenue streams…In February 2023, the company received a $2 billion conditional loan commitment from the Department of Energy to expand battery recycling capacity in the U.S.” [Wards Auto, 4/20/2026, link to article]

INDIANA

Study shows regulations on renewables harm local economies: “A new study from Ball State University shows that county-level restrictions on industrial-level renewable energy negatively impact local economies…The study found that Indiana counties with renewable energy restrictions experienced a net GDP loss of about $204 million, while adjacent counties without restrictions saw a net gain of about $13 million.” [Indiana Public Media, 4/20/2026, link to article]

Solar companies working with county to address concerns: “At Monday’s regular Dubois County Commissioner meeting, Commissioner Chad Blessigner discussed ongoing conversations with EDP Renewables regarding the second phase of their solar project in the Duff area. The company had come to the county to gauge their willingness to negotiate agreements to begin the process of expanding north of the railroad tracks crossing on County Road 600 West. With the moratorium in place, the county said any approvals would be on pause. The discussions come as the county prepares for the end of its current moratorium on solar developments. EDP is expected to seek approval for its second phase expansion once the moratorium expires in eight months.” [Dubois County Free Press, 4/21/2026, link to article].

LOUISIANA

Parish Council Tables Cell Tower, Advances Opioid Initiatives: “Residents raised objections involving setbacks, safety, potential storm damage, property impacts and whether additional safeguards or even a moratorium on future solar and similar large-scale land use projects should be considered.” [The Daily News, 4/22/2026, link to article]

Northern Rockies

WYOMING

NextEra inks a transmission interconnection agreement for an unspecified 200 MW of wind power under development in western Wyoming: “Months after cancelling its massive 600-megawatt, $729 million Jackalope wind energy project in Sweetwater County, NextEra Energy Resources is advancing a handful of other renewable energy projects in the state…The company recently inked an agreement that secures access to Black Hills Energy’s electric transmission system for 200 megawatts of “wind power” generation still under development on the state’s east side, according to a federal filing…The agreement, which reserves space on Black Hill’s Pumpkin Buttes-Windstar 230 kilovolt line, doesn’t specify a particular NextEra project. However, the Florida-based company operates wind farms in Laramie and Converse counties — both with the potential for expansion. NextEra is also advancing a wind, solar and battery storage proposal in Platte County dubbed the Chugwater Energy Project…PacifiCorp — parent company to Rocky Mountain Power, Wyoming’s largest electric utility — recently wiped new wind and solar from its future plans in the state.” [WyoFile, 4/21/2026, link to article]

CO/NM/NV

COLORADO

Delta County commissioners extend battery storage moratorium six more months: “Delta County commissioners voted unanimously to extend a temporary moratorium on battery energy storage system applications through October 21, giving the county another six months to finalize regulations currently being drafted with the Delta County Planning Commission…Chairman Lane said the county can expect a more heavily attended public hearing when the draft regulations themselves come up for formal consideration later this year.” [Delta County Independent, 4/22/2026, link to article]

AZ/UT

UTAH

19 cities across Utah could be on clean energy sources, if their councils pass an ordinance: “Environmental groups are rallying people in 19 communities across Utah to contact their town and city councils, urging them to pass an ordinance saying they want to be on 100% net renewable energy…The Public Service Commission, the entity that regulates electricity rates in Utah, approved the Community Clean Energy Program last month. But cities across the state that signed up to participate years ago must pass an ordinance by June 2 indicating they're still in…The program has the communities partner with Rocky Mountain Power to ensure that the electricity they take from the grid comes from renewable energy sources like solar and wind by 2030. The 19 communities represent as much as 25% of Rocky Mountain Power's electricity in Utah…[C]ouncils across the state will need to make the deadline. Some will consider the ordinance in the next week or so. HEAL Utah and other groups are urging people to reach out to their elected officials and say they want it.” [Fox 13 Salt Lake City, 4/20/2026, link to article]

Northeast

MASSACHUSETTS

Judge blocks GE Vernova exit from Vineyard “A Massachusetts judge has blocked GE Vernova from abandoning work on the 806MW Vineyard Wind offshore project off the US north-east coast. Suffolk County Superior Court Judge Peter Krupp issued a preliminary injunction preventing a GE Vernova unit from terminating its contract and ceasing work. Krupp wrote that the project ‘is at a critical phase and the loss of VW's principal contractor would set the project back immeasurably and threaten VW's financing.”’ [Renews.biz, 4/20/26, link to article]

Mid-Atlantic

VIRGINIA

Solar lawyer on HB 711 and solar developers: It’s still crucial to not lose “The contrast between high hopes of developers supporting bills like HB 711 and the actual realities of the bill’s language highlights an important fact: you want to win at the county level, because your special use permit hearing and vote is both your first chance and, almost certainly, your last chance to get the project approved… If enacted, HB711 would foreclose counties from maintaining zoning ordinance provisions that create actual or ‘de facto’ moratoriums on solar.”  [Cardinal News, 4/21/26, link to article]

Located in East Tennessee, the Bobwhite project is expected to begin construction in 2027 and reach commercial operation by late 2029.” [pv magazine, 4/21/26, link to article]

Midwest

INDIANA

Tippecanoe County solar ordinance vote moved up to May 4 “A final vote for the proposed Tippecanoe County solar ordinance will come sooner than previously expected, county commissioners said during a meeting Monday. The ordinance, which received a majority vote of approval from the Tippecanoe County Area Plan Commission on April 15, was scheduled to be heard by county commissioners during their May 18 meeting.” [Lafayette Journal & Courier, 4/20/26, link to article]

Marshall County approves permanent ban on data centers, effective immediately “Commissioners also advanced a first reading on regulations for large-scale solar farms, proposing a cap of five acres of solar panel area… The solar farm ordinance will be voted on at the next commissioners' meeting.” [WSBT 22, 4/20/26, link to article] (more on this here)

MICHIGAN

Solar rep booed as Michigan township considers proposal to add 1k acres of panels “Residents argue sufficient area farmland already is dedicated to green energy. They are suspicious of the power source and the applicant. One resident referred to the company as “Ranger danger” and representatives received a largely chilly reception at a public hearing Thursday, April 16, that will continue next month.” [MLive, 4/21/26, link to article]

Ingersoll Township Planning Commission Denies DTE Solar Park Permit in 6-1 Vote “In a 6-1 vote shortly after 1 a.m. Tuesday, April 21, the commission determined that DTE’s Poseyville Solar Park proposal did not meet the standards required under the township’s ordinance for a special use permit…The meeting began at 7:30 p.m. Monday, April 20, at Messiah Church and lasted about 5 1/2 hours…More than 100 people attended, and the Midland County Sheriff’s Office was present because of heightened tensions surrounding the proposal.” [Midland Daily News, 4/21/26, link to article]

South-Central

Kansas county weighs moratorium on solar development “The Jackson County, Kansas Planning Commission met at the county courthouse to deliberate on a recommendation for a formal moratorium of large-scale solar development. If approved by the Board of County Commissioners, the pause would effectively freeze new solar project applications while officials finalize a permanent regulatory framework. The process that has remained in legislative flux since December 2024. The move follows years of escalating debate over the Jeffrey Solar project, a 5,000-acre, 500 MW installation proposed by NextEra Energy Resources.” [pv magazine, 4/10/26, link to article]

MASSACHUSETTS

As the state tries to streamline battery and solar permitting, some residents wary of projects “In March, the state rolled out new regulations to implement a 2024 climate bill that favors renewable energy. The new rules, which go into effect in July, are designed to speed approval of energy projects – including solar and battery storage. Larger projects bypass local permitting entirely and go to the state’s Energy Facilities Siting Board for approval.” [Yahoo!News, 4/19/26, link to article]

McKean County commissioners seek public input at hearing on draft solar ordinance “The McKean County Board of Commissioners will hold a public hearing at 6 p.m. Monday to consider an addendum to the county’s Subdivision and Land Development Ordinance regulating solar energy facilities…Steven Simcox and Brian Bischoff, residents who have been instrumental in the development of protective wind ordinances for Norwich Township and the county, weighed in on the draft Friday… ‘Nobody here is ‘against solar.’ What people are against is irresponsible, poorly sited industrial projects that shift the risk onto the community while outside corporations take the profit.”’ [The Bradford Era, 4/17/2026, link to article]

ILLINOIS

What’s next in Will County solar development “The Will County Board this week showed the strain of local governments being forced by the state to approve solar energy projects regardless of whether they are wanted to or not. Board Speaker Joe VanDuyne, D-Wilmington, proclaimed “our hands are completely tied” as he urged the board on Thursday to approve six solar projects that they had previously rejected…Of the 38 people who spoke to the board during a public comment period, 27 urged approval for solar projects on the agenda.”  [Shaw Local, 4/18/2026, link to article]

Council revisiting postponed solar ordinance “Davenport City Council is again considering how to regulate solar energy projects in the city. A moratorium on new utility-scale and free-standing solar projects in Davenport is expiring in June. In preparation, the city council is revisiting a more restrictive solar ordinance that council members decided to postpone in December to give more time for a new west Davenport land-use study to be done.” [The Dispatch-Argus, 4/19/26, link to article]

NextEra Energy cancels Rice County wind farm project “LYONS, Kan. — A proposed wind farm project in Rice County has been canceled by NextEra Energy, ending a development effort that had been in the works for roughly two years. The Florida-based energy company confirmed Friday it is no longer pursuing the project, citing a recently enacted county moratorium on wind and solar developments as a key factor in its decision.”  [Hutch Post, 4/20/26, link to article]

Landowner reacts to pause in proposed Rice County wind farm project “Rice County landowners are celebrating after learning efforts to bring a wind farm to the county have been put to a stop, though they also worry this is just one battle they've won in a potential war to come… Zimmerman worries the project will continue as soon as the moratorium is lifted. He said it's important his and other Kansas counties fighting against data centers and similar projects keep up the fight.”  [Kake News, 4/18/26, link to article]

Northeast

NEW YORK

'It's absurd.' Huntington residents react as officials mull moratorium on battery storage facilities “The Town of Huntington is moving forward in its decision-making process on the future of battery energy storage facilities, as officials have scheduled a public hearing on a proposed six-month moratorium. The hearing, set for May 5, comes as two battery storage system projects are under consideration in the town, drawing mixed reactions from residents.” [News12, 4/15/26, link to article]

Gainesville extends solar, battery moratorium “The Gainesville Town Board voted Monday evening to extend its moratorium on new solar energy and battery storage facilities. The unanimous vote adds six months to a moratorium that had been in place for six months previously, while members of the town planning board work to draft a permanent law regulating the facilities. While details of the permanent law aren’t yet clear, Town Supervisor David Granger said Wednesday that the planning board is reviewing several other towns’ laws, as well as an example law provided by Wyoming County.” [The Daily News, 4/16/26, link to article]

Paused for Policy: Andes Adopts Utility-Scale Solar, Battery Energy Storage Moratorium “A proposal to temporarily halt large-scale solar and battery storage development drew impassioned testimony Tuesday, April 14, as Andes officials held a public hearing before unanimously…[remaining article text is behind paywall].” [The Reporter, 4/15/26, link to article]

Midwest

ILLINOIS

Yes, Utica can extend moratorium on solar, wind farms “Springfield adopted a law that prevents municipalities from halting green-energy projects. A lawyer for Utica said Monday it doesn’t apply to the village. As previously reported, the Utica Village Board voted 5-0 (Trustee Pete Pawlak was absent) on Thursday, April 9, to extend its moratorium on issuing permits for solar energy and wind farm systems inside village limits. Village officials extended the moratorium, which does not apply to residential solar panels, to see what new regulations come out of the current legislative session.” [Shaw Local, 4/16/26, link to article]

INDIANA

Sparked by AES battery development, residents ask Huntingburg for development moratorium “Huntingburg resident Amanda Souders Harris urged the common council to enact a temporary moratorium on new development within the city’s extraterritorial jurisdiction (ETJ) until the Unified Development Ordinance can be updated to address large-scale projects that current regulations weren’t designed to handle… The request comes as the city grapples with an ongoing solar project by AES that includes a battery energy storage system. Part of the project is located within the city’s extraterritorial jurisdiction, but not the batteries… City Attorney Phil Schneider explained that moratoriums require going through the UDO amendment process, including referral to the planning commission, public hearings and passing an ordinance. State law limits moratoriums to one year, and the city would need to hire consultants to draft development plans or restrictions during that time.” [Dubois County Free Press, 4/15/26, link to article]

Northeast

NEW YORK

Town of Clay to hold public hearing on battery storage law “The Town of Clay will hold a public hearing Monday evening on a proposed local law regulating battery energy storage systems. The hearing is scheduled for 7:38 p.m. at Clay Town Hall and will focus on Local Law No. 4 of 2026, which would establish a new section of town code governing the placement and use of battery storage facilities.” [CNY Central, 4/15/26, link to article]

Glen Cove City Council approves one-year extension for battery energy storage system moratorium “The Glen Cove City Council approved an additional 12-month moratorium on battery energy storage systems and promoted two police officers at its Tuesday, April 14, meeting. The council unanimously approved a ban on any application for a battery energy storage system, which the city defines as a facility that holds electrochemical devices that charge or collect energy from a power grid and then discharge that energy to parcels, facilities, dwellings, or utilities serviced by that grid.” [PoliticsNY, 4/15/26, link to article]

Coxsackie considers battery storage facility ban “The Coxsackie Board of Trustees proposes a ban on industrial battery storage facilities citing fire risks and toxic fumes.” [Daily Gazette, 4/14/26, link to article]

Nothing to see here, folks!

Nothing to see here, folks!

Southeast

GEORGIA

Georgia Power will now let data centers bring their own clean energy “After years of negotiations, data centers and other large customers of Georgia Power have finally won a pathway to pay for their own new clean energy projects to be built and connected to the utility’s grid. The Georgia Public Service Commission approved the utility’s program last week, allowing these companies to identify and commit to paying for solar, battery, and other renewable-energy projects to supply their own power needs.” [Canary Media, 4/15/26, link to article]

Midwest

INDIANA

Solar regulation showdown “In 2021, the county approved zoning requirements – including those for decommissioning, setbacks and others for agriculturally zoned land – for solar projects of 10 acres or more. Commissioners since have questioned whether those requirements contemplated projects that were more than 100 times bigger. In June 2025, county commissioners imposed a one-year moratorium on large-scale solar proposals to allow a review of zoning codes. That moratorium ends June 2… The Area Plan Commission will consider recommendations for rewritten zoning codes for solar energy systems at 6 p.m. Wednesday, April 15, at the County Office Building, 20 N. Third St. in Lafayette. (The discussion will follow an agenda of other subdivision and rezoning requests.) Recommendations from the APC would go to the county commissioners for a final vote as soon as May 4.” [Based in Lafayette, 4/14/26, link to article]

Pulaski County Advisory Plan Commission to hold public hearing on solar moratorium “The Pulaski County Advisory Plan Commission has announced that it will be holding a special session on April 20th at 8:15am Eastern Time. This session will include a public hearing in regards to a proposed ordinance for a moratorium on permits for Commercial Solar Energy Systems within Pulaski County.” [WKVI, 4/15/26, link to article]

IOWA

Davenport Leaders Updated on Solar Farm Ordinance After Almost Year-Long Pause “The city council will discuss the proposals in order to make a decision in June for a possible new updated solar ordinance.” [6 KQWC, 4/14/26, link to article]

South-Central

TEXAS

Texas sharpens attacks on solar power “Texas lawmakers and regulators have unleashed blistering new attacks against solar this month, setting up a showdown that could curb renewable energy growth. State senators grilled solar advocates over whether Chinese-made solar panels could give the Communist Party of China the ability to meddle with Texas’s isolated electric grid. The Public Utility Commission of Texas sent a solar and battery project back to a state administrative court, telling El Paso Electric it should consider natural gas or other traditional generation. And state Attorney General Ken Paxton (R) said his office filed civil investigative demands with four companies as part of a new initiative to investigate “widespread” fraud among rooftop solar companies.” [E&E News, 4/13/26, link to article]

Plains

NEBRASKA

Commissioners Extend Solar Farm, Wind Turbine, and Nuclear Energy Moratorium “[Kearney] County Attorney Melodie Bellamy asked the board to extend the solar farm, wind turbine, and nuclear energy moratorium. The County Planning and Zoning Commis-sion has been working on establishing regulations for these alternative energy sources, but it is a bigger project than originally anticipated. After an initial meeting to gather information and a public hearing to gather public input, Planning and Zoning met on April 1 to discuss proposed regulations. The meeting was lengthy as the board worked to create regulations in the best interest of all. They will meet again to consider the regulations with all proposed changes before the regulations are presented to the commis-sioners for final approval. The board adopted a resolution to extend the moratorium for six months.” [Minden Courier, 4/14/26, link to article]

Pacific Northwest

WASHINGTON

Yakima County commissioners approve new solar ordinance “More solar panels could be coming to Yakima County after commissioners voted on Tuesday to approve an ordinance allowing medium and large-scale solar facilities on some farmland. The decision ends a solar moratorium that has been in place since 2022 in unincorporated Yakima County and has been extended multiple times as the planning department has navigated a complex legal landscape.” [Yakima Herald-Republica, 4/15/16, link to article, link thru paywall]

Understanding the acronyms in Lee’s May 18 town warrant: Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS) and Adaptive Reuse Overlay District (AROD) “When local voters take to the polls next month, they will be faced with two zoning proposals that could change Lee’s skyline in the near future. With that in mind, the Lee Planning Board scheduled two public hearings at Town Hall on April 27, beginning at 6:15 p.m. with a discussion of slated changes to the town’s Adaptive Reuse Overlay District (AROD) followed by a 6:45 p.m. session to address a possible temporary moratorium on the construction of commercial Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS).” [Berkshire Edge, 4/13/26, link to article]

OHIO

This Ohio county put a ban on wind and solar. Will voters reverse it? “Richland County blocked large-scale renewables in most townships. Voters now have a rare opportunity to overturn that policy at the ballot box.” [Canary Media, 4/13/26, link to article]

Southeast

ARKANSAS

Earle residents seek moratorium on proposed wind turbine project “A number of Earle residents are seeking a moratorium against the placing of wind turbines in that area. The Crittenden County Quorum Court is currently in negotiations with a wind turbine company to place some 40 wind turbines on rural farm land in the Earle area. The residents contend that while quorum court meetings are open to the public, they are not publicized as to what is on the agenda and that members of the quorum court are rushing to a decision.” [Wynne Progress, 4/10/26, link to article]

Midwest

ILLINOIS

Will County Loses Power To Vote Against Solar Farms “Despite Will County’s vote on April 8th to have more control over solar farm proposals, WJOL has learned that 12th District Associate Judge Ben Braun ruled the County Board should not have turned down past requests. The lawsuits were filed against the county by Channahon McKinley Woods, RPIL Solar 13, Florence Renewables LLC, NL Gougar Solar 1, Black Road Solar 1 and Black Road Solar 2. The county is ordered to comply by April 17, 2026. County Board leaders will have to put them on the agenda for its April 16 meeting. Also slated for the meeting are applications for solar farms from Earthrise Energy: Plum Creek, which is 2,400 acres, and Pride of the Prairie, which is nearly 6,100 acres. As of June 1, 2026, new Illinois state law (Public Act 102-1123, or the “Wind and Solar Facility Law”) prevents county boards from blocking commercial solar farms that meet state standards, limiting local zoning oversight.” [WJOL, 4/9/26, link to article, additional reporting]

MICHIGAN

Developer behind proposed solar farm in Zeeland, Jamestown Twps, moves forward with state application “RWE, the developer behind the Silver Maple Solar project, filed its application with the Michigan Public Service Commission (MPSC) on April 3.” [Fox 17, 4/9/26, link to article]

Kalamazoo area communities work to address potential large-scale energy storage projects in the region “Four communities across the Kalamazoo area are working to address potential large-scale energy projects through ordinances, moratoriums, and public hearings. Richland Township, Comstock Township, the City of Portage, and Oshtemo Township are facing the issue head-on as Michigan works toward a goal of half of all energy being renewable by 2030.” [Fox 17, 4/10/26, link to article, additional reporting]

Iowa considers standardizing renewable energy standards “Iowa lawmakers are considering a bill that would standardize renewable-energy infrastructure installations. Driven largely by landowner pushback, a third of Iowa's counties currently have regulations that prohibit new wind and solar projects, despite proven economic benefits.” [KMA Land, 4/11/26, link to article]

South-Central

KANSAS

Kansas county weighs moratorium on solar development “The Jackson County, Kansas Planning Commission met at the county courthouse to deliberate on a recommendation for a formal moratorium of large-scale solar development. If approved by the Board of County Commissioners, the pause would effectively freeze new solar project applications while officials finalize a permanent regulatory framework. The process that has remained in legislative flux since December 2024. The move follows years of escalating debate over the Jeffrey Solar project, a 5,000-acre, 500 MW installation proposed by NextEra Energy Resources.” [PV Magazine, 4/10/26, link to article]

Wyoming electric utility dumps wind and solar in long-term planning “Rocky Mountain Power’s parent company, PacifiCorp, updated its long-range planning document in March, shifting its previous upward trajectory for new wind and solar throughout its six-state service region to flatline status. The electric utility has no plans to add more wind or solar facilities in Wyoming, Utah, Idaho and California from 2027 through 2045. ‘Changes in this update are largely driven by the July 4, 2025, repeal of major portions of the Inflation Reduction Act.’” [WyoFile, 4/13/26, link to article]

Southeast

Nothing to see here, folks

Midwest

MICHIGAN

Oshtemo Township hears from experts on BESS ordinance options under state law: Oshtemo Township (Kalamazoo County) held a meeting with experts from EGLE, MPSC and the University of Michigan to discuss drafting a local ordinance for battery energy storage systems (BESS) under Michigan's Public Act 233. A proposed BESS by NewEdge Renewable Power near Van Kal Avenue on the Oshtemo-Almena border triggered a one-year moratorium (approved end of 2025 and potentially extendable). All seven township officials and over 350 families oppose the project; more than 1,500 residents are in the "Say No to Oshtemo BESS" Facebook group with over 1,000 petition signatures. Resident Katie Schneider stated, "I'm a business owner, a farmer and a parent here in Oshtemo Township. I can tell you there are over 350 families with signs in their yard against this project." Experts noted fire/explosion risk but said lithium-ion batteries are "getting increasingly safe without injury" and that a "workable ordinance" must sit in the "sweet spot" above the state minimum to prevent developers from bypassing to the MPSC. No vote was taken; the planning commission will continue drafting with multiple future public-comment meetings. [WWMT, April 9 2026, link to article]

North-Central

Nothing to see here, folks

South-Central

ARKANSAS

Proposed wind turbines causing a storm in rural AR: A proposed 94-turbine wind farm in Crittenden County has sparked strong resident opposition ahead of a possible final vote this month on the first phase. The project would bring an estimated $26 million over 15 years, mostly to school districts like Earle, but residents cite lack of notice, property-value impacts, noise, fire-safety concerns (local departments lack equipment for turbine fires), and long-term effects over 30–40 years. Resident Carrie Fraley said, "These are rights that we have where we live, where we're raising our family, where we want to retire, and I'm concerned about the property value for everyone out here." Volunteer firefighter Scott Fraley added, "The big issue is… our little fire department does not have the means to barely put out a grass fire." Residents are circulating a petition for a moratorium and independent study; the Quorum Court is hiring a consultant on setbacks and impacts. The developer's attorney noted the study towers have operated for 10 years with "no incidents." [WREG, April 9 2026, link to article]

KANSAS

Solar project moratorium may be coming in Jackson County: Jackson County leaders are considering a moratorium on solar projects after years of discussion around NextEra Energy Resources' proposed 500 MW Jeffrey Solar project west of Holton that could use up to 2,000 acres. The project is projected to generate $136 million in new tax revenue over its lifespan, including $24.5 million in the first decade. NextEra representative Laura Gies stated, "Jeffrey Solar will contribute approximately $24.5 million to the area in the first decade of operations and is projected to generate a total of $136 million in new tax revenue over its lifespan. This funding can help support schools, roads and other vital public services." The Jackson County Planning Commission was scheduled to discuss and possibly recommend the moratorium at a public meeting on April 9, 2026 (7–8 p.m. at Memorial Hall in Holton). [KSNT, April 9 2026, link to article]

California

CALIFORNIA

Tiny Boulevard Town Goes Big, Demands $7M And Safety Overhaul From Starlight Solar: The tiny town of Boulevard (San Diego County, pop. ~400) is demanding a $7 million community benefits agreement and major safety changes from Starlight Solar's proposed project on 588 acres along Jewel Valley Road. The development would pair up to 100 MW of solar with a 217.4 MW battery energy storage system. Residents and neighborhood groups want the battery component moved farther back, a second evacuation route, detailed fire plans, water-use assurances, larger setbacks, and funding to refurbish the Backcountry Resource Center and build a playground. They cite precedent from the Jacumba Valley Ranch project ($4 million community fund). The project faces county Planning Commission review on May 8; a town hall is scheduled for April 16. [Hoodline, April 9 2026, link to article]



Daily Template 4.10.26

To:

staff@greenlightamerica.org

Subject

Daily Clean Energy Clips 4/10/26


Northeast

Nothing to see here, folks!

Mid-Atlantic

KENTUCKY

TVA CEO Don Moul to retire July 1: Tennessee Valley Authority CEO Don Moul, who has served as TVA's fourth CEO since April 9, 2025, plans to retire July 1, informing the board of his decision on April 3. TVA is the nation's largest public utility and supplies power to the Glasgow Electric Plant Board. "The Board appreciates Don's service to TVA, its employees and the people of the Tennessee Valley region," Director Mitch Graves said. "Under his leadership, TVA has had strong operational and financial performance delivering reliable, affordable, American energy that helps communities across our seven states prosper." Moul's decision comes after President Donald Trump publicly criticized him and vowed to make his life "miserable." Trump has appointed four of the six board members who will pick the next CEO. In 2025, Moul drew roughly $6 million in direct compensation, but Trump is pressing the board to cap CEO pay at $500,000. [Glasgow News 1, Apr 9 2026, link to article]

Southeast

ALABAMA

Solar power company appeals to south Alabama residents: 'We double crop': About 200 people attended a town hall Wednesday night at the civic center in Bay Minette where Silicon Ranch representatives addressed concerns about the Nashville-based company's proposed 4,500-acre solar farm in Stockton, with tensions boiling over late in the meeting into a verbal altercation that resulted in one woman being escorted out. The solar panels are expected to generate 260 megawatts and be operational by the end of 2028, and Silicon Ranch has a power purchase agreement with Alabama Power to offset energy demand from Meta's forthcoming $1.5 billion data center in Montgomery. "Your bill is not going up because of solar," said Reagan Farr, CEO of Silicon Ranch. "We're not sneaking around." Silicon Ranch officials said 2,500 acres of the site — largely wetlands and bordering areas — will be conserved, while solar panels on the remaining 2,000 acres will host a flock of sheep. "We double crop," Farr said. "We harvest sunlight to produce energy, and we sell grass-fed meat…It basically runs as an agricultural operation after it's built." About 75 residents protested outside the civic center before the event, joined by former U.S. Rep. Jerry Carl, who is running for Alabama's first congressional district. The article also notes developers are looking to build a nearly 3,000-acre solar farm further north in Baldwin County by Tensaw Solar, LLC. [AL.com via Yahoo News, Apr 9 2026, link to article]

GEORGIA

Heelstone Renewable starts construction on 206MW US solar projects: Heelstone Renewable Energy, a Qualitas Energy company, has started construction on two solar photovoltaic projects in the US with a combined capacity of 206MW, supported by long-term corporate power purchase agreements with a US hyperscale data centre developer. The 104MW Alligator Creek Solar facility is located in Wheeler County, Georgia, and the 102MW Murch Solar project is in Van Buren County, Michigan. Heelstone expects both plants to reach commercial operation by the end of 2026. Alligator Creek reached financial close in December 2025 and Murch closed in March 2026. Heelstone appointed Pure Power Contractors for Alligator Creek and Greensol Renewables for Murch. "Backed by long-term PPAs and fully structured project financing, these projects reflect our ability to originate, finance and build high-quality utility-scale renewable assets," said Heelstone Renewable Energy CEO Mike Weich. "The start of construction on Alligator Creek and Murch marks another important step in scaling our platform across key US markets." The milestones follow Heelstone's recent completion of a $200m senior secured corporate credit facility. [Power Technology via Yahoo Finance, Apr 8 2026, link to article]

Midwest

MICHIGAN

Heelstone Renewable starts construction on 206MW US solar projects: Heelstone Renewable Energy, a Qualitas Energy company, has started construction on two solar photovoltaic projects in the US with a combined capacity of 206MW, supported by long-term corporate power purchase agreements with a US hyperscale data centre developer. The 104MW Alligator Creek Solar facility is located in Wheeler County, Georgia, and the 102MW Murch Solar project is in Van Buren County, Michigan. Heelstone expects both plants to reach commercial operation by the end of 2026. Alligator Creek reached financial close in December 2025 and Murch closed in March 2026. Heelstone appointed Pure Power Contractors for Alligator Creek and Greensol Renewables for Murch. "Backed by long-term PPAs and fully structured project financing, these projects reflect our ability to originate, finance and build high-quality utility-scale renewable assets," said Heelstone Renewable Energy CEO Mike Weich. "The start of construction on Alligator Creek and Murch marks another important step in scaling our platform across key US markets." The milestones follow Heelstone's recent completion of a $200m senior secured corporate credit facility. [Power Technology via Yahoo Finance, Apr 8 2026, link to article]

North-Central

MINNESOTA

Xcel Minnesota is building a first-of-its-kind virtual power plant: The Minnesota Public Utilities Commission last week approved Xcel Energy's plan to become the first utility in the nation to build and operate its own virtual power plant. Under the new program, known as Capacity*Connect, Xcel will spend up to $430 million to deploy up to 200 megawatts of batteries, in 1-megawatt to 3-megawatt increments, over the next two years. Almost every other virtual power plant program in the U.S. is organized around third-party companies — like solar and battery vendors or specialized "aggregators" — that tap into energy resources installed and owned by customers. Clean energy advocates, solar industry groups, and state agencies had raised concerns that Xcel's utility-owned model would crowd out competition. Shannon Anderson of Solar United Neighbors said: "The Minnesota story is part of a national trend… There are a number of filings coming, a lot of paperwork to evaluate and justify the program. This is just the beginning of the conversation from our perspective." [Canary Media, Apr 8 2026, link to article]

South-Central

Pacific Northwest

ID/MT/WY

Nothing to see here, folks!

CO/NM/NV

Nothing to see here, folks!

AZ/UT

Nothing to see here, folks!

California

AES pulls out of San Diego area battery project after local opposition: AES Corp. withdrew its application to develop the Seguro battery system in Escondido, 30 miles from San Diego, after a barrage of local opposition. The company had intended to fill a former horse ranch with 320 megawatts of battery containers, which would have been one of the most powerful stand-alone energy storage facilities in the country and would have strengthened the Southern California grid late in the day when solar generation fades and home consumption surges. Residents decried adding large-scale power infrastructure so close to homes and 1,600 feet from Palomar Medical Center Escondido, especially given the multiday fires at other large batteries in the state. AES needed the hospital to sign off on running high-voltage wires through its property, which the hospital board voted down in 2024. AES said in a statement it would prioritize other development efforts but remained "committed to advancing projects that can provide the safe, reliable, and affordable power needed to strengthen the region's electric grid and generate meaningful economic benefits locally." JP Theberge, a board member of the Elfin Forest Harmony Grove Town Council who rallied resistance to the battery, framed his neighbors as a victorious David smiting a corporate Goliath. "The forces of greed are very powerful in this world. The only way to stop them is to be united and determined and forceful," he wrote in a post on the Stop Seguro Facebook group. "This was a great win and we should all be proud of our efforts." [Canary Media, Apr 9 2026, link to article]



Daily Template 4.9.26

To:

staff@greenlightamerica.org

Subject

Daily Clean Energy Clips 4/9/26

Northeast

NEW YORK

New York State clears Cordelio's plan for 300-MW solar project: New York's Office of Renewable Energy Siting and Electric Transmission has issued a final siting permit to Cordelio Power for the Flat Creek Solar Project, an up to 300 MW facility spanning roughly 1,000 acres in the Towns of Canajoharie and Root in Montgomery County. Cordelio plans to build the project in two phases — an initial 200 MW followed by another 100 MW — with startup targeted for late 2028 or early 2029. Flat Creek is expected to generate approximately $10 million in direct payments to the host towns over the first 20 years, $16.6 million to the Canajoharie Central School District, and $9.8 million to Montgomery County. ORES issued the Cordelio approval alongside a separate siting permit for Liberty Renewables' 99 MW Agricola wind project in Cayuga County. [Renewables Now, Apr 6 2026, link to article]

Mid-Atlantic

Nothing to see here, folks!

Southeast

ALABAMA

Solar farm opposition drives Stockton to historic zoning vote: The Baldwin County Commission on Tuesday certified a petition to put a zoning referendum before voters in unincorporated Stockton, likely on June 30, in response to outrage over Nashville-based Silicon Ranch's proposed $350 million, 2,000-acre solar farm near the community. The commission the same day rescinded a prior contract with Jacobs Engineering to oversee the solar farm application after residents raised concerns about the firm's ties to the solar industry. Organizers gathered 176 signatures within 24 hours — well above the roughly 124 required. "It's a citizen driven process," said Commissioner Jeb Ball, who represents the rural northern part of the county. Commissioner Matt McKenzie cautioned residents: "I want the people who are listening to be careful what you ask for. Once it gets zoned, it's a double edge sword." Meagan Fowler, representing the citizens group opposed to the project over concerns about runoff and damage to the Mobile-Tensaw Delta, said, "They do not want to become a town of industrial solar." [AL.com via Yahoo News, Apr 7 2026, link to article]

Silicon Ranch to meet with north Baldwin County residents over proposed solar farm: Silicon Ranch will meet with North Baldwin County residents at 6:30 p.m. Wednesday at the John F. Rhodes Civic Center in Bay Minette to discuss plans for its proposed large-scale solar farm near Stockton. The Baldwin County Commission on Tuesday passed a resolution requiring a special referendum to establish zoning in Planning District 3, and the probate court has 90 days to schedule the election. If voters reject the measure, current conditions remain; a yes vote triggers a 180-day moratorium on development within Planning District 3 while residents form a committee and work out zoning guidelines. The county has not yet received building applications from Silicon Ranch. [Fox10 / WALA, Apr 8 2026, link to article]

One-year moratorium on solar farms fails on Alabama Senate procedural rule: The Alabama Senate on Tuesday passed SB 354, sponsored by Sen. Greg Albritton, R-Atmore, which would prohibit new solar facilities in Mobile and Baldwin counties for one year, but failed to transmit it to the House because unanimous consent is required after the 26th legislative day and multiple senators objected. The bill passed 27-4 with two amendments — one narrowing the moratorium from statewide to just Mobile and Baldwin counties and requiring 30-day public hearing notice before construction, and another adding carbon dioxide storage facilities to the prohibition. The legislation was prompted by the controversy over Silicon Ranch's proposed 2,000-acre solar project on a 4,500-acre parcel in Stockton. "It's not stopping the industry. It is not restricting the industry. It is simply changing looking at a way to find another process that we could be more open," Albritton said. [Alabama Reflector via Yahoo News, Apr 8 2026, link to article]

Midwest

INDIANA

Knox County Commissioners Move Forward on Solar Ordinance Revisions, Form Advisory Committee: Knox County Commissioners voted 2-1 to send proposed amendments to the county's 2020 solar energy ordinance to the Area Plan Commission for review. The amendments would establish a 300-foot setback between commercial solar energy systems and any existing residence or principal structure, and would replace current screening rules with stricter standards requiring opaque fencing and vegetative buffers. Commissioners also unanimously formed a new 12-member Energy Land Use Advisory Committee to continue revising the solar ordinance and begin developing regulations for data centers, wind energy, and battery storage, none of which are currently covered under county ordinances. "I think the ordinance has got great bones, but very weak in meat and so we need to put meat to it," Commissioner TJ Brink said. [We Are Knox County (WAKC), Apr 7 2026, link to article]

Local News: Wednesday April 8th, 2026: Posey County commissioners declined to modify their solar farm ordinance after a final public hearing in which landowners and energy company representatives urged officials to reduce the current 500-foot setback from property lines to 300 feet, which solar supporters said is the industry standard. Commissioners said the current language represents a compromise between residents who support solar energy and those who oppose it. Some attendees argued stricter setbacks could discourage solar companies from investing in the area, but commissioners expressed confidence that interest in solar development will remain strong. The ordinance is scheduled for a final vote at the Area Plan Commission meeting the following day. [Posey County Radio, Apr 8 2026, link to article]

Commissioners vote not to change solar ordinance: Posey County, Indiana residents gave their final input at a county commissioners meeting on proposed solar farm ordinance language that would keep the 500-foot setback from property lines rather than reducing it to the 300 feet sought by solar developers. Companies including ENGIE disagreed with the current wording. "I think the way that the ordinance is currently drafted and being sent back to the Area Planning Commission without modifications to the solar portion, is an impediment to investment and economic development in the county," said Ryan Economy. Commissioner Greg Newman explained the commissioners' rationale: "I personally believe that the changes will not prevent solar from coming into the county. And that's my own personal belief. We just tried to find a balance between those that would be affected, that didn't want to participate in the project, and those landowners that did." [WEHT Eyewitness News via Yahoo News, Apr 8 2026, link to article]

MICHIGAN

Judge vacates permit for 810-acre Northern Waters Solar project in Cheboygan County: A judge has overturned Cheboygan County's approval of EDP Renewables' proposed 110 MW, 810-acre Northern Waters Solar Park in Grant Township, which would power the equivalent of more than 30,000 homes. Nineteen residents had asked the court to reverse the county's approval of the special use permit. Judge Gauthier ruled that because the application was for a special use permit, findings of fact on each section of the zoning ordinance must be made, even though the county argued its Compatible Renewable Energy Ordinance made large solar a permitted use by right. A few months after the original approval, the planning commission removed the CREO from its ordinance and placed a temporary moratorium on large-scale solar, but "the day after this court ruled in January, the board of commissioners rescinded that ordinance," the plaintiffs' attorney said. County Administrator Jeffrey Lawson said the county will respect the decision. "Just the community coming together to fight this. We were unwavering, relentless, and we knew that we were fighting for justice," said resident Sherry Long. [UpNorthLive, Apr 2026, link to article]

Oshtemo Township officials clarify status of energy systems ordinance and their process: The Oshtemo Township Planning Commission is continuing work Thursday on developing a zoning ordinance for battery energy storage system facilities, five months after the township board passed a one-year moratorium on such developments in November. The Planning Commission will hear from public sector and academic guest speakers, with additional scientific and technical experts expected to provide input on BESS safety provisions. In a news release Tuesday, township officials pushed back on public comments suggesting little work is happening on the ordinance or that it is being developed behind closed doors: "Those claims are not accurate. No ordinance drafting has occurred in closed session." NewEdge Renewable Power gave a presentation to the planning commission last August but no formal proposals have been submitted. A recall effort is currently targeting five board members, with organizers citing concerns over BESS safety and environmental risks. [WIN 98.5 / WKZO, Apr 7 2026, link to article]

North-Central

Nothing to see here, folks!

South-Central

KANSAS

Jackson Co Planning Commission to consider solar moratorium: The Jackson County Planning Commission is set to decide Thursday evening whether to recommend a temporary pause on large-scale solar development in the county. At a recent town hall, Commissioners Linda Gerhardt and Keith Kelly voiced opposition to large solar developments, while Commissioner Mark Pruett said he wouldn't support any project larger than 2,000 acres. County officials have been working on commercial solar regulations since December 2024. At the center of the debate is the proposed Jeffrey Solar project by NextEra Energy Resources, which would cover roughly 5,000 acres near Delia in southwestern Jackson County. [MSC News / KNZA, Apr 7 2026, link to article]

Plains

Nothing to see here, folks!

Pacific Northwest

Nothing to see here, folks!

ID/MT/WY

WYOMING

How much more wind energy might Wyoming see? It's complicated: A WyoFile analysis finds it's difficult to size up how much additional wind development is on the horizon for Wyoming, with no single clearinghouse tracking Wyoming-specific project information. The state currently has about 1,500 wind turbines with a generating capacity of approximately 3,700 MW, roughly 25% of total electrical generation capacity. Citing a court ruling, the Wyoming state lands board in February began invalidating two wind leases it had approved last year — one for the Pronghorn H2 Clean Energy Project in Converse County and one for the Sidewinder Clean Hydrogen Project in Niobrara County. Cheyenne area resident Wendy Volk recently helped launch a petition asking Wyoming officials to pause renewable energy permitting to conduct a "cumulative, landscape-level impact analysis." "We need to improve the process and transparency," Volk said. The piece also notes that entering election season, Republican U.S. House candidates including sitting Secretary of State Chuck Gray and Reid Rasner are campaigning on opposition to wind expansion. [WyoFile via News From The States, Apr 7 2026, link to article]

CO/NM/NV

AZ/UT

Daily Clean Energy Clips 4/8/26


Northeast

NEW YORK

Solar company submits wetlands permit application: York Run Solar, LLC has filed a joint application with the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers for a wetlands permit, including a water quality certification submission to the New York State Office of Renewable Energy Siting and Electric Transmission, as it advances toward construction of a 90-megawatt solar facility on approximately 550 acres across the towns of Busti and Kiantone in Chautauqua County. The project received its ORES siting permit in April 2025. Construction is complicated by the fact that both towns have active moratoriums on commercial solar, wind, and battery energy storage systems — extended in June 2025 through May 31, 2026 — though those moratoriums do not apply to projects already permitted through ORES. [Post-Journal / Observer Today, Apr 7 2026, link to article] [article text]

Mid-Atlantic

Nothing to see here, folks!

Southeast

Nothing to see here, folks!

Midwest

ILLINOIS

Will County commission says no in advisory vote on 6,100-acre solar complex: The Will County Board Planning and Zoning Commission voted 4-2 against Earthrise Energy’s Pride of the Prairie solar complex — a proposed 600+ MW facility spread across 6,100 acres on 96 farm properties in Manhattan, Green Garden, and Wilton townships — following a packed two-night public hearing that drew hundreds of residents. The vote is advisory; the project next went to the County Board Land Use and Development Committee, which on April 3 split 3-3 on Pride of the Prairie while advancing the company’s second project, Plum Valley Solar (2,250 acres), on a 4-2 vote. Both projects now head to the full county board for a final decision. “The problem we have with the Earthrise Pride of the Prairie is it’s just too darn big,” said Manhattan Township Supervisor James Walsh, who called it a “monster project.” Supporters included building trades unions, solar advocates, and farmers who stand to benefit from leasing their land. [Shaw Local / Herald-News, Apr 1 2026, link to article]

North-Central

MISSOURI

Greene County Commission rejects battery storage site plan: The Greene County Commission voted 2-0 on April 6 to deny Alabama-based Sofos Power’s request to rezone 40 acres at 4873 N. Rocky Lane — just north of Springfield — to develop the Summit Resilient Power Project, a lithium-ion battery energy storage system. The commission rejected the plan despite changes the developer had made to address concerns, including reducing battery containers from 242 to 222 and adding stormwater infrastructure and fire hydrants. The county’s fire chief testified that any incident at the facility would require a Region D emergency response, with hazmat assistance potentially coming from Kansas City, St. Louis, or Jefferson City — hours away. “If Springfield fire is out on two and a half house fires, their resources are tapped out as well,” said Nelson Prewitt, chief of the Ebenezer Fire Protection District. County staff had recommended approval with eight conditions; both commissioners present voted to deny, consistent with the unanimous recommendation of the Greene County Planning Board in February. [Springfield News-Leader, Apr 6 2026, link to article]

MINNESOTA

Public to weigh in as Morrison County considers pausing renewable energy projects: The Morrison County Board of Commissioners held a public hearing today on a proposed one-year moratorium on new solar gardens, solar farms, and wind energy conversion systems — with a vote to follow. The moratorium, if approved, would give the county time to update its existing renewable energy ordinance and would not affect small-scale personal systems or projects above certain megawatt thresholds that fall under the jurisdiction of Minnesota’s Public Utilities Commission. [Hometown Source / Morrison County Record, Apr 7 2026, link to article]
