Southeast

SOUTH CAROLINA

Proposed solar farm in Sumter County withdrawn amid community pushback: TOCE SC Solar One, a subsidiary of Texas-based Treaty Oak Clean Energy, has withdrawn its application with the South Carolina Public Service Commission to build and operate the White Palmetto Solar Project — a 170-megawatt, 1,700-acre solar facility proposed near the intersection of Borden and Black River roads, about 12 miles northwest of the city of Sumter. The withdrawal came on March 20, two days after a public hearing at which residents voiced concerns about the project’s impact on farmable land and what they described as the developer’s failure to act as a good neighbor. The project had already been denied a special exception by the Sumter County Zoning Board of Appeals in May 2025, with the company subsequently appealing to the state PSC over the county’s objection. “They kept trying to say that, you know, we want to be good neighbors. But all of their actions showed that they really didn’t,” said Tanya Eddins, who spoke at the March 18 hearing. State Sen. Jeff Zell said the episode is expected to prompt broader discussion about how solar projects are regulated across South Carolina. [WLTX, Apr 5 2026, link to article]

Midwest

MICHIGAN

Judge vacates permit for 810-acre Northern Waters Solar project in Cheboygan County: A judge has vacated the special use permit granted by the Cheboygan County Planning Commission for the Northern Waters Solar Park, a 110-megawatt, 810-acre solar facility proposed by EDP Renewables North America in Grant Township. The planning commission approved the permit in August 2025, but a group of 19 residents subsequently sued the county and EDP Renewables, arguing that the commission failed to make required findings of fact on each section of the zoning ordinance before granting approval. Judge Gauthier sided with plaintiffs, ruling that because the application was for a special use permit, the commission was required to address each applicable section — including sections 18 and 20 — even under a compatible renewable energy ordinance. The county had previously enacted a temporary moratorium on large-scale solar projects following an earlier court ruling, before the Board of Commissioners rescinded that ordinance. EDP Renewables had anticipated beginning construction in 2027 with power delivery to approximately 30,000 Michigan homes by 2028. [UpNorthLive / WPBN, Apr 2 2026, link to article]

INDIANA

Pulaski County Commissioners to meet April 6: The Pulaski County Commissioners are scheduled to vote on an extension to the county’s existing moratorium on battery energy storage systems at their April 6 meeting at the Pulaski County Courthouse in Winamac. [WKVI, Apr 3 2026, link to article]

Tippecanoe County continues debate on new solar farm ordinance: With a one-year moratorium on large-scale solar projects set to expire in June, the Tippecanoe County Area Plan Commission held its second of three public meetings on a proposed new 12-page solar ordinance. The most contested provisions include a 400-acre cap on individual projects, a 6,000-acre countywide maximum, setback distances from non-participating property lines, and a proposed outright ban on battery energy storage systems co-located with solar developments. The moratorium was triggered in part by the proposed 120-megawatt, 1,700-acre Rainbow Trout Solar Project in western Tippecanoe County, which was rejected by the Board of Zoning Appeals in August 2025 on a 4-3 vote and is now in litigation. The full Area Plan Commission is scheduled to hold a public hearing on the proposed ordinance on April 15, with a vote by county commissioners expected in May or June. [Star City TV, Apr 2 2026, link to article]

North-Central

Nothing to see here, folks!

South-Central

TEXAS

Why Doesn’t Texas, the Leader of Onshore Wind Energy, Have Any Offshore?: Inside Climate News reports that Texas state officials have led a sustained and largely successful effort to block offshore wind development in the Gulf of Mexico, even as the state generates more than a fifth of the country’s onshore wind power. The key barrier is political: the Texas General Land Office, whose approval is required for any offshore cable landing or substation, signaled to investors years ago that it would not grant such permits. When the Biden administration auctioned two Gulf lease sites off the Texas coast in 2023, neither attracted bids. Meanwhile, five offshore wind projects off the East Coast resumed construction this month after a federal judge blocked the Trump administration’s stop-work order — but Texas has none in the mix. Studies show the region’s offshore wind potential is comparable to its onshore success, and the state’s existing oil and gas workforce could transfer to the sector. “It signaled to investors that Texas was a risky place to invest,” said Stacy Ortego of the National Wildlife Federation. [Inside Climate News, Apr 2 2026, link to article]

ID/MT/WY

CO/NM/NV

Nothing to see here, folks!

AZ/UT

ARIZONA

Scoop: This GOP Lawmaker Is Aiming to Stop an Arizona Wind Farm: Republican Rep. Eli Crane is lobbying three separate federal agencies — the FAA, the Fish and Wildlife Service, and the FCC — to block Repsol’s Lava Run wind project, a proposed 500-megawatt wind farm in the White Mountains of Apache County, Arizona, according to audio from a late-March tele-town hall posted to social media. Crane told attendees he opposes the project and is doing everything he can to get Trump administration officials to intercede. The project is sited entirely on state and county land, which Crane acknowledged makes federal intervention challenging, but he noted Lava Run is likely to require federal permits for disturbing protected bird species — creating a potential avenue for the administration to block future construction. Apache County is also in the process of updating its renewable energy ordinance. Repsol is separately developing a 450-megawatt solar project on the same site. [Heatmap News, Apr 3 2026, link to article]

California

California just hit an inflection point for batteries: On March 29 at 7pm, California’s grid batteries discharged 12.3 gigawatts of power — roughly equivalent to six Hoover Dams, and approximately 43% of total grid demand at that moment — setting a new state record. Nearly all of that storage capacity was built in the last five years, driven by sharp cost declines: battery costs have fallen 99% over three decades, with roughly a third of that drop coming in just the past few years. The milestone reflects how battery storage has unlocked a structural shift in California’s grid, allowing midday solar surplus to be captured and discharged during evening peak hours, reducing reliance on gas peaker plants and imported power. California’s grid now carries 17 gigawatts of battery storage and is targeting more than 50 gigawatts. “There’s a huge, huge additional amount that’s still going to come online over the next few years,” said Nicolas Fulghum, senior energy and climate data analyst at Ember. [Fast Company, Apr 3 2026, link to article]

Whitman County should finish wind energy code “Wind facilities introduce additional ignition risks, and without strict, enforceable fire prevention and response standards, the consequences could be severe for farms, homes, and rural communities…Strengthening the code now will not stop or prohibit wind development, it will ensure it is done responsibly, with proper oversight and safeguards in place.” [Whitman County Gazette, 4/2/2026, link to article]

Letter: County should take action on Crossvine Solar permit “I am concerned that projects of this scale are moving forward without adequate transparency, safety review, or meaningful public input. Battery energy storage systems carry documented risks, including fire hazards and emergency response challenges…” [Whitman County Gazette, 4/2/2026, link to article]

MARYLAND

Solar farm near Berlin moving forward – OC Today-Dispatch “A solar farm on the outskirts of Berlin that drew opposition from the Worcester County Commissioners is advancing anyway under state authority that overrides local zoning. Arizona-based developer TurningPoint Solar is scheduled to meet next Wednesday with the county’s Technical Review Committee for a major site plan review. If approved, the proposal would next be heard by the county’s planning commission for a favorable or unfavorable vote, followed by a public hearing.” [Solar Now, 4/3/2026, link to article]

GEORGIA

Georgia Power begins construction on new battery energy storage facility in Jefferson County “The new Wadley BESS will help strengthen the grid and the area’s growing renewable energy resources… ‘On behalf of the Jefferson County community, we welcome this Georgia Power project,’ Mitchell McGraw, chairman of the Jefferson County Board of Commissioners, said. ‘We’re so proud to have your investment in Jefferson County, and we hope for more in the future.”’ [Daily Energy Insider, 4/2/2026, link to article]

MICHIGAN

Judge vacates permit for 810-acre solar farm project in Cheboygan County “A months-long legal battle in Cheboygan County over a proposed 800-acre solar farm has taken a major step forward after a judge overturned the project’s approval.Last week, the issue ended up in a courtroom. On one side, the county defending its approval of the solar farm, and the other, 19 concerned residents asking a judge to reverse that decision.” [UpNorthLive News, 4/2/2026, link to article]

Fayette Township Finalizes CREO Ahead Of Upcoming Hearing “The Fayette Township Board officially approved a new renewable energy ordinance during a brief special meeting on Wednesday night…The timing of the vote is important. A public hearing is scheduled for April 16th regarding the Heartwood II Solar Project.That project is a proposal to add 1,350 acres of solar panels in the township.” [Radio Hillsdale, 4/2/2026, link to article]

ILLINOIS

Public hearing in Joliet on Monday for 6,100-acre solar complex planned for Will County “Plans for a 6,100-acre solar complex scattered across three townships in Will County will be presented at a public hearing on Monday. The Pride of the Prairie Solar plan is one of two solar complexes covering about 8,500 acres that will be considered at two county meetings in the week ahead.” [Shaw Local, 3/28/2026, link to article]

Ameren tests dynamic line rating technology to boost transmission capacity on congested lines “DLR technology gives grid operators real-time insight into weather data and the precise operating conditions of transmission lines. With this information, operators can safely move electricity when and where it’s needed most. By utilizing potential capacity gains on some of the busiest transmission lines already in place, DLR helps strengthen reliability, improve safer operations and reduce costs.” {Daily Energy Insider, 4/3/2026, link to article]

INDIANA

‘A piece of Swiss cheese’: Work still to be done on Tippecanoe solar ordinance “A 6,000-acre limit and significant setback distance requirements were just two policies discussed during the second Tippecanoe County solar ordinance review meeting Wednesday evening… The April 15 APC meeting will serve as the next major landmark before an official proposal can be sent to the county commissioners.” [The Purdue Exponent, 4/2/2026, link to article, more on this here and here]

Northeast

Offshore wind energy more reliable, affordable for New England ratepayers, report finds: “[A] report, released by the Union of Concerned Scientists in February, found that offshore wind energy could have significantly lowered the risk of a blackout in the New England winter season. Expensive resources, like natural gas peaker plants, are typically deployed when the energy grid is stressed. Prices can spike rapidly and ratepayers “bear the burden.” Stephanie Francoeur, senior vice president of communications at the Oceantic Network, said Vineyard Wind and Revolution Wind could have saved over $800 per megawatt-hour during January’s winter storm.” [Concord Monitor, 3/29/2026, link to article]

Opinion: Winter proves New England can create a clean energy future “On the same day earlier this month, Vineyard Wind completed its 804MW project off Massachusetts and Revolution Wind off Rhode Island began producing power to light hundreds of thousands of homes in Rhode Island and Connecticut… Wind helped keep our lights burning this winter. During the heat wave that hit the region last summer, rooftop solar and batteries saved us from losing power…” [The New Boston Light, 3/30/2026, link to article]

MAINE

Maine tries again to unlock wind energy — but this time it has help “After years of false starts and amid an acute regional energy crunch, large-scale onshore wind power could finally take off in Maine in 2026. Utility regulators in five New England states are considering developers’ proposals to build up to 1.2 gigawatts of onshore wind capacity in Maine’s far north, following a deadline for bids earlier this month.” [Canary Media Light, 3/31/2026, link to article]

CONNECTICUT

Lamont visits East Windsor solar array, criticizes loss of open space “Gov. Ned Lamont criticized the loss of farmland and other open space for the construction of Connecticut’s largest solar array on Tuesday. The tour highlighted a tricky political question facing Lamont and other Democrats who are supportive of the state’s long-term climate goals: how to build clean, renewable sources of electricity without angering the people living alongside those projects.” [Hartford Business Journal, 4/2/2026, link to article]

Portage follows Michigan trend, puts halt on battery storage and data centers “Portage officials approved a pause on battery energy storage systems (BESS) and data center proposals during its meeting on March 24…It’s a preemptive move that several Michigan cities are pursuing as interest ramps up…The issue has sparked months-long debates in Comstock, Oshtemo and Pavilion townships.”  [MLive, 4/1/2026, link to article]

Tippecanoe County solar projects could be much smaller “Large-scale solar projects in Tippecanoe County would be a lot smaller than previously proposed, according to new language introduced in the county's solar ordinance amendment.Tippecanoe County commissioners have proposed a 400-acre cap on individual large-scale solar projects.The new proposal follows a halted 1,700-acre solar project and a subsequent lawsuit filed by the developers.”  [Journal & Courier, 4/1/2026, link to article]

Letter writer encourages Tippecanoe officials to embrace solar energy “the APC and other local leaders in Tippecanoe County should prioritize the well-being of the entire community by implementing a deployable solar ordinance. And although this ordinance is clearly a controversial topic in the community, I encourage them not to bend to the whims of a small population of angry residents who resist change, even if their voices are loud.” [Journal & Courier, 4/1/2026, link to article]

Battery storage developer officially exploring path to state approval for Watsonville facility, a move that could bypass local laws “The developer behind a proposed battery storage facility near Watsonville is now exploring a path to approval from the state’s energy commission…He added that the decision to submit a pre-application followed ‘a careful analysis’ of changes made to the county’s battery storage ordinance in January by the board of supervisors.” [Lookout Santa Cruz, 4/1/2026, link to article]

Texas and California lead the country in battery energy storage “Texas stands out as a focal point for recent battery development. In February, the state passed California to become the country’s leader in battery storage capacity, with 14,984 megawatts in Texas and 14,365 megawatts in California, based on an analysis of U.S. Energy Information Administration data. If this sounds familiar, it’s probably because Texas just passed California in 2025 to become the country’s leader in utility-scale solar.” [Inside Climate News, 4/2/2026, link to article]

The plan, a roughly 180 megawatt solar energy center in Harmony Township, has neighbors lining up on opposite sides: union supporters touting jobs and apprenticeships versus residents warning of lost farmland, drainage problems and traffic headaches.” [Hoodline, 3/31/2026, link to article]

The drafted ordinance will now move to the Quincy Township and Village boards for final consideration and potential adoption into the local zoning code.” [WTVB, 4/1/2026, link to article]

That 640-acre project will be built as part of a total of 2,000 acres of solar panels and battery storage in Jasper and Lawrence counties near La Russell to produce about 150 megawatts of electricity.” [Yahoo!New, 3/30/2026, link to article]

NEVADA

IOWN Energy closes sale of 127 MW Nevada battery project “IOWN Energy completed the sale of the Roccasecca project in Boulder City, Nevada, on behalf of its client, Eolus North America. The Roccasecca project has a 15-year tolling agreement in place. It is currently under construction and is expected to reach commercial operation in 2026. ” [PV Magazine, 4/1/2026, link to article]

Northeast

MASSACHUSETTS

Hearing on Worthington’s Solar Moratorium Bill April 8th “A state legislative hearing will be held on Wednesday, April 8, 2026, from 10 a.m. to noon at the Massachusetts State House, Room 222, to discuss Bill H5294, which would create a temporary moratorium on large-scale ground-mounted solar and battery storage projects in Worthington.” [Newsbreak, 03/31/2026, link to article]

Mid-Atlantic

Nothing to see here, folks!

Southeast

ALABAMA

Judge Rules Alabama Power Can Keep Its Solar Fee, Among the Nation’s Highest “Despite a sunny climate, Alabama ranks 49th among U.S. states in residential solar installations—lower than Alaska. Advocates say the steep solar fee is part of the reason why.A federal judge ruled last week that Alabama Power can continue charging its small solar customers one of the highest standby charges in the nation, dismissing a lawsuit that argued the fee was illegal under the Public Utility Regulatory Policies Act.” [Inside Climate News, 3/31/2026, link to article]

Midwest

ILLINOIS

Bill would require data centers in Illinois to invest in clean energy or pay more “A sweeping new proposal in Springfield would force data centers to disclose more about their water use, energy consumption, and environmental impact while tying them more directly into Illinois’ clean‑energy transition.Senate Bill 3830 creates a data center self‑direct program allowing operators to cut their renewable‑energy charges if they help bring new clean‑energy projects online. To qualify, data centers must sign long-term contracts with new wind, solar, or storage facilities and retire the environmental credits tied to them. Some facilities could offset up to 90% of mandated renewable‑energy costs” [Yahoo! News, 3/31/2026, link to article]

INDIANA

Solar Farm Ordinance Hits Home Stretch “Round 2 of public comment on proposed changes to Tippecanoe County’s large-scale solar energy systems is set for Wednesday afternoon, as sides line up and a deadline for zoning rules before a moratorium lifts at the start of June. The moratorium came after a proposal surfaced for a 1,700-acre, 120-megawatt Rainbow Trout Solar Project in western Tippecanoe County. That project was filed for required zoning exceptions before the moratorium went into effect. That project was rejected by the Board of Zoning Appeals on a 4-3 vote in August, amid pushback by neighbors and despite pleas from renewable energy advocates.” [Based in Lafayette, 3/31/2026, link to article]

MICHIGAN

Michigan approves 1,332MW of BESS with 332MW supporting Oracle data centre “The Michigan Public Service Commission has approved six battery energy storage system (BESS) projects totalling 1,332MW of capacity in the US state. The data centre-related BESS projects approved by the MPSC include the initial 332MW out of 1,383MW of company-owned energy storage facilities that Green Chile Ventures (Oracle) must develop to meet the data centre’s contractual demand. The total capacity of the battery storage projects approved for this data centre exceeds the capacity of DTE Electric’s 1,150MW Blue Water Energy Centre, Michigan’s latest natural gas-fired plant, which was approved by the MPSC in 2018.” [Energy Storage News, 3/31/2026, link to article]

AZ/UT

ARIZONA

Arizona cuts key renewables policy as clean energy market takes off “Sunny Arizona closed out 2025 as the second-biggest state for battery and solar construction. Now, a policy that helped kick-start this success could be going away. The Arizona Corporation Commission, the elected body that regulates utilities, unanimously voted in early March to eliminate the state’s renewable portfolio standard. The policy, which the commission set in 2006, called for 15% renewable electricity by 2025. The state hit that target; thus, in the words of Commissioner Kevin Thompson, it was time to move beyond ​“mandates that have outlived their useful life.”” [Canary Media, 3/31/2026, link to article]

California

This friendly robot just installed 100 MW of solar power “In one of the largest real-world demonstrations of construction robots being used at utility-scale solar construction so far, a Maximo robot has completed the successful installation of 100 MW of solar capacity at the sprawling AES Bellefield solar complex. At full tilt, the latest Maximo robot-equipped crews have nearly doubled the output of traditional installation methods at similar solar locations throughout Southern California.” [Electrek, 3/29/2026, link to article]

Mid-Atlantic

PENNSYLVANIA

What you can do about your rising electric bills in Pennsylvania “Tuesday, March 31 marks the end of Pennsylvania’s winter moratorium on utility shutoffs, meaning for those behind on their electricity bills, companies could cut off their power starting April 1… ​​A recently released annual survey by the Energy Information Administration shows an uptick in the number of U.S. households struggling to afford energy in 2024… the data shows almost a quarter of households sacrificed food and medicine to pay for their energy bills. Two years later, these trends are increasing… Several factors have combined to raise electricity costs in Pennsylvania, including the planned increase in hyperscale data centers, demand outpacing supply, rising supply costs and infrastructure expansion…” [WHYY, 3/30/2026, link to article]

ALABAMA

Stockton solar site battle moves to State House “State Sen. Greg Albritton and State Rep. Matt Simpson introduced complimentary bills that if passed, could temporarily stop the Stockton facility or any other solar site from moving forward… Farr said his company has already met with legislators in Montgomery and he is confident Albritton’s and Simpson’s bills will fail. The public meeting [on the Stockton Solar Farm] is set for Wednesday, April 8 at 6:30 p.m. at the John R. Rhodes Civic Center adjacent to City Hall.” [Fox10 News, 3/27/2026, link to article] (Read more here)

KANSAS

Solar company urges Crawford County to approve project, citizens have concerns “John Copyak, a representative from Shasta, which is the company behind the proposed solar energy project… attended the Crawford County planning and zoning hearing Thursday night… He’s pushing for a moratorium prohibiting large scale energy projects like his in the county — to be lifted earlier than originally approved… His new proposed date to lift the moratorium would be April 17, as opposed to the original deadline of December of this year… no further action was made on lifting the moratorium.” [Yahoo! News, 3/28/2026, link to article]

CO/NM/NV:

COLORADO

How tiny Kit Carson County has grown into a green energy giant “Kit Carson County is home to eight wind farms generating about 2.9 terawatt-hours of electricity a year- That figure is set to rise with the two projects under construction and at least four more wind farms in various stages of permitting and planning. The county is also in the process of permitting a solar installation. One solar-plus-battery installation has been permitted and a second solar-plus-storage project is applying for a permit, as is a standalone battery storage facility. “There are maybe five others kicking around,” said Kelly Alvarez, who oversees planning applications for the eastern Colorado county.” [The Colorado Sun, 3/29/2026, link to article]

AZ/UT

UTAH

Utah regulators OK program meant to promote increased use of renewable energy “Utah regulators have given the green light to a new program in the works for seven years that's meant to promote increased use of renewable energy sources to power the state. The Utah Renewable Communities initiative, rooted in legislation passed by Utah lawmakers in 2019, HB411, calls for the participation of as many as 19 Utah communities in a partnership with Rocky Mountain Power to promote renewable energy. Now it will be up to elected leaders in the 19 communities to adopt ordinances by June 2 formalizing their intent to take part.” [KSL, 3/28/2026, link to article]

Northeast

NEW YORK

Board holds public hearing on moratorium for commercial solar energy systems: “During the regular meeting of the Franklinville Town Board on March 10, the board suspended their meeting to hold a public hearing on a proposed local law to ‘establish a temporary six-month moratorium on the acceptance, processing and approval of applications for commercial and industrial solar energy systems in the town.’...Another public hearing will be scheduled once amendments are completed.” [Herald Courier, 0326,2026, link to article]

Town of Mount Morris extends new solar project moratorium for six months: “The Mount Morris Town Board voted to approve a six-month extension of their moratorium on new solar and battery storage projects last Thursday at its regularly scheduled board meeting. The previous moratorium had been in place for one year…A public hearing on proposed Local Law No. 3 of 2026, entitled: ‘A Local Law Regulating Certain Solar Energy Systems in the Town of Mount Morris, New York’ was also opened at the March 19 meeting and the board listened to comments and answered questions from the public, who cited various concerns, ranging from fires to road damage. The public hearing on the proposed solar law will remain open for one month so the board can consider public input.” [The Daily News Online, 03/26/2026, link to article]

NOTICE OF PUBLIC HEARING  For the purpose of Establishing a One (1) Year Moratorium on “Battery Energy Storage Systems in the Town of Walton”: “For the purpose of Establishing a One (1) Year Moratorium on ‘Battery Energy Storage Systems in the Town of Walton.’ Please take notice that the Town of Walton will hold a Public Hearing on Monday, April 13, 2026 at 6:00 P.M at the Walton Theatre, 81 Gardiner Place, Walton, NY 13856, regarding, establishing a One (1) Year Moratorium on ‘Battery Energy Storage Systems in the Town of Walton.’” [Catskills Today, The Reporter, 03/26/2026, link to notice]

NEW JERSEY

New Jersey Gov. Sherrill signs energy storage procurement bill into law: “New Jersey Gov. Mikie Sherrill signed a bill into law that modifies certain requirements for transmission-scale energy storage procurement under the state’s incentive program…the legislation will modify project maturity requirements to expand the number of projects eligible to receive incentive awards under the second segment of the transmission-scale Garden State Energy Storage Program (GSESP)...Sherrill approved six new energy projects within 45 days of taking office—three large-scale solar projects that will provide 24 MW of electricity, and three battery energy storage projects that will provide 355 MW of large-scale battery storage.” [Daily Energy Insider, 03/26/2026, link to article].

Mid-Atlantic

VIRGINIA

Fairfax County Adopts Zoning Regulations for Large-Scale Battery Energy Storage Systems: “The Board of Supervisors of Fairfax County, Virginia, amended its zoning ordinance establishing a comprehensive regulatory framework for large-scale battery energy storage systems (BESS). The amendment, which became effective on March 18, 2026, integrates BESS into the county’s existing utility and zoning framework as a “light utility facility” and introduces specific use permissions, development standards and application requirements.” [JD Supra, 03/26/2026, link to article]

Board prepares for new solar laws: “The Charlotte County Board of Supervisors discussed solar and battery energy storage amendments that might be needed if several current pieces of legislation related to battery energy storage systems (BESS) and solar development become law…The board referred the subject of solar and battery energy storage to the county’s planning commission for further review, but during its meeting March 11, supervisors decided to hold a meeting jointly with the commission since the county needs to recommend amendments for a comprehensive plan no later than May 22 based on two proposed bills…The county has about 30,000 acres near transmission lines in approved projects, with about 20,000 of that outside the solar fence line. Supervisors discussed whether the amount of acreage outside of solar facilities’ fencelines should be limited since this land could be used for battery energy storage in the future…” [The Charlotte Gazette, 03/26/2026, link to article]

Southeast

Nothing to see here, folks!

Midwest

INDIANA

Despite roadblocks, Indiana's solar capacity nearly doubled last year: “All in all, the state's solar capacity nearly doubled in 2025, according to a report from Wood Mackenzie and the Solar Energy Industries Association, a trade group. Indiana saw the 3rd largest solar capacity influx in the country last year, behind energy giants Texas and California…And finding physical space to put the panels remains an ordeal throughout the country: one rough night in a county zoning meeting can halt a project years-in-the-making…[Topher Anderson, the director of programs and operations for the Indiana Conservative Alliance for Energy] worries that projects will continue to get caught in the crossfire of local anti-renewable sentiment, but ultimately, he thinks that solar opportunities will prove too alluring for Indiana to ignore.” [Indy Star, 03/26/2026, link to article]

North-Central

MISSOURI

Missouri Solar Fight Puts Farmers’ Futures, and Land, at Stake: “Legislation under discussion would either temporarily halt new solar projects or impose additional requirements on future developments, including local approval, acreage limits, and changes to tax policy. Supporters say the proposals would allow the state to establish clearer rules. Opponents warn they could disrupt projects already underway and create uncertainty for rural landowners…For Callaway County farmer Fred Atkinson, the debate reflects a reality he has been navigating for decades…Through conservation practices, he reduced soil loss and rebuilt productivity on land that had been worn down over generations…Despite those improvements, Atkinson said the economics of farming have become increasingly difficult to sustain…A statewide moratorium could halt projects already in development, while additional requirements such as acreage limits or increased taxes could make future projects financially unworkable, they said. Others warned that shifting policy could discourage long-term investment in Missouri at a time when neighboring states are competing for energy projects.” [the Missouri Times, 03/26/2026, link to article]

IOWA

Large-scale solar ordinance becomes law: “Kossuth County now has ordinances outlining the siting, operation and decommissioning of utility-scale solar farms. Supervisors approved the final readings of the ordinance during their meeting on Tuesday, March 24. The regulations apply to solar farms of 10 megawatts or more spanning 30 acres that are generating power for use off site.” [Kossuth County Advance, 03/26/2026, link to article]

Cherokee County bans solar batteries: “The Cherokee County Board of Supervisors imposed an indefinite moratorium on solar batteries”  [Iowa Public Notices, 03/26/2026, link to notice]

Hardin County Zoning Commission to review zoning fees and new ordinance March 26: “...[O]n Thursday, March 26, 2026…a new zoning section for battery energy storage systems [will be considered].” [Iowa Daily Brief, 03/26/2026, link to notice]

South-Central

KANSAS

$400 million solar project sparks debate at Crawford County zoning hearing: “Crawford County residents packed a public hearing Thursday evening to voice their opinions on renewable energy zoning regulations that could determine the fate of a $400 million solar project…Despite the opposition, Shasta Power representatives attended the hearing to address the Planning and Zoning Commission directly and present their case for the solar project…The Planning and Zoning Commission will use input from Thursday's hearing to develop a comprehensive plan that will eventually go before the County Commission for a final vote. Until that comprehensive plan is completed, Crawford County's solar moratorium remains in effect, preventing any new solar developments from moving forward.” [KOAM, 03/26/2026, link to article].

California

County board gives final OK to state's largest solar project: “California’s largest solar energy project won approval Tuesday with the Kern County Board of Supervisors’ 4-0 vote in favor of a 2-gigawatt photovoltaic installation expected to create 1,300 construction jobs at its peak and generate tax revenue of $1.5 billion during its roughly three-decade lifespan. Complete with batteries capable of delivering 16 gigawatt-hours of energy, the Buttonbush Solar and Storage project represents a significant shift, in that Kern’s other utility-scale solar farms are located in the eastern portion of the county.” [Bakersfield.com, 03/24/2026, link to article]

Northeast

MAINE

Before Trump axed clean energy sector, report shows it was a boon for jobs in Maine: “The clean energy sector in Maine grew in 2024 — employing more than 16,000 workers, making it New England’s fastest-growing clean energy workforce in recent years, according to a new state analysis…But those numbers don’t account for the fallout from decisions by President Donald Trump’s administration in the last year…Nonetheless, the Maine Department of Energy Resources highlighted recent state-level programs geared at the clean energy industry — including a January announcement that the department awarded three grants totalling $1.2 million to support energy efficiency workforce training.” [Maine Morning Star, 03/24/2026, link to article]

Maine tries again to unlock wind energy. This time, it has help: “After years of false starts and amid an acute regional energy crunch, large-scale onshore wind power could finally take off in Maine in 2026. Utility regulators in five New England states are considering developers’ proposals to build up to 1.2 gigawatts of onshore wind capacity in Maine’s far north, following a deadline for bids earlier this month…The coordination between Connecticut, Maine, Massachusetts, Rhode Island, and Vermont — all of which have ambitious clean energy goals — means this procurement is more likely to succeed than those that have fizzled out in years past.”  [Canary Media, 03/26/2026, link to article]

Mid-Atlantic

KENTUCKY

Lexington denies breaking open meeting law, but considers new rules for ‘work groups:’ “After concern over whether residents got enough say in solar panel regulations proposed by a Lexington council “work group,” the city’s Urban County Council will consider putting new rules and definitions in place for these groups and task forces…The move follows recent controversy around public transparency and engagement from a solar work group, made up of four council members and two city staffers, which met behind closed doors to draft policy around rural solar installations in Fayette County…Meanwhile, the ordinance that regulates solar on agricultural land is moving forward.” [Lexington Herald Leader, 03/35/3036, link to article]

Southeast

ALABAMA

FOX TEN NEWS AT 4:30 AM: “It's a race against time as a pair of local legislators try to put the brakes on a new solar site set up to go up in Stockton, state Senator Greg Albritton and state Representative Matt Simpson. Each introduced bills that could temporarily stop the Stockton facility or any other solar site from moving forward. The first bill would put a one-year moratorium on new solar farm development. The second would give county commissioners regulatory control over any future solar facilities built in their jurisdictions. That bill has already passed the House committee. The moratorium is expected to go to committee next week. The Senate version of the bills are still waiting to move forward.” [Fox 10 News, 03/26/2026, link to article, additional reading here, here, here].  

SOUTH CAROLINA

[Letter to the Editor] Your county council knew. They all knew. “County Council Seats 1, 2, 6, and 7 Are on the 2026 Primary Ballot. Remember What They Did. There is a word for what happened to Williamsburg County residents during the passage of the county’s solar ordinance. That word is betrayal…For more than twelve months, your elected representatives discussed, negotiated, and planned a deal that would place approximately 12,000 acres — roughly 18 square miles — of Williamsburg County land into industrial solar development…Every council member who stayed silent for over a year while Project Leiden was being arranged made a choice….Demand a full accounting of every meeting, communication, and agreement connected to Project Leiden — from its first appearance over a year before the ordinance through the moment the name was changed on the floor.” [Post and Courier, 03/35/3036, link to article]

Midwest

ILLINOIS

DeWitt County Board Sets Hearing Date for Another Solar Application; Approves Text Amendment Changes to Wind, Solar Codes: “...[A]t last Thursday night's DeWitt County Board meeting, the Board approved text amendment changes to the County's wind and solar ordinances. Witte indicates this was simply an action to bring the County's code in line with the State of Illinois.” [link to article]

MICHIGAN

Hundreds gather in Richland Township to discuss proposed Consumers Energy solar farm project: “Consumers Energy is proposing "Liberty Farms Solar," a project that would develop more than 2,000 acres of mostly farmland north of M-89. Roughly half of the 2,000 acres would feature solar panels…The township's planning commission held a public hearing on the project's application Wednesday night. Neighbors took the microphone to say they value the farmland and want to see it stay as-is.” [Fox 17, 03/25/2026, link to article]

Council says ‘no’ to solar power field; company warns it will seek state override: “City Council members rejected a proposed solar power field west of the Twentynine Palms Public Cemetery Monday. The council voted 3-1 to deny approval…Project applicant E-Group Solar wanted to install 160,000 solar panels on 184 acres north of Two Mile Road, west of the Noel’s Knoll Road extension…E-Group Solar spokesman Robert Smith confirmed the company intends to petition the state to bypass local control and get project approval from the California Energy Commission.” [Hi-Dersert Star, 03/25/2026, link to article]

Northeast

OHIO

Ohio blocks big solar farm, despite apparently fake public comments: “Ohio regulators have blocked yet another major solar project because of local pushback, even though a significant number of public comments opposing the array appear to be fabricated…The Ohio Power Siting Board decided last Thursday to deny a permit for the 94-megawatt Crossroads Solar Grazing Center, which would combine solar panels with sheep grazing in central Ohio. Although the project otherwise met all legal requirements, the board concluded that it ​’fails to serve the public interest…’ The Crossroads Solar case exposes deeper flaws in Ohio’s renewable energy siting process, some say.” [Canary Media, 03/24/2026, link to article]

NEW YORK

Oyster Bay Town Board hears resident concerns as it looks to extend battery energy storage system moratorium again: “Several Town of Oyster Bay residents expressed concerns about battery energy storage systems on Wednesday, March 24, at a hearing held as the town board considers a 12-month moratorium on approving them, following a series of six-month moratoriums…Montesano said the current moratorium is set to expire on April 30. The proposed extension would extend the ban until April 30, 2027…The board left the record open for 20 days and is expected to vote on the moratorium extension at its next meeting on Tuesday, April 14.” [Long Island Press, 03/24/2025, link to article]

[Tweet]: “#NYSPSC Office of Renewable Energy Siting and Electric Transmission (ORES) to hold Fort Edward Solar LLC, Oral argument held before Director Hagos for a major renewable facility siting permit in the Town of Fort Edward, Washington County” [@NYSDPS, 03/24/2026, link to post].

With Battery Storage Site Planned in Shadow of Staten Island (NY) Bridge, New Law Would Take on Safety Issues: “The legislation would require the operators of large outdoor battery storage facilities to provide the FDNY with live, continuous access to critical system data, including real-time monitoring of conditions that could signal potential safety risks. It would also mandate annual inspections by independent, qualified engineers not affiliated with the facility’s owner or operator, with the results submitted to city agencies and made publicly available…’By requiring independent inspections and direct data access for FDNY, the bill introduces an additional layer of accountability and strengthens the city’s ability to respond to potential incidents,’ [Tottenville Councilmember, Frank] Morano said.” [Fire Engineering, 03/25/2026, link to article].

Town of Lewis approves one-year moratorium on renewable energy projects: “On Monday evening, March 16, the Town of Lewis board voted unanimously to go ahead with a one-year moratorium on certain renewable energy developments following an emotional and at times deeply personal public hearing in which residents voiced overwhelming support for slowing the process down.” [Boonville Herald, 03/25/2026, link to article].

ALABAMA

Alabama lawmakers in race against clock to halt massive solar farm project: ‘Slow it down’: “Legislation in both chambers would impose a one-year moratorium on new solar farm developments in Alabama. Lawmakers are also weighing separate bills that would give county commissioners in Mobile and Baldwin counties regulatory authority over future solar projects…The one-year moratorium appears in SB354/HB617, while the commission oversight proposal is included in SB358/HB618…Albritton noted that in the final days of the session, lawmakers will need unanimous consent to move legislation between chambers.” [AL.com, 03/24/2026, link to article]

Midwest

MICHIGAN

Portage puts moratorium on data centers, battery storage facilities: “As expected, the city of Portage has put a moratorium on any new plans for data centers and electrical battery storage facilities in town…Without discussion, the council voted to impose the moratorium until the end of this year, or until they can put city zoning and ordinance amendments in place to regulate the operations.” [WIN 98.5, 03/25/2026, link to article].

California

Observers say a plan to convert 136,000 acres of farmland into a 21,000 MW solar-plus-storage complex in California’s Central Valley can be a model for clean energy development in the state and nation: “The Valley Clean Infrastructure Plan (VCIP) envisions converting 136,000 acres of land into 21 gigawatts of battery-backed solar power — nearly as much utility-scale solar capacity as has been installed in California to date…None of this has happened yet, and completing it will take 10 years or more. But after years of work with developer Golden State Clean Energy, VCIP is now poised to move from concept to reality. In December, Westlands Water District’s board approved the programmatic environmental impact report that lays out a master plan for the project…Water shortages are the primary reason that Westlands growers are seeking alternatives to farming.” [Canary Media, 03/24/2026, link to article]

East County green energy boom sparks concern over impacts to natural landscape, cultural sites: “East County has become the region’s epicenter in the renewable energy development boom…John Bathke, an assistant professor of Native American and Indigenous Studies at San Diego City College, characterized the trend in East County as ‘green colonialism.’ The term refers to the imposition of renewable energy projects on disadvantaged and Indigenous communities…Starlight Solar is a proposed 588-acre solar and battery storage project in Boulevard that would transform sections of natural space nestled between the Old Highway 80 and the U.S.-Mexico border…Durckel said ‘it’s possible’ the project may go to the county’s Planning Commission on May 8.” [KPBS, 03/24/2026, link to article].

NEW YORK

As state pushes battery tech, towns and neighbors fret over fire risk Some communities are wary as companies scramble to bring energy storage systems online: “In recent months, more than a dozen companies have called Salina town officials with similar proposals: Let us install an energy storage system in your town…Places like Syracuse, Salina, Lysander and Oswego have approved six-month or yearlong moratoriums on the systems. They say they need time to make sure the batteries are safe and in the right place.” [Post-Standard (Syracuse, NY), 03/24/2026, link to article].

The project is pending final approval from the Will County Board…The next Will County Board meeting is scheduled for 9:30 a.m. on April 16 at 302 N. Chicago St., Joliet.” [Now.Solar, 03/24/2026, link to article]

Solar ordinances approaching for Catlin, Tilton board action: With their moratorium on solar farms set to sunset at the end of April, the villages of Tilton and Catlin are looking to update their related zoning regulations…Tilton’s village board has scheduled a special meeting Thursday to address utility-scale solar projects. Catlin officials are expected to follow suit in the next few weeks…Catlin board members have said they are developing new language addressing industrial-scale solar and wind farms to focus on health, safety and setbacks. AZ/UT

Nothing to see here, folks!

California

California’s SB 886 could make storage a prerequisite for data center interconnection: “A new bill making its way through the California State Senate could effectively make energy storage a prerequisite for data centers to interconnect. The California Technology Innovation and Ratepayer Protection Act (SB 886) aims to protect ratepayers from cost impacts of data centers by ensuring that large load users pay the entirety of the grid costs they trigger. In practice, it could turn storage into a de-facto box on the interconnection checklist despite its framing as a ratepayer protection bill. While a previous version of the bill laid out a strict onsite behind-the-meter storage mandate, the latest text instead includes a requirement that developers of large projects – defined in the bill as those being 25 MW or larger – cover half of their hourly energy needs with dispatchable zero-carbon supply. This must be prefunded and pre-committed for a 15-year time period.” [PV Magazine, 03/24/2026, link to article].  

Monterey County seeks input on Battery Energy Storage System ordinance, with first meeting in Prunedale: “The County of Monterey Housing and Community Development Department (HCD) kicked off the first of what will be many community meetings to solicit input and develop an ordinance to regulate Battery Energy Storage System (BESS) facilities in unincorporated areas of Monterey County. Around 40 people gathered at Prunedale Grange on Wednesday, March 18, along with several local officials…The ordinance will be built iteratively, beginning with a legal and technical foundation, then incorporating community and expert input to create a draft.” [Monterey County NOW, 03/23/2026, link to article].

Supervisors hit pause on energy storage projects: “...[T]he San Benito County Board of Supervisors has temporarily frozen approvals for large-scale energy storage projects. At its March 10 meeting, the board voted 4-1 to impose a 45-day moratorium, which can be extended to nearly two years, to allow the county to draft rules alongside stakeholders and the one company currently pursuing a local project, Allium Solar. Supervisor Kollin Kosmicki framed the moratorium as a matter of public safety.” [BenitoLink, 03/24/2026, link to article]

Daily Template 3/24

To:

staff@greenlightamerica.org

Subject

Daily Clean Energy Clips 03/24/2026


Northeast

MASSACHUSETS

Massachusetts Gov. Maura Healey signs an executive order establishing a 15-month time limit for final permitting decisions on large-scale solar projects: “Massachusetts Governor Maura Healey signed an executive order to accelerate clean energy deployment across the state. The directive establishes a 15-month limit for final state and local permitting decisions on large-scale infrastructure…Current project timelines often exceed three years due to fragmented local reviews and grid constraints.” [PVMagazine, 03/20/2026, link to article]

OHIO

Public hearing on wind, solar installations March 26 ahead of potential commissioners vote: “The Portage County Commissioners will host a public hearing regarding certain wind and solar installations at 6:30 p.m. March 26 at the county Emergency Management Agency headquarters…If the feedback provided at the public hearing prompts them to do so, the commissioners will on April 2 consider enacting a zoning resolution restricting large and economically significant wind and solar farms from all or part of the eight townships that have passed their own zoning resolutions giving the commissioners authority to do so…A draft resolution the commissioners circulated to township officials throughout the county last September cites health, safety and welfare concerns associated with such facilities [solar and wind].” [the Portager, 03/23/2026, link to article]

Mid-Atlantic

KENTUCKY

Proposed Henderson County wind farm ignites fight over rural identity Proposed Henderson County wind farm ignites fight over rural identity: “A proposed wind energy project that could become Kentucky’s first utility-scale wind farm has sparked intense debate in Henderson County…At a March 18 public hearing, the Henderson City-County Planning Commission began a yearlong series of meetings to craft new land-use and siting rules for wind turbines while a county moratorium on new wind projects remains in place. The hearing focused on where and under what conditions such development could be allowed…The commission is scheduled to hold additional hearings on noise, shadow flicker and other issues, with the next one set for May 20. Once rules are drafted, the Planning Commission will make a recommendation to the Henderson Fiscal Court.For now, the moratorium prevents any new wind applications while the county decides how – or whether – to allow them.” [National Wind Watch, 03/21/2026, link to article].

Southeast

SOUTH CAROLINA

Proposed solar farm in Sumter County withdrawn amid community pushback: “In a new filing with the Public Service Commission of South Carolina, TOCE SC Solar One requested to withdraw its application to build and operate a 170-megawatt solar facility in the county.” [WLTX19, 03/22/2026, link to article, additional reading].

Midwest

MICHIGAN

Large solar park proposed in Midland County: “Developers with DTE Electric are proposing the Poseyville Solar Park in Ingersoll Township…The plan calls for a 129-megawatt solar energy system — covering more than 626 fenced acres across multiple parcels near Freeland and Midland.” [WNEM5, 03/13/2026, link to article, additional reading].

Northern Rockies

IDAHO

Jerome County, Idaho’s commissioners propose restricting utility-scale solar and wind projects to industrial zones: “Large-scale renewable energy projects on public lands, like Lava Ridge, are largely on hold… Jerome County is crafting a new energy ordinance with hopes of striking a balance between public wishes and private property rights. Under the existing ordinance, energy projects are allowed in any zoning type in Jerome County, including agricultural. However, commissioners have heard steady public opposition to industrial energy proposals for wind and solar, which have multiplied in recent years…In May 2025, the commission put a moratorium on energy projects while they developed a new ordinance governing private lands. Under the draft ordinance, energy production would only be allowed in industrial zones…An open house for the comprehensive plan and energy ordinance is on Monday, March 23, and a hearing on the energy ordinance is scheduled for April 13.” [Idaho News 6, 03/19/2026, link to article].

Utah firm rPlus Energies secures more than $650 million in debt and tax equity financing for its proposed 520 MW Blacks Creek solar facility in Ada County, Idaho: “rPlus Energies has secured more than $650 million in financing to advance a major solar project in Idaho, positioning the company to meet rising power demand driven by data center expansion. The Salt Lake City-based developer announced it has finalized debt and tax equity financing for the Blacks Creek Energy Center, a 520 MWdc (400 MWac) solar facility in Ada County…The company previously acquired the Blacks Creek project from Qcells in late 2025 as part of a broader 900 MW solar and storage portfolio in Ada County.” [ConstructionOwners.com, 03/21/2026, link to article].

Webster to Hold April 2 Hearing on Development Moratorium “The Town of Webster will hold a public hearing on Thursday, April 2, 2026, at 7:30 p.m. at the Town Board Meeting Hall, 1002 Ridge Road, to consider Local Law No. 2 of 2026. The proposed law would create a temporary six‑month moratorium on certain development permits and approvals while the town updates its Comprehensive Plan and zoning laws.” [NewsBreak, 3/19/26, link to article]

Lawmakers weigh one-year pause on Baldwin County solar farm “Alabama lawmakers are considering a one-year moratorium on new solar power projects, a move that could temporarily halt a large solar farm planned near Stockton in North Baldwin County. The proposal, HB619, was discussed this week in the House Transportation, Utilities, and Infrastructure Committee and is expected to face a vote soon.” [NewsBreak, 3/19/26, link to article, additional reporting]

ILLINOIS

Madison County Board Votes Against Solar Energy Facility Near Granite City “The Madison County Board has voted to block a Commercial Solar Energy Facility from building near Granite City… During their regular meeting on March 18, 2026, the Madison County Board was asked to approve a Special Use Permit for Arena Libra Solar, LLC, to develop a Commercial Solar Energy Facility in an Agricultural District in Nameoki Township near Granite City.” [Edglen Today, 3/19/26, link to article, additional reporting]

IOWA

Supervisors Approve 1st Reading of Solar Ordinance “The Kossuth County Board of Supervisors approved the first reading of an ordinance regulating utility scale solar energy systems during their meeting Tuesday… Nobody spoke about the ordinance during a public hearing that was held prior to the first reading. The board will hold a second reading during their meeting next week.” [KLGA/KLGZ, 3/19/26, link to article]

Jerome County drafts a new ordinance to restrict renewable energy projects to industrial zones only. “Jerome County is crafting a new energy ordinance with hopes of striking a balance between public wishes and private property rights… Under the existing ordinance, energy projects are allowed in any zoning type in Jerome County, including agricultural. However, commissioners have heard steady public opposition to industrial energy proposals for wind and solar, which have multiplied in recent years… Faced with the choice of starting the ordinance drafting process over from scratch and allowing several permits to advance, the commission opted to move forward during a meeting on Thursday. They scheduled a hearing on the draft ordinance for possible adoption for April 13, with the concession that some parts of it may need to be relaxed in the future.” [Idaho News 6, 3/19/26, link to article]

Cortlandville considers extending temporary ban on solar energy systems “The Cortlandville Town Board is considering extending a temporary ban on approving new solar energy permits. The moratorium, which began in October, expires April 9.” [607 News Now, 3/18/26, link to article]

Lysander to Hold Hearing on Solar Farm Moratorium March 19 “The Town of Lysander will hold a public hearing on Thursday, March 19, 2026, at 6:30 PM to talk about ending the current pause (moratorium) on new solar farms. Concern growing over company trying to lease land for wind farms “Officials say First Mile Development, a firm based out of Minnesota, has been trying to solicit members of the community… Officials say there is a moratorium in the works that is currently with the planning and zoning commission.” [14 News, 3/18/26, link to article, more reporting]

MICHIGAN

Neighbors push back against proposed 1,900-acre solar farm in Zeeland, Jamestown townships “The vast majority of neighbors who attended a series of public hearings this week spoke out against a proposed 200-megawatt solar farm in Zeeland and Jamestown townships. Energy company RWE is proposing the "Silver Maple Solar" project, which would sit on 1,900 acres currently zoned for agriculture in both townships… The public hearings — held Tuesday at Evergreen Ministries in Hudsonville and Wednesday in Zeeland Township — are a required step before RWE can submit a state application to the Michigan Public Service Commission. The state-level application process is made possible by Public Act 233.” [Fox 17, 3/18/26, link to article]

Clark County denies applications for proposed solar farm “Clark County leaders moved Wednesday to deny special exemptions for a proposed solar energy farm after more than five hours of public comment… Leaders took no action on a separate request for a use variance from the developers.” [WLKY, 3/18/26, link to article]

Johnstown sets hearings on batteries, parking, chickens [Leader-Herald, 3/17/26, link to article (paywalled)]

KENTUCKY

Henderson County to host first public hearing on wind energy rules Wednesday “The Henderson City-County Planning Commission will hold the first of seven scheduled public hearings Wednesday to develop regulations for wind energy conversion systems… Officials intend to use the series of hearings to gather technical data and public input during a two-year moratorium on wind energy projects. The Henderson County Fiscal Court passed the moratorium in October 2025 to allow the planning commission time to draft the county's first wind turbine ordinance.” [44 News, 3/17/26, link to article]

McLean County residents solicited about using land for windmills “First Mile Development, LLC, a firm specializing in solar and wind energy based out of Minnesota, began sending out solicitation letters to members of the McLean County community about opportunities for wind farms to begin development within the county in February.” [Messenger-Inquirer, 3/17/26, link to article]

ALABAMA

Environmental groups, developer push back against Alabama’s proposed solar farm freeze “An effort to temporarily halt and regulate a controversial South Alabama solar farm is raising concerns that legislation moving through Montgomery could inadvertently slow the expansion of clean energy across the state. The issue surfaced Tuesday during a public hearing on legislation that would impose a one-year moratorium on new solar power facilities. The bill was discussed before the Alabama House Transportation, Utilities, and Infrastructure Committee.” [AL.com, 3/17/26, link to article]

Midwest

MICHIGAN

1,900-acre Ottawa County solar farm faces pushback at public meeting “A large-scale commercial solar farm proposed for Ottawa County is attempting to gain the favor of residents after months of public outcry… The project, dubbed Silver Maple Solar, could generate enough electricity to power over 34,000 homes, according to RWE… Residents have packed several Zeeland Township Planning Commission meetings, voicing concerns about farmland loss, aesthetics, environmental harm and more. Considering the project’s timeline, RWE is considering bypassing township leaders and asking the Michigan Public Service Commission for approval, based on Public Act 233.” [MLive, 3/17/26, link to article]

Plains

NEBRASKA

Lincoln County Board OKs new zoning rules with 1st soil 'overlay districts' “If and when Lincoln County has a wind or solar farm to consider, its developers will face extra regulations should they want to erect them on sensitive Sandhills or Loess Hills soils. The County Board Monday made the county Nebraska's first to adopt soil-based "overlay districts" by twice voting 5-0 to replace its 2012 comprehensive plan and to rewrite Lincoln County's zoning regulations accordingly. Commissioners made no changes to the comprehensive plan as redone by Marvin Planning Associates. The David City firm, which developed the 2012 plan, worked on its update and the rewritten zoning regulations over the past three years. Adoption of the zoning revisions included all but one amendment devised Feb. 10 by the county's Planning Commission, as well as other changes offered Monday by firm principal Keith Marvin and County Board members.” [North Platte Telegraph, 3/16/26, link to article, link to text]

Northeast

NEW YORK

Hartwick schedules public hearing on solar energy moratorium “The Hartwick Town Board has scheduled a public hearing regarding a possible moratorium on building new solar energy systems.Town Supervisor Cody Moore said Tuesday, March 10 that the Town Board approved the public hearing date at its Monday, March 9 meeting. The hearing is slated for 6:30 p.m. Friday, March 20 at Town Hall. Moore added that a special meeting likely would be held directly after the public hearing to vote on the proposed six-month moratorium.” [Daily Star, 3/10/26, link to article]

Gloversville considers battery, solar facility bans “The City of Gloversville will have a series of public hearings March 24 on its way to banning both battery-energy storage systems and large-scale solar facilities in the city.” [Leader-Herald, 3/15/26, link to article]

Town of Rochester sets public hearing on proposed battery facility moratorium “The Rochester Town Board decided Thursday to schedule a public hearing on a proposed local law establishing a moratorium on the construction of commercial battery storage facilities. The hearing is set for 6:30 p.m. Thursday, March 26, at the Harold Lipton Community Center, 15 Tobacco Road.” [Daily Freeman, 3/14/26, link to article, link behind paywall]

Mid-Atlantic

PENNSYLVANIA

Residents press McKean County commissioners on solar projects, ordinance still pending “Residents asked commissioners to consider banning the project and issuing a moratorium on solar development in the county. According to Pennsylvania’s Municipalities Planning Code, county commissioners do not have the authority to take either action; zoning and land‑use decisions, including any moratorium, must be enacted by the township that holds zoning power. Back in October, however, when commissioners were working to adopt the county’s wind energy ordinance, they indicated the county was working on similar protective ordinances for projects such as solar and battery storage. Five months later, commissioners have said the county is in the final stages of completing its ordinance for adoption.” [Bradford Era, 3/11/26, link to article]

Ransom Twp. defends rejection of solar farm amid appeal “Ransom Twp. argues a proposed solar farm the supervisors rejected last year adversely affects the township and its residents as the developer appeals the supervisors’ denial of the project. In an opposition brief filed last month, then-township Solicitor Kevin Conaboy said residents opposed to the plans presented credible evidence of the adverse effects the project would have on the area and Pivot Energy didn’t provide proof the project would be compatible with township zoning.” [Times-Tribune, 3/16/26, link to article, link behind paywall]

KENTUCKY

Dame discusses possibility of wind mills moving into the county “During Thursday's [McLean County] Fiscal Court meeting, Dame and the four county magistrates voted to allow County Attorney Donna Dant to begin drafting a moratorium concerning the potential windmill construction in the county. The draft, once approved, will be sent to the members of the McLean County Planning and Zoning Committee for any changes, before being presented to the public via a public hearing. The moratorium will amend the existing ordinance to prohibit the construction of windmills in the county for a period of one year, while additional information is gathered about the impact of the windmills on the county.” [McLean County News, 3/12/26, link to article]

Southeast

ALABAMA

Alabama senator seeks statewide ban on solar installations “Alabama Senator Greg Albritton (R) has introduced AL SB354, which seeks to pause all solar power construction statewide. The bill made it out of its initial committee on March 11, and was introduced to the broader legislative body on March 26. As the bill is currently written, it would be implemented immediately upon appropriate signatures being collected.” [PV Magazine, 3/16/26, link to article]

Midwest

INDIANA

Huntingburg board denies AES appeal over expired development plan “The Huntingburg Board of Zoning Appeals unanimously denied Crossvine Solar One LLC’s appeal of the city’s determination that its commercial solar development plan had expired, following a lengthy hearing on March 11, 2026. The board affirmed Planning and Zoning Administrator Paul Lake’s December decision that Crossvine’s development plan was void for failing to obtain all applicable permits and to commence construction within two years of approval, as required by the city’s Unified Development Ordinance.” [Dubois County Free Press, 3/12/26, link to article]

Pacific Northwest

OREGON

A proposed Willamette Valley solar park is still in the mix. Neighbors want to nix it “It’s been almost three years since a proposed solar park in Linn County met fierce pushback from local residents and farmers. Now, after a long lull, there are signs the plan to install solar panels across more than a thousand acres of prime farmland could take shape. There’s still no application, but recent meetings with energy regulators suggest the developers intend to move forward before their May 19 deadline.” [OPB, 3/13/26, link to article]

Amazon buys site of Oregon’s largest planned solar and energy storage project “After outbidding regional utilities with an $83 million offer in January, Amazon acquired the site of a planned 1,200-megawatt solar project and up to 7,200-megawatt battery storage capacity development late last month... But the $2.3 trillion tech company still has state regulatory hurdles to clear before it can begin construction, and a tight timeline if the company plans to take advantage of expiring federal solar tax credits. The Sunstone Solar Project… is slated to be the largest solar and battery storage project so far in Oregon, with plans to be located on private land in Morrow County… Oregon Department of Energy’s senior policy advisor for siting, Sarah Esterson, said the agency has been in contact with Pine Gate Renewables about transferring the site certificate since November…. It could take between three and six months, according to Esterson… The Oregon Energy Facility Siting Council has reviewed and approved dozens of requests to transfer in the past, she said, but an entity like Amazon would be a first for the state.” [OPB, 3/11/26, link to article]

Northern Rockies

Nothing to see here, folks!

CO/NM/NV

NEW MEXICO

Massive solar farm proposed near Stanley receives little pushback at hearing “Santa Fe County is weighing another utility-scale solar and battery energy storage development, which with more than 700,000 solar panels, would be one of the largest projects ever proposed in the state. A Thursday meeting on Linea Energy’s Globemallow project just north of Stanley in southeastern Santa Fe County showed much less resistance and interest than the Rancho Viejo solar project. Only a handful of people attended a Sustainable Land Development Code hearing officer meeting about the proposal. By contrast, the controversial Rancho Viejo project saw hundreds of people turn out to marathon meetings.” [Santa Fe New Mexican, 3/12/26, link to article]

AZ/UT

Nothing to see here, folks!

California

Vacaville Moves to Block Lithium-Ion Battery Storage Projects “The Vacaville City Council voted unanimously on Tuesday to pass an ordinance that aims to severely limit Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS) in the city, culminating a year-long effort on the issue. Since the last council discussion of the ordinance, city staff updated it to disallow Lithium-ion battery chemistry in the city. Multiple BESS projects in the area are already working their way through the California Energy Commission’s AB 205 alternate permitting pathway, which would supersede the city’s ordinance if approved.” [GovTech Industry Insider, 3/12/26, link to article]

Subject

Daily Clean Energy Clips 3/13/26

Have an awesome weekend!

~ Jaxon T.

Northeast

Nothing to see here, folks!

Mid-Atlantic

OHIO

Are faked public comments about to tank an Ohio solar farm?: Open Road Renewables’ 94-megawatt Crossroads Solar Grazing Center in Morrow County — a dual-use agrivoltaic project pairing solar generation with sheep grazing about an hour north of Columbus — is facing potential denial by the Ohio Power Siting Board despite an investigation by Canary Media finding that a substantial number of public comments opposing the project appear to have been fabricated. Company vice president Doug Herling reviewed comments submitted to the siting board and identified at least 34 instances of apparently false names or addresses; Canary Media independently verified the existence of only one of those commenters. After removing fake, anonymous, and duplicate submissions, Herling found that more than 78% of verifiable public comments supported the project, with support running above 58% within Morrow County itself. The anti-solar group Concerned Citizens of Morrow County had publicly claimed opposition ran 10-to-1 — a figure that did not hold up under cross-examination during the seven-day evidentiary hearing. The siting board’s staff reversed its earlier recommendation in favor of the project after a last-minute resolution by Cardington Township, where a single trustee changed his vote. The board is expected to rule by March 19. “When comments are submitted under false identities, false addresses, or with misrepresented affiliations, it undermines the credibility of the process and is unfair to residents who participated honestly and in good faith,” said Bella Bogin, director of programs at Ohio Citizen Action. [Canary Media, Feb 16 2026, link to article]

VIRGINIA

Siting of battery energy storage projects; commercial solar photovoltaic generation facilities: The Virginia General Assembly passed HB891, which strips localities of the authority to require a special exception or any other local land use approval for battery energy storage systems sited on parcels already approved for utility-scale solar under an active special use permit. The bill, signed by the House Speaker on March 12, also exempts such BESS installations from the state’s host siting agreement requirements — the negotiation process localities have used to secure community benefits and revenue from energy projects — and from giving localities written notice of intent to locate. The Virginia Association of Counties formally requested a gubernatorial veto, warning that the bill eliminates local governments’ ability to account for fire safety costs and public safety resources. A companion bill, SB443, has also passed both chambers and awaits the governor’s action. [LegiScan / Virginia General Assembly, Mar 12 2026, link to bill]

Southeast

ALABAMA

Bill to pause new solar developments advances to Alabama Senate for vote: The Alabama Senate Transportation and Energy Committee voted to advance Senate Bill 354, sponsored by Sen. Greg Albritton, R-Atmore, which would impose a one-year moratorium on the construction of new large-scale solar power facilities statewide — with an amendment carving out territory served by the Tennessee Valley Authority. The bill was prompted by community backlash over a proposed $300 million, 4,500-acre Silicon Ranch solar project in Stockton, in north Baldwin County, intended to help power a Meta data center. “The public is very concerned with what the reclamation requirements are, if any. They’re concerned about any ecological matters that come into play, and lots of questions that I don’t know how to answer,” Albritton said. “I’m asking that we pass this moratorium on construction of any new ones, until we can find and get some answers as to those policies and procedures.” The TVA carve-out was introduced by Sen. Steve Livingston, R-Scottsboro, citing concerns about Redstone Arsenal: “The last thing you want is a war fighter to have a little hiccup, because we’ve had a little power interruption.” The sole public hearing speaker, John Dodd of Energy Alabama, opposed the bill, arguing it amounts to hitting “a kill switch on an entire industry overnight” rather than fixing the process failures that enabled the Stockton project. “If the process failed the public, then we should fix the process,” Dodd said. The committee advanced the bill with Sens. Robert Stewart, D-Selma, and Merika Coleman, D-Birmingham, in opposition. If enacted, the moratorium would take effect immediately and would not apply to solar farms already built or under construction. [Alabama Political Reporter, Mar 12 2026, link to article]

Midwest

INDIANA

Solar farm proposal earns ‘unfavorable’ recommendation: The Montgomery County Advisory Plan Commission voted 6-2 to send an unfavorable recommendation to the Montgomery County Board of Commissioners on a rezoning request tied to the proposed Copper Box Solar Project. The petition, filed Jan. 13 by ENGIE North America, seeks to rezone about 1,800 acres of agricultural land across 19 parcels in Madison and Union townships to an agricultural overlay district to allow construction of a commercial solar energy system, with approximately 890 acres within fenced solar array areas. Lead project developer Tom Schoder said the project would generate up to 200 MW of electricity — enough to power about 33,000 homes — represent a roughly $300 million investment, generate at least $20 million in tax revenue over its 30-year lifespan, create about 250 temporary construction jobs, and connect to a Duke Energy transmission line feeding into a Crawfordsville substation. Opponents raised concerns about removing productive farmland from agricultural use, potential fire hazards, environmental impacts, and possible effects on nearby property values. The commission voted to send an unfavorable recommendation based on three factors: the request was inconsistent with the comprehensive plan, inconsistent with the current conditions and character of the area, and inconsistent with the most desirable use for which the land is adapted. The Montgomery County Board of Commissioners will make the final decision at a future meeting following a public hearing. [Journal Review, Mar 11 2026, link to article]

North-Central

Nothing to see here, folks!

South-Central

KANSAS

Douglas County commissioners extend permit for planned solar farm north of Lawrence: Douglas County commissioners voted 4-1 on March 11 to extend by one year the conditional use permit for the Kansas Sky Energy Center, a large solar farm proposed north of Lawrence, while a court injunction continues to block construction. The extension was requested by the developer in December 2025 after a scheduled trial was pushed from April 2026 to November, with a pretrial conference set for September. [Kansas Press Association / Lawrence Times, Mar 11 2026, link to article]
