Daily Clips 3.12

To:

staff@greenlightamerica.org

Subject

Daily Clean Energy Clips 3/12/26


Hope everyone has a wonderful Thursday! 💥😎

~ Jaxon T.


Northeast

MAINE

Harrington adopts ordinance prohibiting utility‑scale solar farms: Voters at Harrington’s annual town meeting enacted a Solar Energy Ordinance banning utility-scale solar and for-profit power generation, with the measure drawing overwhelming support and no objections from the roughly 35-person audience gathered at Narraguagus High School. The ordinance preserves personal and commercial on-site solar with Planning Board approval, and permits net metering for energy offset — but not for profit. Its stated intent is to preserve rural character and “prevent industrial encroachment incompatible with community values.” Ground-mounted systems are subject to strict dimensional limits: maximum 25 feet tall, 25-foot setbacks from property lines, 45 feet from town roads, and 70 feet from state roads, with a maximum 2,500 square feet of airspace coverage. [The Maine Monitor, Mar 10 2026, link to article]

Mid-Atlantic

PENNSYLVANIA

Toxic waste cleanup may delay Washington County solar farm: St. Louis-based Cornerstone Solar LLC has applied to build a 200 MW solar farm on an abandoned strip mine in Jefferson Township, Washington County — the same site where the EPA is now moving to remove approximately 4,500 barrels of toxic waste illegally dumped and buried since the late 1970s. EPA on-scene coordinator Kevin Clark updated residents at a March 6 community meeting at the Eldersville Fire Hall, saying the cleanup — capped at $2 million in Superfund money — will proceed independently of the solar permitting process, and that the EPA is not involved in overseeing the solar farm application. The township is holding a planning commission meeting and public hearing on the Cornerstone application. [Pittsburgh Post-Gazette, Mar 9 2026, link to article] (paywalled — details sourced from [Observer-Reporter, Mar 7 2026]; thread to watch)

Southeast

Nothing to see here, folks!

Midwest

MICHIGAN

50,000-acre west Michigan wind turbine plan dead after years of debate: Apex Clean Energy has abandoned its Montcalm Wind Project after failing to secure enough land leases, falling roughly 20,000 acres short of its 50,000-acre goal across up to 11 townships in Montcalm County. The project generated years of heated local opposition, including board recalls, sheriff’s deputies at public meetings, and repeated clashes before planning commissions. Farmers who had signed leases saw the project as a significant income opportunity, while opponents raised concerns over property values, setbacks, noise, and turbine height. Apex secured commitments from around 500 landowners before pulling out. [Michigan Public, Mar 10 2026, link to article]

ILLINOIS

Crete Village Board annexes property for construction of 2,400-acre solar farm: The Crete Village Board voted 5-1 on March 10 to annex approximately 200 acres and approve construction of a 2,400-acre, 260 MW solar farm by Earthrise Energy (Arlington, VA) in the Plum Valley area, spanning both the village and unincorporated Will County. The company already operates an existing natural gas plant in Crete and plans to use that infrastructure for grid connection. The project is expected to power roughly 50,000 Illinois households, with construction anticipated later in 2026 pending county approvals and grid connection targeted for 2028. The vote drew strong resident backlash; attorney Steven Becker said a client may have grounds to sue over notice violations, while board member Holly Milburn defended the outreach effort, saying the board had sent mailers and visited 172 households. [Chicago Tribune, Mar 10 2026, link to article]

North-Central

WISCONSIN

Wisconsin bill would give municipalities say on wind, solar projects: A public hearing was held March 10 before the Senate Committee on Utilities, Technology and Tourism on Senate Bill 3, which would require wind and solar projects of 15 MW or more to obtain approval from every affected local municipality before the Public Service Commission can act. Under current law, projects under 100 MW require local approval, while those at 100 MW or above go directly to the PSC. Municipalities would have 90 days to approve or deny a project under the bill; failure to act would constitute automatic approval. Supporters included local officials, the Wisconsin Farm Bureau, Realtors, and the MacIver Institute. Opponents included utilities, environmental groups, trade unions, and the Conservative Energy Forum, which argued the bill fragments energy planning and threatens grid reliability. Co-sponsor Sen. Howard Marklein (R-Spring Green) said the bill targets solar and wind specifically because “those were the two projects that we heard about” from constituents. [WSAW, Mar 11 2026, link to article]

South-Central

Nothing to see here, folks!

Plains

Nothing to see here, folks!

Pacific Northwest

OREGON

Avangrid installs all panels at 120 MW Oregon solar park: Avangrid has completed installation of more than 250,000 solar panels — assembled by SEG Solar at its Houston facility — at the Tower Solar project in Morrow County, located just west of Boardman on approximately 900 acres of industrially zoned land owned by the Port of Morrow. The project totals 166 MWdc / 120 MWac and is expected to reach commercial operation later in 2026, delivering energy to the Portland General Electric grid via the Green Future Impact voluntary program and supporting a new QTS data center campus. The project created approximately 200 construction jobs, mostly regional union labor, and is expected to generate roughly $20 million in combined PILOTs and property taxes. CEO Jose Antonio Miranda said: “This is a major milestone for Tower Solar and demonstrates how investment in America’s energy infrastructure drives economic growth nationwide.” [Renewables Now, Mar 10 2026, link to article]

ID/MT/WY

Nothing to see here, folks!

CO/NM/NV

Nothing to see here, folks!

AZ/UT

UTAH

Creekstone secures land for 1 GW of solar to power Utah data centre: Creekstone Energy has executed a long-term solar lease with Utah’s School and Institutional Trust Lands Administration for approximately 13,000 acres in Millard County, with Millard County granting zoning approval for the first phase. The company plans to develop more than 1 GW of solar over the next 18 months to power its Delta Gigasite — a master-planned AI hyperscale data center campus of more than 1,100 acres designed for 10+ GW of total capacity. Lease revenue will benefit Utah’s public education system. CEO Ray Conley said: “We are building more than a data center campus — we are building an energy ecosystem.” COO Chase Sheffield added: “With 13,000 acres now under lease, we have the land position to build a solar portfolio that materially offsets the power demands of our hyperscale tenants.” First solar generation is expected within one year, with data center deliveries beginning in 2027. [Renewables Now, Mar 2026, link to article]

California

CALIFORNIA

Vacaville adopts Battery Energy Storage Systems ordinance: The Vacaville City Council voted unanimously on March 10 to adopt a BESS ordinance, the culmination of a year-long community process. Key provisions include a citywide ban on lithium-ion battery chemistry, increased setbacks from 300 to 500 feet for sensitive uses, and a required financial assurance plan; one parcel — the former Gibson Canyon Creek Wastewater Treatment Plant — is retained for potential non-lithium BESS development. Multiple BESS projects are already working through the California Energy Commission’s AB 205 alternate permitting pathway, which could supersede the city ordinance. Derek Johnson, speaking on behalf of Keep Vacaville Safe’s Sarah Dunn, said the ordinance “represents years of careful work by the city staff, residents and this council to understand the benefits, risks and long-term impacts of large-scale lithium-ion BESS facilities in Vacaville.” [The Vacaville Reporter, Mar 11 2026, link to article]


Daily Clips 3.11

To:

staff@greenlightamerica.org

Subject

Daily Clean Energy Clips 3/11/26


Happy Wednesday! Did you know the world’s largest wind turbine, located in China, produces 26 MW of power and stands about 1,120 feet tall—making it even taller than the Eiffel Tower!

~ Jaxon T.

Northeast

NEW YORK

Gloversville considers ban on battery storage facilities: The Gloversville Common Council discussed, on March 10, a proposed ban on commercial battery energy storage systems, and may set a public hearing for March 24. Mayor Gary Antonucci said: "It hasn't been proven enough that it's safe enough. It's the safety of our residents." The draft law states the city finds that "utility-scale solar energy systems and commercial battery energy storage systems may have significant impacts on land use patterns, public safety, infrastructure capacity, emergency response preparedness, environmental conditions, and the long-term redevelopment objectives of the city." Exceptions would be made for battery systems serving on-site residential or commercial buildings without re-selling power, and for solar installations generating no more than 110% of on-site consumption. At least 13 municipalities in the Greater Capitol Region have enacted or plan to enact a moratorium or ban. Nearby, a 10 MW BESS in Northampton has already been approved; the same developer, Carson Power, proposes a 20 MW system in St. Armand in Essex County. [Leader-Herald / Daily Gazette, Mar 10 2026, link to article]

Town keeps battery storage moratorium: The Town of Dunkirk in Chautauqua County voted unanimously to extend its moratorium on battery energy storage systems by an additional six months to allow more time to draft a local law. Town Board member Phil Leone, who initially proposed the local law, said: "We're on our way to creating our new battery energy storage system law, but we just need some additional time." There is currently no BESS proposal before the board. Supervisor Shari Miller said: "We are not doing this in a vacuum. We have a lot of people that have their hands on it." The board has another workshop scheduled for March 26 at 5:30 p.m. Nearby, the Town of Hanover has already passed a local law banning BESS entirely following a proposed project that drew significant public outcry. [Observer Today, Mar 10 2026, link to article]

Mid-Atlantic

KENTUCKY

Proposed makeover of utility regulator met with warnings consumers would lose their voice: A Republican priority bill, Senate Bill 8, would expand the Kentucky Public Service Commission from three to five members — with two new appointments going to the Republican state auditor rather than the Democratic governor — and would administratively attach the PSC to the auditor's office. Critics warned the bill would make it harder for independent consumer advocates to intervene in utility rate cases, including those involving renewable energy. Former PSC Chair Kent Chandler said the measure "raises the bar for intervention, limits the likelihood of intervention, and ultimately reduces the evidence available to the commission in making their decisions." The Kentucky Resources Council called it a "calculated effort to silence independent advocacy for residential customers." After pushback, sponsor Sen. Brandon Smith (R-Hazard) stripped the intervention barriers before the GOP-controlled Senate passed the modified bill 30-5 on March 7. One clean energy advocate noted the bill still does not address existing laws that make it "nearly impossible" for utilities to propose lower-cost renewable alternatives to aging fossil fuel plants. [Kentucky Lantern, Mar 3 2026, link to article]

Southeast

Nothing to see here, folks!

Midwest

ILLINOIS

Robinson City Council Meets Tonight: The Robinson City Council in Crawford County has placed on its agenda a review of an ordinance "adopting a moratorium for the acceptance of applications, permitting, and permit approval for solar farms within the corporate limits and extraterritorial limits of the City of Robinson." No vote has yet been taken and no project or MW size has been disclosed. [WTYE FM, Mar 10 2026, link to article]

MICHIGAN

Michigan township seeking control over project that could add 1,000 acres of solar panels: Grass Lake Charter Township in Jackson County is pushing to retain local authority over NextEra Energy's proposed 100 MW Grass Lake Solar project, which would span approximately 700–1,000 acres across Grass Lake and Leoni Townships. Under Michigan's Public Act 233, enacted in 2023, the state's Public Service Commission can approve large-scale solar projects of 50 MW or more, potentially bypassing local ordinances it deems incompatible or "unworkable." Township Supervisor John Lesinski warned: "They might honestly come to a point where we can't agree on a final draft, at which case they could go to the MPSC and bypass a local community." Planning Commission Chair Tim Golding added: "Because if they don't want to work with us, they're just going to go to the state anyway, and they're going to put it wherever they want it." The project would generate approximately $15 million in local tax revenues and 100–200 construction jobs. [MLive, Mar 2026, link to article]

North-Central

Nothing to see here, folks!

South-Central

Nothing to see here, folks!

Plains

Nothing to see here, folks!

Pacific Northwest

WASHINGTON

Battery storage moratorium extended: Klickitat County Commissioners extended a moratorium on battery energy storage systems — and solar — for another six months at their March 3 meeting. Planning Director Scott Edelman reviewed the history: the moratorium was originally passed in September 2024 to allow time to develop standards and an ordinance, and has been extended multiple times since. The planning commission was comfortable with a draft in December, but asked for a legal review by the prosecutor's office; former PA David Quesnel then resigned, and his replacement has not yet had time to provide feedback. Edelman said: "I don't anticipate another extension." [Columbia Gorge News, Mar 9 2026, link to article]

ID/MT/WY

Nothing to see here, folks!

CO/NM/NV

Nothing to see here, folks!

AZ/UT

ARIZONA

Arizona Corporation Commission ends state's renewable energy standard: The Arizona Corporation Commission voted unanimously on March 4 to repeal the state's Renewable Energy Standard and Tariff rules, which since 2006 had required utilities to source 15% of their power from renewables by 2025. Arizona Public Service, Tucson Electric Power, and UniSource Energy Services have collectively collected more than $2.3 billion in REST surcharges from customers. The ACC said the mandates "are no longer needed and the costs are no longer justified," arguing utilities are also saddled with above-market solar contracts entered into to comply with the rules. Commission Chair Nick Myers said repealing REST does not mean renewable energy will disappear: "Any generation source, whether it be wind, solar, natural gas, whatever, is going to have to bid for their place in the market on an even playing field." Critics argued the repeal ignores the documented benefits of the program, including reduced pollution and more affordable long-term energy. Prior to the vote, APS had already dropped its voluntary goal of reaching 100% clean energy by 2050, announcing instead a major investment in natural gas. [Utility Dive, Mar 2026, link to article]

California

Nothing to see here, folks!

Daily Clips 3.10

To:

staff@greenlightamerica.org

Subject

Daily Clean Energy Clips 3/10/26


Happy Tuesday! Hope some of y’all are getting to enjoy some warmer weather this week! ☀️
~ Jaxon T.

Northeast

Nothing to see here, folks!

Mid-Atlantic

KENTUCKY

Planning commission recommends extension of BESS moratorium: The Henderson City-County Planning Commission voted unanimously to recommend extending a proposed one-year moratorium on battery energy storage systems to two years, with the clock starting only after the Henderson County Fiscal Court formally approves it — potentially pushing the start to April or May. The fiscal court originally passed a resolution for a one-year BESS moratorium in January, after voting to begin the process at a December meeting. Planning Commission Chair David Dixon said the purpose of the moratorium is to have more time to "amend or rewrite the current (BESS) ordinance." Airline Road resident Deirdre McConathy, whose family farm abuts a plot of land where a BESS had been proposed before the company dropped its plans in December, has been a vocal supporter of the moratorium and advocated for the longer two-year timeframe. The fiscal court will now decide whether to accept the two-year recommendation, maintain its original one-year proposal, or chart a different course. [The Hendersonian, Mar 6 2026, link to article]

Southeast

ARKANSAS

Concerned Citizens of the Ozarks speaks out against wind turbines: Residents opposing a proposed 200 MW wind project in Washington County gathered at the Lincoln Community Center on February 23 as Concerned Citizens of the Ozarks pushed back against renewable energy company RES's early-stage Trillium Wind Project, north of Lincoln. George Caudle, a retired Carroll County attorney, urged justices of the peace from the Washington County Quorum Court who attended the meeting to "seriously consider what protections in the county need to be in place in the event of a wind project." JP Willie Leming of Lincoln said Washington County is already a step ahead of most counties because it uses a conditional use permit process, but acknowledged "it still has work to do." The 34-turbine, 200 MW project would power roughly 40,000 homes according to the company; RES said it is committed to engaging with the local community and that "local knowledge and feedback are vital in shaping any new development." [Washington County Enterprise-Leader, Mar 4 2026, link to article]

Midwest

Nothing to see here, folks!

North-Central

MINNESOTA

Public to weigh in as Morrison County considers pausing some renewable energy projects: The Morrison County Board of Commissioners has scheduled a public hearing for April 7 to vote on whether to adopt a one-year moratorium on new solar gardens, solar farms, and wind energy conversion systems of certain sizes — driven by growing interest in large-scale renewable projects and the need to update county ordinances before more projects advance. Land Services Director Amy Kowalzek stressed the moratorium would not affect small-scale residential or farm systems, nor projects large enough to fall under the Minnesota Public Utilities Commission's jurisdiction. "Those are the three things that we're looking at putting a moratorium on — not the small-scale ground mount, like if someone's going to put three panels up for their personal use, that's not what we're talking about here," Kowalzek said. The county also proposed a working group of commissioners, planning commission members, an industry representative and a county resident to develop updated ordinance language. [Morrison County Record, Mar 7 2026, link to article]

South-Central

KANSAS

Shasta Power seeks lifting of solar moratorium in Crawford County: Shasta Power presented survey results to the Crawford County Commission showing strong local support for its proposed solar project, but commissioners indicated the current moratorium on solar development will remain in place until a comprehensive county plan is completed. The company surveyed 37 residents living immediately adjacent to the proposed solar site north of 620th Avenue and reported that more than 70% supported the project. "It tells you that people want this to happen. They just want clarity. So we're asking the county for clarity," said co-founder and managing partner Max Roe. Commissioners told Shasta Power that no decision can be made until the county completes its comprehensive planning process; the moratorium is set to expire in December but could end earlier. Owner-manager John Copyak expressed concern about the timeline's uncertainty, saying delays could jeopardize tax credit eligibility: "My concern is this could take quite a long time. And we'd like to keep the project alive, making deposits and securing the tax credit." If approved, Copyak said the project represents a "$400 million investment in Crawford County." No MW capacity was disclosed. [KOAM News Now, Mar 6 2026, link to article]

KANSAS

Douglas County commissioners to consider one-year extension for North Lawrence solar farm permit: Douglas County commissioners are weighing a one-year extension for the Kansas Sky Energy Center near North Lawrence — a 1,105-acre project covering 8 million square feet that would power roughly 30,000 homes — after ongoing litigation has prevented construction from beginning. Grant Township and more than 20 businesses and residents sued in 2024 to overturn the commission's approval, alleging violations of zoning regulations, ignored flood risks and rushed permitting. A trial is not expected to begin until at least November 30, 2026. A representative for the project warned that without the extension, "denial would cause undue harm to the Applicant by effectively terminating the project." Commissioners also considered a funding agreement allowing the county to recover costs for third-party inspections tied to the conditional use permit. [Lawrence Journal-World, Mar 6 2026, link to article]

Plains

Nothing to see here, folks!

Pacific Northwest

Nothing to see here, folks!

ID/MT/WY

Nothing to see here, folks!

CO/NM/NV

Nothing to see here, folks!

AZ/UT

ARIZONA

Avantus Closes Over $300 Million in Construction Financing with BBVA and CIBC for Kitt Solar and Energy Storage Project: Avantus has closed a financing package of more than $300 million with BBVA and CIBC for the Kitt Solar and Energy Storage Project in Pinal County, currently under construction in the City of Eloy. The project features 100 MWac/130 MWdc of solar paired with 400 MWh of battery storage, with a PPA signed with Arizona Public Service and commercial operation expected by end of 2026. The financing includes construction funding, a tax equity bridge loan, and letters of credit, with RES serving as EPC and Fluence providing the battery system. "The financing demonstrates the continued bankability and commercial viability of utility-scale solar and storage projects in the region, and Avantus' growing presence in Arizona," said CEO Cliff Graham. The project is expected to create up to 500 construction jobs and generate over $25 million in local tax revenues once operational. [Business Wire, Mar 4 2026, link to article]

California

Nothing to see here, folks!



Daily Clips 3.9

To:

staff@greenlightamerica.org

Subject

Daily Clean Energy Clips 3/9/26


Happy Monday - hope everyone had a restful weekend!

~ Jaxon T.

Northeast

CONNECTICUT

CT Siting Council Approves Expansion of Heavily Opposed Gravel Pit Solar: The Connecticut Siting Council voted unanimously 6-0 on March 5 to approve a 30 MW expansion of East Windsor's Gravel Pit Solar — the largest solar installation in Connecticut — adding 150 acres to the existing 120 MW project owned by New York-based DESRI Holdings. Opposition was fierce, with 2,223 petition signatures and pushback from state Sen. Saud Anwar. "East Windsor cannot absorb all of the solar in Connecticut," said Christina Dahl of opposition group East Windsor Citizens for Responsible Solar Development. DESRI Executive Director Aaron Svedlow said the project provides "lasting benefits for both the community and the state." Also in the pipeline: DESRI's proposed 100 MW Saltbox Solar project in East Windsor and Ellington. [CT Mirror, Mar 6 2026, link to article]

Mid-Atlantic

nothing to see here, folks

Southeast

nothing to see here, folks

Midwest

ILLINOIS

Clayco Launches Solar & Storage Business Unit, Eyes Illinois Project: Chicago-based Clayco announced the launch of Clayco Power and Energy, a new business unit focused on utility-scale solar and battery storage, led by Ryan Johnson. The unit is expected to generate about $300 million in annual revenue and add nearly 1,000 employees by 2027. Clayco is currently designing a solar and battery storage project on 350 acres in Manteno — about an hour south of Chicago — with construction scheduled to start this June. CEO Anthony Johnson said "solar must be part of the solution" and "the fastest type of power to bring to the market." No MW capacity was disclosed for the Manteno project. [Chicago Sun-Times, Mar 4 2026, link to article]

North-Central

MINNESOTA

Google-Xcel Energy Deal Includes World's Largest Grid Battery: Google and Xcel Energy announced Feb. 24 plans to build what Xcel calls "the largest battery project by gigawatt-hour energy capacity announced to date in the world" — a 300 MW/30 GWh iron-air battery from Form Energy — to support a new Google data center in Pine Island, MN. The full package includes 1,400 MW of wind and 200 MW of solar, funded through a new Clean Energy Accelerator Charge tariff paid entirely by Google. Form Energy CEO Mateo Jaramillo said modules will ship by end of 2028, calling it "the scale of project that we expect to bid and participate in going forward." The tariff will be filed with Minnesota regulators in the coming weeks. [Utility Dive, Mar 4 2026, link to article]

WISCONSIN

Public Hearing Held in Town of Morgan Amid Solar Project Pushback: A public hearing was held in the Town of Morgan, Oconto County on March 5 as residents pushed back against NextEra's plans for a 1,000-acre solar project. Residents raised concerns about panels taking up farmland and the potential for data centers to follow the large energy supply. "You may check your boxes and move on, but the people who are living with the consequences do not walk away reassured or respected, we walk away dismissed and invisible," said Jolene Barkhaus. NextEra personnel present at the meeting declined to comment. [WBAY, Mar 5 2026, link to article]

South-Central

TEXAS

Zelestra Breaks Ground on Two Texas Solar Projects Totaling 441 MW: Zelestra commenced construction in January 2026 on the 253 MWdc Echols Grove facility in Lamar County and the 188 MWdc Cedar Range facility in Hopkins County, with McCarthy Building Companies as lead EPC contractor. Both are backed by long-term PPAs with Meta as part of a broader 1.2 GWdc partnership. Some 704,000 bifacial modules will be installed across 2,400 acres, with full commercial operation expected by end of 2027. "Echols Grove and Cedar Range are two major projects in our partnership with Meta, which will see 1.2 GW of new clean energy capacity online in the U.S. soon," said Zelestra U.S. CEO Phil North. The projects will support 400+ local jobs and ~$20 million in local economic activity. [reNews, Mar 5 2026, link to article]

Plains

nothing to see here, folks

Pacific Northwest

nothing to see here, folks

ID/MT/WY

nothing to see here, folks

CO/NM/NV

nothing to see here, folks

AZ/UT

nothing to see here, folks

California

CPUC Orders Utilities to Procure 6 GW of Clean Capacity by 2032: The CPUC voted unanimously on Feb. 27 to order all state load-serving entities to procure 6 GW of additional non-fossil capacity from 2029–2032, citing anticipated reliability shortfalls from surging demand. The mandate requires 2 GW online by each of 2030, 2031, and 2032, with PG&E facing the largest individual obligation at 1,077 MW. LSEs must pursue "any viable projects that can still qualify for federal tax credits." Outgoing President Alice Reynolds called it a "thoughtfully balance[d]" order, while Commissioner Darcie Houck cautioned against "excess procurement that could needlessly add to ratepayer costs." Thursday's meeting was Reynolds' last before her appointment to the CAISO Board of Governors. [Utility Dive, Feb 27 2026, link to article]

Northeast

Mid-Atlantic

Southeast

Nothing to see here, folks!

Midwest

INDIANA

Public hearing date set for Clark County solar energy project: “As contentious debate over Clark County’s proposed solar project continues, the Clark County Board of Zoning Appeals has scheduled a public hearing to gather input from residents in support of and opposed to the project. Developed by Orion Renewables, the Idlewild Clark County Solar Project would be located in the northeast portion of the county east of New Washington…The public hearing is scheduled for March 18 at 5 p.m. at the Clark County Government Center, where the zoning board will allow comments ahead of a potential vote in a future meeting.” [News and Tribune, 03/05/2026, link to article].

North-Central

MINNESOTA

World’s ‘largest’ grid battery part of Google-Xcel Energy agreement: “Google and Xcel Energy said last week that they intend to build what Xcel calls “the largest battery project by gigawatt-hour energy capacity announced to date in the world” to support a data center in Minnesota powered by 1.6 GW of renewable energy…The agreement outlines the deployment of 1,400 MW of wind, 200 MW of solar and Form’s 300-MW battery on Xcel’s Upper Midwest grid…Google said it will pay all costs associated with the buildout through a new rate structure modeled after the Clean Transition Tariff…” [Utility Dive, 03/04/2025, link to article].

South-Central

TEXAS

Origis Energy secures $545 million for Texas solar and storage portfolio: “Miami-headquartered Origis Energy announced it has secured $545 million in project financing for a portfolio of solar and energy storage assets located in Texas. The funding, which includes construction and term debt as well as tax equity, will support the completion of three utility-scale projects. The funds are earmarked for three specific projects: the 150 MW solar plus 50 MW storage “Lone Star” facility, the 200 MW “Bluebonnet” solar project, and an additional 100 MW solar plus 100 MW storage expansion. All three projects are expected to reach commercial operation by the end of 2026.” [PV Magazine USA, 03/05/2026, link to article].

Plains

SOUTH DAKOTA

Pennington County Commission to vote on changes related to wind and solar energy: “The commission is now set to vote on changes to portions of the county's official Comprehensive Plan relating to utility-scale wind and solar zoning projects.” [Rapid City Journal, 3/5/2026, link paywalled, facebook post discussing article].

Daily Clips 3.5

To:

staff@greenlightamerica.org

Subject

Daily Clean Energy Clips 3/5/26

It’s rainy in D.C. while I’m writing this email, feel free to reply to this email with the weather you’re experiencing where you are right now! Happy Thursday & happy reading!

~ Rikki

Northeast

NEW YORK

Editorial: Take the Incremental Path: “Southold is the last town on the East End to still have a moratorium on applications for BESS systems in place. The town’s reasoning, at first, was that it was waiting for New York State to adopt new codes after several BESS fires throughout the state in 2023. The state adopted those codes in July of 2025, but we haven’t heard a peep since from Southold Town Hall about drafting its own BESS code, which could very easily be modeled on codes already in place in Riverhead and Southampton. The town’s BESS moratorium, extended each year since 2023, is due to expire this April, and the Town Board has just scheduled an April 21 public hearing on extending it for another year.” [East End Beacon, 03/03/2026, link to article].

Mid-Atlantic

Nothing to see here, folks!

Southeast

Nothing to see here, folks!

Midwest

INDIANA

Knox County Commissioners propose updates to solar ordinance: “Knox County Commissioners outlined proposed updates to the solar ordinance Tuesday, after hearing feedback from residents at a public comment meeting over the weekend…Commissioner President TJ Brink said he believes the ordinance needs stronger language and clearer standards. The first reading of the revised ordinance is scheduled for the April 7 meeting at 10 a.m. at Vincennes City Hall.” [We Are Knox County, 03/04/2026, link to article].

Posey County pushes back solar ordinance vote: “After a public hearing, Posey County Commissioners decided to push back on adopting new language that would allow for the development of new solar farms…Companies like Engie are looking to build new farms in Posey County. However advisor Elizabeth Bennett says if the new ordinance is accepted companies will leave and not invest in Posey County…Everyone who spoke at the commission meeting opposed further restrictions but there are those in the county who support it. Another hearing on the new language in the ordinance will take place April 7th in Posey County. a vote on the revised ordinance has not yet been set.” [WEHT Evansville, 03/03/2026, link to article]

North-Central

Nothing to see here, folks!

South-Central

KANSAS

Are Kansas wind, solar farms an economic boom or bust? “Lona DuVall, director of the Finney County Economic Development Corporation, said energy production is likely the future of the region, and her county can be at the forefront, if county residents choose to be…Just south of Finney County, Seward County Economic Development Director Eli Svaty said there are billions of dollars to be made in rural America right now…Neal Coffey is a resident of Seward County. He’s now retired, but spent his career in the energy industry, including gas, oil and even battery production. He has spoken at county commission meetings bringing up several worries when it comes to wind projects…Coffey feels counties are not properly prepared with regulations for the new companies coming in and setting up shop…A report from Climate Central found in 2024 that almost half of the energy produced in Kansas came from renewable sources, mostly wind turbines.” [The Topeka Capital-Journal, 03/03/2026, link to article].

Plains

Nothing to see here, folks!

Pacific Northwest

WASHINGTON

3/03 Klickitat County Commissioners meeting: “Klickitat County Commissioners voted yesterday to extend a moratorium on battery energy storage systems for another six months, allowing time for a legal review of a proposed ordinance on the subject.” [Gorge Country Media, 03/04/2026, link to article].

ID/MT/WY

MONTANTA

Montanans want more solar, natural gas development, are less interested in new coal plants: “The results of the poll, conducted in late December and early January, come as the Trump and Gianforte administrations work to expand domestic energy production amid a surge in demand wrought by growth in artificial intelligence and other electricity-intensive industries…Gov. Greg Gianforte and Montana’s federal delegation, all Republicans, are aligned with President Donald Trump in backing coal, which has fueled Montana’s largest power plant for more than 40 years. Jon Tester, the last Democrat to hold a statewide office, supported both renewable and fossil fuel energy sources during his tenure at the U.S. Senate, occasionally countering the Biden administration’s efforts to tighten emission regulations and restrict new federal coal leasing.” [Montana Free Press, 03/03/2026, link to article].

CO/NM/NV

Nothing to see here, folks!

AZ/UT

Nothing to see here, folks!

California

California orders utilities to add 6 GW of non-fossil capacity by 2032: “The California Public Utility Commission voted unanimously on Thursday to order state load-serving entities to procure a total additional 6 GW from 2029 to 2032 to cover an anticipated reliability shortfall resulting from demand growth…The order mandates that 2 GW be brought online by 2030, then the same in 2031 and 2032.” [UtilityDive, 02/27/2026, link to article].

Northeast

Nothing to see here, folks!

Mid-Atlantic

Southeast

Nothing to see here, folks!

Midwest

ILLINOIS

Logan county discusses data centers and battery storage “Logan County’s Zoning and Economic Development committee met Monday evening, and their discussion revolved around data centers and battery storage sites. They also added stipulations to their ordinance regarding data centers, specifically addressing insurance and decommissioning processes… DeRoss tells us public will factor into their final decision, along with the result of the third-party study.” [Fox Illinois, 3/2/26, link to article]

Pacific Northwest

WASHINGTON

Whitman County blocks wind farms, Harvest Hills looks for solutions “The Board of Whitman County Commissioners has adopted a new ordinance prohibiting locally permitted wind energy projects in the county. Steelhead Americas, the parent company of Harvest Hills Wind, released a statement today… In their statement, Harvest Hills acknowledged the ordinance as a roadblock but emphasized their commitment to pursuing a wind energy project. They plan to continue their efforts through the Washington State Energy Facilities Site Evaluation Council.” [KHQ, 3/2/26, link to article]

Daily Clean Energy Clips 03/02/3036


Northeast

MAINE

Greenville is being eyed for Maine’s latest battery storage project: “Officials in Greenville are considering a proposal to build a battery energy storage facility at a former steam plant…The local green light is a first step for a major energy project…Town Manager Mike Roy said that a Clean Maine Carbon official had put the town in touch with Ground Floor, it’s not clear how involved the company is with the project or how it may benefit from the battery facility to share the space…[Clean Maine Carbon President Patrick Jones said], ‘We’ve got 100 acres,’ he said. ‘If it works, the town likes it, everybody’s happy, then let’s see if we can’t do something in Greenville that’s positive.’” [The Maine Monitor, 02.25.2026, link to article].  

Mid-Atlantic

Nothing to see here, folks!

Southeast

GEORGIA & ALABAMA

Southern Company awarded $26.5B loan by Energy Department: “Development of electricity infrastructure by the Southern Company in Georgia and Alabama capable of generating up to 16 gigawatts of power will be funded through a $26.5 billion loan under Office of Energy Dominance Financing, said Energy Secretary Chris Wright…The Southern Company’s plans call for about 5 gigawatts of new gas-fired generation along with 6 gigawatts of nuclear power through upgrades and license renewals for eight existing reactors.

The funds will also be used to modernize hydroelectric power generation, in the development of battery storage systems, and on transmission and grid enhancement projects, according to the Department of Energy.” [Regional Media News, 02/27/2026, link to article]

Midwest

Nothing to see here, folks!

North-Central

IOWA

LETTER: Clinton County's wind ordinance detrimental to taxpayers: “In many Iowa counties, wind energy development has become a stable and significant source of property tax revenue. Counties that host wind projects routinely receive millions of dollars annually from turbine valuations…The importance of this revenue becomes even clearer under the state’s new property tax limitations…With the ordinance now in place, Clinton County has removed one of the few remaining tools available to expand its tax base at a time when financial flexibility is already constrained.” [Clinton Herald, 02/25/2026, link to LTE]

MINNESOTA

Community forum focuses on future of nuclear power in Minnesota – Brainerd Dispatch: “Currently, there’s a moratorium on building new nuclear power plants in Minnesota. But to meet future power demands, experts say that rule should be reconsidered. Nuclear power was the subject of a Monday, Feb. 23, Rosenmeier Forum at Central Lakes College in Brainerd…Currently, Minnesota has three nuclear power plants, all owned by Xcel Energy — one in Monticello and two at Prairie Island near Red Wing…Blaine asked whether the mandate to be 100% clean energy by 2040, signed into law by Gov. Tim Walz in February of 2023, would be possible without nuclear energy. Hult said it was theoretically possible but in reality it would be a stretch to reach that goal.” [BYTESEU, 02/27/2026, link to article]

South-Central

TEXAS

AES, Google ink energy deal for new Texas data center: “The AES Corp. announced Feb. 24 that it has reached agreements to develop new energy generation projects that will be co-located with a new Google data center in Wilbarger County, Texas…The projects are designed to power the planned data center campus while minimizing strain on the local electric grid.” [Daily Energy Insider, 02.26.2026, link to article]

CO/NM/NV

COLORADO

Heads up Logan County -- County Commissioners reviewing wind farm regs: “PUBLIC HEARING There will be a meeting of the Logan County Commissioners on Tuesday, March 17, 2026 at 9:30 AM, in the Logan County Courthouse, 2nd floor meeting room, 315 Main Street, Sterling, Colorado, to review the Logan County Wind Energy Facility Regulations” [Colorado Accountability Project, 02/27/2026, link to notice]

AZ/UT

Nothing to see here, folks!

California

Is the future of Puente Hills Mall in batteries and data?: “The Puente Hills Mall, once immortalized as a filming location in the Hollywood classic "Back to the Future" and now mostly-shuttered, may soon become home to battery energy storage facilities and data centers, sparking environmental and economic concerns from nearby communities…The council voted unanimously to change zoning laws to allow battery energy storage systems at sites like the former mall…City officials declined on-camera interviews but acknowledged plans are in theory works for data centers at the Puente Hills Mall property and other locations. But for now, they say the city's primary focus is battery energy storage.” {ABC 7 Los Angeles, 02/26/2026, link to article]

Daily Clips 2.27

To:

staff@greenlightamerica.org

Subject

Daily Clean Energy Clips 2/27/26


Northeast

Nothing to see here, folks!

Mid-Atlantic

PENNSYLVANIA

Protect McKean County from RWE’s 480 MW Stargazer Solar & Transmission Project: Residents and landowners launched a Change.org petition on February 25 demanding an immediate halt to RWE Solar Development LLC’s proposed 480 MW utility-scale solar project and associated transmission lines across Keating, Hamlin, Norwich and Sergeant Townships in McKean County. The petition states, “We, the residents, landowners, camp owners, sportsmen, business owners, and families who cherish McKean County, Pennsylvania, demand that the RWE Stargazer Solar Project be stopped immediately.” It warns of permanent conversion of hardwood forests, trout streams, dark skies and rural character near the Allegheny National Forest, noting RWE has already secured land options, leases and easements since 2024. Petitioners add, “We are not asking for adjustments. We are not asking for mitigation. We are demanding that this project be stopped.” [Change.org, Feb 25 2026, link to article]

Southeast

Nothing to see here, folks!

Midwest

INDIANA

Dearborn County approves one-year moratorium on solar farms, battery storage and data centers: After a packed public meeting that lasted several hours at South Dearborn High School, Dearborn County Commissioners approved a 12-month moratorium on all new applications for solar farms, battery storage facilities and data centers. The decision follows Linea Energy’s (San Francisco) proposal for a large solar + battery storage project on 1,000 acres in the Manchester area, which triggered anti-solar signs across the county and neighboring areas. No applications can be submitted during the ban. [WRBI Radio, Feb 26 2026, link to article]

North-Central

MINNESOTA

Gigantic Form Energy iron-air battery to power Google data center in Minnesota: Form Energy will build a 300 MW / 30 GWh iron-air battery system in Pine Island, Minnesota, under a deal with Xcel Energy to deliver round-the-clock clean power to a Google data center, with the ability to dispatch for up to 100 hours straight. Google is funding 1.4 GW of new wind and 200 MW of solar to charge the batteries and covering all new grid infrastructure costs. Form Energy CEO Mateo Jaramillo said, “They don’t treat us like mom and give us cookies when we feel bad — they hold us to a very high standard.” Batteries are expected to start delivery in 2028, with full clean-energy installations phasing in through 2031. [Canary Media, Feb 24 2026, link to article]

South-Central

Nothing to see here, folks!

Plains

Nothing to see here, folks!

Pacific Northwest

Nothing to see here, folks!

ID/MT/WY

Nothing to see here, folks!

CO/NM/NV

COLORADO

Utility-scale battery energy storage facility planned in Hayden: Jupiter Power is developing a 400 MW standalone battery energy storage system on a 72-acre site (30–40 acre battery footprint) on Routt County Road 51 southwest of the Hayden Power Station. The project, approved by the Colorado PUC last week as part of 10 bid projects, will sell power to Xcel Energy under a power purchase agreement and connect via a new transmission line to the Mount Harris substation. Hayden Town Manager Mathew Mendisco said, “Development like this is always good… We are excited to have land near the airport developed, and we think this seems to be a good fit.” Construction is expected to start in 2027 with commercial operations targeted for 2028. [Steamboat Pilot, Feb 25 2026, link to article]

Solar power taking only tiny fraction of Colorado farmland despite fears, industry study shows: A new COSSA industry study shows all ground-mounted solar projects covered just 14,326 acres across 39 counties at the end of 2024 (utility-scale ≥1 MW used 12,601 acres), less than 1% of the state’s 30.2 million farmland acres; another 6,700 acres were added in 2025. COSSA CEO KC Becker stated, “Farmland isn’t being lost to renewable energy, it’s being lost to suburban sprawl,” and noted that even the 60,000–80,000 acres needed for 12,000 MW of large-scale solar by 2050 would equal only about one-tenth of one percent of all land in Colorado. Examples include an 80 MW array on 475 acres in Delta County (later reworked for sheep grazing) and a 100 MW project on 640 acres in San Miguel County that was abandoned due to opposition. [Colorado Sun, Feb 24 2026, link to article]

Massive solar project proposed where crops once grew: NextEra Energy has proposed the Spud Valley Energy Center — a 600 MW solar + 600 MW battery storage project on 2,578 acres of former cropland near Mosca and Hooper in Alamosa County. Project manager Evan Reimondo said a number of participating landowners “have already either retired their wells or they’re participating in CREP to rest their lands,” and added, “Colorado’s demand for electricity is going to keep growing… By permitting for 600, it gives us that future flexibility.” The initial 200 MW / 200 MW phase targets operation by the end of 2029, with full buildout later as transmission improves; the county is now reviewing the 1041 permit application. [Alamosa Citizen, Feb 22 2026, link to article]

AZ/UT

Nothing to see here, folks!

California

Nothing to see here, folks!

SOUTH CAROLINA

[Change.org Petition] Call on Williamsburg County Council to amend our solar ordinance: “We, the undersigned residents and concerned citizens of Williamsburg County, respectfully call on our County Council to immediately amend the ordinance permitting a 12,000-acre solar farm development in our community...We are asking the Williamsburg County Council to amend this ordinance, eliminate all wetland acres from the total percentage, extend the minimum distance to the nearest residence, eliminate all fee in lieu of taxes for current solar and any future solar or battery storage. We also demand a public response as to why the project was concealed by a code name for the first two readings and only changed after the public comment period and minutes before third reading. A 180 day moratorium to all solar and Battery storage…” [Change.org, 02/24/2026, link to petition].

Northeast

Nothing to see here, folks!

Mid-Atlantic

Nothing to see here, folks!

Southeast

Nothing to see here, folks!

Midwest

MICHIGAN

Huron County residents rally against solar farms and data centers: “As Huron County officials weigh zoning changes and potential proposals for renewable energy developments on farmland, a small but passionate group of agriculture-minded residents has stepped forward…When asked to rank which projects the group is most concerned about, they said solar farms and data centers worry them most because of land footprint and the potential for significant water demand to cool the data centers…One of the group’s main concerns is what happens if farmland continues to disappear…During the Feb. 4, 2026…the Planning Commission voted to recommend two amendments to the Huron County Zoning Ordinance following a lengthy public hearing.The amendments were scheduled to go to the county board of commissioners for review and a vote Feb. 24.” [Midland Daily News, 02.24.2026, link to article]

INDIANA

Tweet: “2/23/2026 ➡️ By unanimous vote, Huntington County passed a moratorium. This halts all Data Centers, CO2 capture, and Battery Energy Storage Systems projects until the County has adequate time to hear from the public and draft proper ordinances.” [Twitter Account: @MikeThompsonIN, 02.23.2026, link to post]

Dearborn County Commission recommends year-long moratorium on solar farms: “The Dearborn County Planning Commission voted nearly unanimously Monday to recommend a 12-month moratorium on all solar project developments and data center proposals…The vote came after hundreds of people filled the South Dearborn High School auditorium and debated solar farm construction in the county for hours…Linea Energy is planning what could be a 1,200-acre solar farm in Manchester Township…Development director Chris Barry, whom we've talked to in previous reports, addressed the crowd during a half-hour-long presentation about the projects…The Planning Commission's moratorium recommendation will now be considered by the full Dearborn County Commission at a special meeting on Tuesday at 6 p.m. at the Dearborn County Government Center.” [WCPO Cincinnati, 02.23.2026, link to article]

North-Central

WISCONSIN

Dunn County residents share thoughts on massive solar project: “Muddy Creek Solar LLC is seeking approval to construct a 322-megawatt solar farm in the town of Spring Brook. The facility would span 128 parcels of land owned by 28 local families. As of Feb. 23, all public comments submitted by area residents have become part of a permanent legal record…No date has been set for a final ruling. The Muddy Creek Solar project is planned to begin construction in 2027, pending PSC approval.” [WEAU 13 News, 02.24.2026, link to article]

Daily Clips 2.24

To:

staff@greenlightamerica.org

Subject

Daily Clean Energy Clips Month/Date/Year

Northeast

NEW YORK

New moratorium on BESS development advances in Newfane: “The Newfane town board is moving to call another six-month moratorium on local siting of battery energy storage systems (BESS)...A public hearing on the proposed local law declaring the moratorium will be held at 6:45 p.m. Feb. 25, next Wednesday, ahead of the board’s 7 p.m. business meeting at the town hall. The board imposed a six-month hold on local BESS development in March 2025 and did not extend it, nor was a local law drafted to regulate BESS facilities. The new moratorium comes after feedback from the town’s fire board, and as three town-approved, 5mW solar energy generation projects move to the construction stage and a fourth project is pending.” [Lockport Union-Sun & Journal, 02/20/2026, link to article]

Mid-Atlantic

VIRGINIA

Board sets March 9 hearing on solar ordinance: “The Patrick County Board of Supervisors scheduled a joint public hearing with the Patrick County Planning Commission for its March 9 meeting to consider a proposed solar energy ordinance…The Patrick County Board of Supervisors scheduled a joint public hearing with the Patrick County Planning Commission for its March 9 meeting to consider a proposed solar energy ordinance.” [The Enterprise, 02/17/2026, link to article].

Southeast

Nothing to see here, folks!

Midwest

MICHIGAN

Bushnell Twp. Board tables DTE battery energy storage system request until March: “After hearing questions and concerns from residents Tuesday night, the Bushnell Township Board voted to table until March a request from DTE Energy to build a battery storage facility near Sheridan. DTE is requesting a special use permit for the energy system in Bushnell Township’s portion of the Fish Creek Solar Park.” [Daily News, 02/20/2026, link to article]

North-Central

Nothing to see here, folks!

South-Central

TEXAS

Retired EV Batteries Scored a New Gig: Bolstering Texas’ Grid: “An excess of energy produced across Texas, largely due to the state’s solar and wind fleet, signals it’s a good time to buy. It’s then that 500 batteries, which once fueled General Motors’ electric vehicles, charge up…The 24 megawatt-hour battery project is the first site online in Texas for B2U Storage Solutions, a California-based company. The firm plans to get three more battery sites connected to the Texas grid, bringing the total capacity to 100 megawatt hours of storage across Texas.

Nothing to see here, folks!

Pacific Northwest

WASHINGTON

Yakima County Commissioners nearing ordinance regarding solar energy usage: “Yakima County Commissioners are actively working towards an ordinance that would allow solar facilities on some farmland in Yakima County…Commissioner Amanda McKinney says that the board should seek more legal analysis before reaching a decision.” [kimatv.com, 02/19/2026, link to article]

Daily Clips 2.20

To:

staff@greenlightamerica.org

Subject

Daily Clean Energy Clips 2/20/26


Northeast

NEW YORK

Somerset Solar development looking for IDA incentives: The proposed 125-megawatt Somerset Solar Project on 700 acres at the former Somerset Generation Station (a decommissioned coal plant) in Niagara County is seeking $12.5 million in Niagara County Industrial Development Agency tax incentives. The $276.6 million project already received its state siting permit in 2024. [Niagara Gazette, Feb 19 2026, link to article]

Lysander Town Board Meeting on Feb 19: Hearings on Energy Moratoriums: The Lysander Town Board will hold public hearings tonight (Feb. 19) on extending both the battery energy storage system moratorium and the solar farm moratorium through June 20, 2026. Work session begins at 6 p.m., regular meeting at 6:30 p.m. at 8220 Loop Road. [NewsBreak, Feb 19 2026, link to article]

Mid-Atlantic

Nothing to see here, folks!

Southeast

Nothing to see here, folks!

Midwest

ILLINOIS

Board delays vote on Pontoon Beach solar farm: The Madison County Board voted Wednesday night to postpone action on a permit for Arena Renewables’ proposed 3-megawatt solar farm along Route 162 in Pontoon Beach. The zoning board of appeals had recommended denial; concerns included possible disturbance of Native-American artifacts. Developer Sam Walch highlighted the project’s financial benefits while resident Jim Greenwald voiced neighborhood worries. [Advantage News, Feb 19 2026, link to article]

North-Central

Nothing to see here, folks!

South-Central

Nothing to see here, folks!

Plains

NORTH DAKOTA

Stutsman County Commission approves solar farm ordinance: On Feb. 17 the Stutsman County Commission approved zoning ordinance amendments for solar energy facilities by a 4-1 vote, setting a 1,320-foot setback from occupied residences (down from the proposed half-mile). The changes pave the way for New Leaf Energy’s 247-megawatt Buffalo solar project on 1,600 acres north of Jamestown (construction possible summer 2028). Commissioner Levi Taylor said, “I’m not so much concerned about the noise… as much as what it looks like.” Developer William Dean called a half-mile setback “overly prescriptive” and “a denial” of the project. [Jamestown Sun, Feb 18 2026, link to article]

Pacific Northwest

WASHINGTON

Grant Co. solar projects moving forward: Multiple solar projects are advancing in Grant County, including the 400-MW Dry Falls Solar near Coulee City (conditional use permit issued, negotiating BPA contract) and the 300-MW Appledale Solar northwest of Quincy (conditional use permit received). Planning Director Jim Anderson-Cook noted “overwhelming support at the public hearing” for one project, adding support from local labor unions. Royal Slope Solar (260 MW) is targeting an April hearing. [Tri-City Herald, Feb 18 2026, link to article]

Daily Clips 2.19

To:

staff@greenlightamerica.org

Subject

Daily Clean Energy Clips 2/19/26


Northeast

RHODE ISLAND

Denial of special permit for battery storage facility reversed: Superior Court Judge Jeffrey A. Lanphear vacated the Smithfield zoning board’s denial of a special use permit for a battery energy storage facility off Mountaindale Road. GDM ESS, LLC filed its master plan application Nov. 22, 2023 (certified complete Dec. 17, 2023) before the town’s Feb. 9, 2024 ordinance amendment prohibiting energy storage. The judge stated, “The Master Plan Application is unmistakably an application for development... This entitled the project to the protections of §45-24-44.” The board must now consider the permit under the ordinances effective Dec. 17, 2023. [RI Lawyers Weekly, Feb 16 2026, link to article]

Mid-Atlantic

Nothing to see here, folks!

Southeast

Nothing to see here, folks!

Midwest

MICHIGAN

870-acre solar farm proposed in Ingham County: A 90-megawatt, $100 million Acceleration Solar Project on 873 acres across Leslie, Vevay and Onondaga townships in Ingham County is under review by the Michigan Public Service Commission. Leslie Township Supervisor Dallas Henney stated, “We can’t do much to stop it. To be honest with you, there’s not a lot you can do, you just try to work with them.” Ranger Power’s Brady Friss said, “Once operational, the project will produce enough energy to power an estimated 20,000 homes.” Resident Amy Clark wrote to the MPSC, “I know the majority of the community absolutely does not want these industrial solar sites taking over the farmland in our community,” citing property values, drainage, fire hazards and wildlife. Construction is set for 2027 with operations in 2028; the project is expected to generate $25 million in tax revenue. [Lansing State Journal, Feb 16 2026, link to article]

Dorr Township officials begin preliminary discussion on BESS, residents voice concerns: Dorr Township planning commission held a preliminary discussion on battery energy storage systems amid resident concerns linked to a proposed Microsoft data center. One resident stated, “When you told us to not bring up or mention the data systems or the AI data center for the campus that’s potentially coming here, it’s directly related to the BESS systems. In fact, they need them because they draw so much power, the same kind of power we’ve been warning you about.” No vote was taken; the commission is drafting a BESS-specific zoning ordinance under state law PA 233 with a future public hearing planned. [WWMT, Feb 17 2026, link to article]

INDIANA

Starke County approves new moratoriums on solar projects, battery energy storage systems: Starke County Commissioners unanimously approved new moratoriums on solar projects and battery energy storage systems through July 25. A plan commission representative stated they have been “actively hashing out language for future solar projects but still need more time and expect to have everything finalized in May,” at which point the moratorium would lift for updated zoning ordinances; the prior moratoriums were set to expire this week. [ABC57, Feb 17 2026, link to article]

North-Central

IOWA

Iowa bill seeks to end constantly blinking red lights on wind turbines: A House subcommittee advanced House File 2081 requiring wind turbines to use aircraft detection lighting systems instead of constantly blinking red lights. Rep. Dean Fisher, R-Montour, stated, “Constituents who used to have just a nice sunset view out their window now are looking at a row of flashing red lights all night long. Very annoying, very disturbing.” The bill would mandate new wind farms install sensor-based lights and existing turbines retrofit by 2028. About 27% of Iowa’s turbines already have the systems; the Sierra Club and Iowa Environmental Council support the measure with no opposition registered. [Iowa Capital Dispatch, Feb 16 2026, link to article]

MISSOURI

Neighbors pack hearing over proposed battery storage site in north Greene County: Neighbors packed a hearing over a proposed battery energy storage facility on 40 acres in north Greene County near Farm Road 84 and Rocky Lane, featuring 242 battery containers covering 16.5 acres. Opponent Gayle Emery stated, “A fire event of this type could put this company completely bankrupt immediately... We will be left holding the bag for maintaining and getting rid of this thing and cleaning up.” Tracy Ojakangas added, “We would be responsible for most of the things that will happen in the fire department... So, our taxes will go up probably … And also our insurance would go up because we would have to insure our property and our buildings.” Developer Sofos Power pledged collaboration with local fire departments on prevention plans and highlighted millions in tax revenue and jobs. The Greene County Planning Board will make the final decision. [Yahoo News, Feb 18 2026, link to article]

South-Central

TEXAS

Clearway energises USD-685m solar-storage complex in Texas: “Clearway Energy Group brought online the Pine Forest Energy Center in Hopkins County, a combined 300 MW solar project paired with 200 MW of battery storage. The company said the project totaled a $685 million investment and is expected to generate ‘over USD 58 million in property tax revenue’ over the next 20 years. The article notes the solar output is contracted via virtual power purchase agreements, and the standalone BESS is intended to ‘enhance grid reliability’ by shifting energy to higher-demand periods.” [Renewables Now, Feb 18 2026, link to article]

Plains

South Dakota just approved its largest wind farm ever: The South Dakota Public Utilities Commission approved its largest wind farm ever — the 333-MW Philip Wind Project in Haakon County — on Feb. 12 via settlement agreement. The project includes up to 87 turbines at a $750 million cost, a 5.5-mile 230-kV transmission line and other infrastructure; construction could start as soon as June 2026. PUC vice chairperson Kristie Fiegen said, “That meeting gave us an opportunity to get additional information, make changes, and develop conditions that have made this project better.” Commission chair Chris Nelson stated, “The developer met the legal requirements for approval.” [Electrek, Feb 17 2026, link to article]

Pacific Northwest

2.18 Daily Clips

To:

staff@greenlightamerica.org

Subject

Daily Clean Energy Clips 2/18/26


Northeast

NEW YORK

Clymer extends battery storage moratorium “The Clymer Town Board voted unanimously to push its battery‑storage moratorium out another year, to Jan. 31 2027, saying they’re waiting for state guidance and don’t want to permit large battery projects without clear rules. Supervisor Brian Willink admitted they’re ‘basically kicking it another 12 months down the road,’ but board members noted there’s nothing stopping them from renewing the moratorium annually . The extension means any battery‑storage proposals will remain on hold until at least early 2027 while officials study regulations and monitor state actions .” [Post‑Journal, Feb 17 2026, link to article]

Ulster Town hearing on Terra‑Gen battery storage plan to include adjacent Central Hudson land “Ulster officials have scheduled a scoping hearing for Terra‑Gen’s proposed 250‑MW lithium‑ion battery energy storage facility at the former Coleman High School. The hearing will also consider adding a strip of land owned by utility Central Hudson to the site, meaning the project could cover roughly 15 acres with ‘a 250‑megawatt lithium‑ion battery energy storage system’ on 12 of those acres. The scoping session is the first step in the state environmental review; residents and agencies can suggest issues for the forthcoming draft environmental impact statement, and the project will need further town approvals.” [Daily Freeman, Feb 16 2026, link to article]

Mid-Atlantic

VIRGINIA

Neighbors concerned about impact of Botetourt County wind farm “Botetourt County’s Rocky Forge Wind project is Virginia’s first utility‑scale wind farm, with 13 turbines just under 80 MW that will power about 20,000 homes . Local bed‑and‑breakfast owners Parke and Jennifer Slater fear the towers will mar views and hurt their business: ‘We have people that come from all over the world… and with the wind farms coming, we haven’t had anybody who has said … do you have wind farms that we can come and see?’ . Construction crews have built roads and foundations and will begin erecting turbines this spring; Apex Clean Energy says it has worked with the county on road upgrades and expects the wind farm to be fully operational by year‑end despite continued protests .” [WDBJ7, Feb 16 2026, link to article]

Southeast

Nothing to see here, folks!

Midwest

MICHIGAN

Fayette Township begins review process for proposed solar ordinance “After months of protests over the 1,300‑acre Heartwood II solar project, the Fayette Township Board voted to send its Compatible Renewable Energy Ordinance to the planning commission. Resident Stephen Oleszkowicz called the unanimous vote ‘a change in tide’ because the ordinance would let the township set siting and safety rules instead of deferring to the state . The commission will hold a public hearing, incorporate community feedback and then return the ordinance for adoption; without a local ordinance, developer Ranger Power could bypass the township and seek direct state approval .” [WILX, Feb 17 2026, link to article]

Amid resolute opposition to new solar farm, township makes move to retain local control “About 100 residents packed a Feb. 16 meeting in Fayette Township to oppose the 140‑MW Heartwood Solar II project, which would put panels on roughly 1,000 acres . The board voted to ask the planning commission to review a compatible renewable‑energy ordinance and hold a public hearing ‘as soon as practical’ ; Supervisor Nate Baker recused himself because his father leased land to the developer . Without local rules, Ranger Power can petition the Michigan Public Service Commission for approval under a 2023 state law, so residents say adopting the ordinance quickly is the only way to keep the project under township control .” [MLive, Feb 17 2026, link to article]

North-Central

Nothing to see here, folks!

South-Central

Nothing to see here, folks!

Plains

Nothing to see here, folks!

Pacific Northwest

WASHINGTON

Will farmers be able to ‘farm the sun’ on agricultural land in Yakima? “Yakima County commissioners are considering rules to allow agrivoltaics — solar panels raised high enough to graze livestock or grow crops underneath — on previously unfarmable land . More than a dozen people spoke in favor at a Feb. 15 meeting; Yakima resident Jean Mendoza said agrivoltaics could help the state meet energy needs, noting that ‘whenever we start looking at something new, there’s a trial‑and‑error period’ . Property owner Jacob Jones proposed a 2‑MW project combining solar and sheep grazing, arguing it would improve soil health and give low‑income residents access to community‑solar credits ; commissioners sent the proposal to county attorneys and said the goal is to protect prime farmland while possibly exempting unfarmable plots .” [OPB, Feb 15 2026, link to article]

ID/MT/WY

WYOMING

Wyoming moves to pull the plug on Pronghorn and Sidewinder wind projects “On Feb. 5 the Wyoming State Land Board voted 3–2 to start a legal process that could cancel leases for the Pronghorn H2 and Sidewinder wind projects on state lands; Secretary of State Chuck Gray, Superintendent Megan Degenfelder and Auditor Kristi Racines supported the motion, while Gov. Mark Gordon and Treasurer Curt Meier dissented . Gray cited a judge’s ruling that the board broke its own rules when approving the Pronghorn lease, and Racines said both leases may be illegal . Developer Paul Martin of Focus Clean Energy said they invested millions and would contest the move but won’t sue yet because the board opted for due process rather than outright cancellation; critics warn a reversal could trigger a multibillion‑dollar breach‑of‑contract claim .” [Cowboy State Daily, Feb 5 2026, link to article]

OHIO

A new Ohio bill could be a de facto statewide ban on solar and wind “A bill now working its way through the Republican-controlled Ohio legislature threatens to raise even more barriers for wind power and solar farms. On Tuesday, the Ohio Senate’s Energy Committee held its third hearing on Senate Bill 294… The bill would declare it to be state policy ​“in all cases” for new electricity-generation facilities to ​“employ affordable, reliable, and clean energy sources.” But the bill’s definitions not only veer from common usage in ways that would exclude renewables but also threaten to block wind and solar development altogether.” [Canary Media, 2/12/26, link to article]

IOWA

Lawmakers seek to standardize county regulation of renewable energy facilities “A House subcommittee advanced a bill Wednesday that would create standard language for counties to adopt pertaining to setbacks, moratoriums, property tax and other siting considerations for renewable electric power generation facilities. Rep. Shannon Lundgren, R-Peosta, who chaired the committee, said the bill would help renewable energy developers know that “we are open for business here in the state of Iowa” which she said is essential to meet energy demands in the state.” [Iowa Capital Dispatch, 2/11/26, link to article]

SOUTH DAKOTA

South Dakota PUC gives go ahead to Philip Wind Project “The South Dakota Public Utilities Commission accepted a joint settlement stipulation, submitted by the PUC staff and Philip Wind Partners, LLC, and granted a permit to construct the Philip Wind Project at the PUC’s regular meeting Feb. 12, 2026, in Pierre. The proposed project, spanning 68,300 acres in Haakon County, will be capable of generating 333 megawatts of energy and will cost approximately $750 million to construct. The project plans to include up to 87 turbines and an approximate 5.5-mile long 230-kV generator transmission tie line.” [SD PUC, 2/13/26, link to press release]

Solar-farm freeze continues in Davenport as council grants extension “The Davenport City Council voted to extend a moratorium on new solar farms and freestanding solar panels Wednesday… until June 25... Officials said no one has reached out to the city about potential solar farms or freestanding solar so far. The moratorium will end early if leaders approve changes to solar regulations before then.” [KWQC, 2/11/26, link to article]

NEBRASKA

Friends, foes of wind both impact rewrites “Tuesday night's public hearings on a combined 449 pages of Lincoln County zoning documents focused entirely on one subject: wind farms. Seven speakers urged the county's Planning Commission to take their side, including landowners who have agreed to lease space inside the footprint of a proposed 52-turbine project north of Maxwell and Brady. After two hours, the board had twice voted 7-0 to recommend approval of updates to the county's 2012 comprehensive plan and zoning regulations. County commissioners will take them up after final public hearings March 16 in the county courthouse's district courtroom in North Platte.” [North Platte Telegraph, 2/12/26, link to article, link to text]

Northeast

NEW YORK

Northampton Town Board holds tense public hearing for lithium battery facility “Tonight at a meeting of the Northampton Town Board they held public comment with over 100 concerned residents and advocates in attendance on a proposed lithium battery storage system. The board agreed unanimously to vote on whether to hold a moratorium in March according to advocate Sara Will-White. The Adirondack Park Agency and Northampton Planning Board had already approved plans to install a lithium battery storage system at 1030 Ny-30 for the developer CarsonPower before the February 10 meeting. In response a public petition by Sara Will-White to oppose the facility gathered over 1,000 signatures as of February 10.” [News 10, 2/11/26, link to article]

MASSACHUSETTS

City Council passes first reading on BESS moratorium “After a public hearing and recommendation by the Planning Board, the [Westfield] City Council held a second public hearing on the moratorium for battery energy storage systems on Feb. 5, followed by a unanimous vote to approve the interim restriction on projects through Sept. 30. A second reading is scheduled for Feb. 19.” [MassLive, 2/11/26, link to article]

The commission will review the case at its Austin headquarters.” [Covering Katy News, 2/10/26, link to article]

MICHIGAN

Opposition to solar expansion continues at Fayette Township Board meeting in Jonesville “In Hillsdale County, neighbors are pushing back against Ranger Power's "Heartwood 2" solar project. The project is a proposed expansion into hundreds of acres on the East side of Fayette Township. Monday, Fayette Township's Board chose a legal firm to help draft a solar ordinance to regulate such developments. Ranger Power submitted a letter to the Board that suggests an appeal to the state for an override of local authorities under Public Act 233 may be in the works.” [Fox 47 News, 2/9/26, link to article]

Considering a one-year moratorium on data centers, battery storage facilities “The City of Portage is considering a one-year moratorium on all new data center and battery energy storage system (BESS) facility developments until officials are able to review potential impacts, as well as zoning rules and ordinances. City leaders say the move is proactive, but follows similar projects being proposed in communities surrounding Kalamazoo and across Michigan. The proposed moratorium could last up to 12 months if approved.” [WWMT, 2/10/26, link to article]

North-Central

WISCONSIN

'We will not be railroaded': Wheatland residents push back as battery storage project could return “Dozens of Wheatland residents packed a town board meeting, speaking out against a proposed 200-megawatt lithium-ion battery energy storage facility they believed had already been stopped… The proposed facility would be built on farmland near homes and a grade school. While the Wheatland town board previously voted against the project, Robin Energy Storage withdrew its application — but told TMJ4 its next steps include to resubmit.” [TMJ4, 2/9/26, link to article]

NEW YORK

New Hartford Town Board institutes solar moratorium to evaluate law on large-scale projects “The New Hartford Town Board has imposed a temporary moratorium on large-scale solar projects in the town, which will allow it to review its existing law and make any needed changes.” [Daily Sentinel, 2/4/26, link to article]

Energy storage facility proposed on Trudeau Road “An energy company is proposing an energy storage battery facility on Trudeau Road. The St. Armand town board is considering adopting a one-year moratorium prohibiting these types of facilities, to give them time to gather information about the pros and cons and to make an informed decision… The company, Carson Power, is scheduled to have representatives attend a town board meeting on Feb. 19 for an informational presentation. Town Supervisor Davina Thurston said the board will also vote on the moratorium that night.” [Adirondack Daily Enterprise, 2/7/26, link to article]

GEORGIA

County Officials Provide Update on SPLOST Vote, Solar Moratorium, and Clean Annual Audit “The video also addressed the commission’s vote to extend the county’s moratorium on solar farms for an additional 90 days. Officials said the extension is designed to give the county more time to review solar regulations and consider potential updates to ordinances and permitting.” [The Mountain-Valley Independent, 2/6/2026, link to article]

Midwest

MICHIGAN

Quincy Twp. residents object to battery storage facility “Residents questioned developer EDF Renewables, but Zoning Administrator Andrew Craig said it wasn't the appropriate time. Craig explained that the Joint Planning Commission of the village and township is drafting an ordinance to regulate energy storage, and questions to EDF will be answered after its adoption, when EDF applies for a permit. EDF representatives outlined the proposed Santina Energy battery storage project” [Coldwater Daily Reporter, 2/7/2026, link to article]

Solar, battery ordinance amendments advance in Huron County, head to commissioners “The Huron County Planning Commission approved two amendments to the county ordinance governing utility-scale solar energy systems and battery energy storage during its Feb. 4 meeting. The amended ordinance will now be sent to the Huron County Board of Commissioners for review. The amendments added a host community requirement for utility-scale solar and battery energy storage projects and require developers to submit project completion reports to the county.” [Huron Daily Tribune, 2/6/26, link to article]

MISSOURI

Missouri declares war on solar “A Missouri State Senator, with vocal support from the Governor, has submitted a bill stopping all solar construction immediately, and placing a moratorium on all new solar construction starts until December 31, 2027 – or when new rules are developed by the state.” [PV Magazine, 2/6/2026, link to article]

South-Central

TEXAS

Vote set to halt East Texas lithium battery storage construction “Van Zandt County residents are one step closer to winning a long-fought battle against a lithium battery storage project being built right in their backyards, ahead of a Commissioners Court vote to pause construction… Residents are raising safety concerns and fears that, if prohibited items are used, they could cause a lithium fire at the facility… On Wednesday, the Van Zandt County commissioners court will vote to pause construction on all projects until the Attorney General issues a final determination.” [KETK, 2/5/2026, link to article]

OREGON

Oregon bill seeks to temporarily fast-track siting of renewable energy projects “Oregon lawmakers are considering a bill that would fast-track siting and permitting of renewable energy projects. House Bill 4031 would temporarily give counties power to grant renewable energy permits that are currently issued by the state. This could cut siting and permitting times significantly, according to supporters of the bill, and could help developers qualify for federal tax credits that can reduce overall costs by up to 30%.” [OPB, 2/4/2026, link to article]

Running Weekly Clips

Weekly Template 06/22/26 - 06/26/26

To:

staff@greenlightamerica.org

Subject

Weekly big picture news and trends 06/22/26 - 06/26/26

Clean energy trends and big picture news

Solar uses just 0.07% of US farmland, new SEIA map shows “The Solar Energy Industries Association (SEIA) has launched a new interactive map showing that solar energy occupies just 0.07% of US farmland and 0.04% of the country’s total land area, highlighting the relatively small footprint of solar development compared with other common land uses.The analysis also found that there is no state in the country where solar development occupies more than 0.5% of prime farmland. ” [Review Energy, June 22, 2026, link to article]
