But another state is seeing storage expand quickly as it looks to store more of its abundant, cheap solar power for later. Arizona saw blistering growth in utility-scale battery capacity last year, more than doubling its fleet to a total of 4.7 gigawatts at the end of 2025, according to U.S. Energy Information Administration data analyzed by research firm Cleanview. The two leading states each installed far more capacity last year than Arizona did, but neither of these more mature markets grew as quickly. California expanded its fleet by 29%, to 15.2 GW, while Texas’ grew by 69%, pushing it to just over 14 GW of total installed capacity.” [Canary Media, 2/12/26, link to article]

Why Texas Is Outpacing New York in Renewable Energy Development “Over the last two years, Texas added more wind and solar power than New York has in the last two decades, a gap that has increased even as the mandates required by the state's 2019 Climate Act are looming. Those mandates, which Gov. Kathy Hochul wants to delay, include having an energy system relying entirely on clean energy by 2040. But nearly seven years after they were established, the state isn't on track to hit that mandate or its near-term target of converting the grid to 70% renewable energy by 2030. Texas never set ambitious clean energy goals, but is building faster than New York, in part because of deregulation, limited local opposition and investments into the infrastructure that carries power from rural parts of the state to urban cores. Other states leading in renewable energy development, including Kansas, Oklahoma and Florida, have no state clean energy mandates.” [Governing, 3/11/26, link to article]

Utilities are spending billions on the data center boom. What are the risks? “The data centers being planned and built across the U.S. need a massive amount of electricity, and utilities are racing to build new generation and grid infrastructure to meet unprecedented demand growth — driven in significant part by these large load customers — while coming up with contract structures that protect their other customers. But several factors are working against utilities as they seek to manage risk, experts and industry sources say, potentially leaving them and their ratepayers or members vulnerable to stranded investments.” [Utility Dive, 3/6/26, link to article]

Interior bends on solar, but wind energy on public lands remains stalled “The Interior Department’s pivot to again consider some solar projects on federal lands hasn’t revived wind energy. A small wind farm partially on federal land in Wyoming that won approval from Interior in the final days of the Biden administration has sat in limbo for more than a year. The Two Rivers Wind Project needs a final sign-off from the Bureau of Land Management before it can start construction, said a local official following the development of wind energy in the region. It’s the only wind farm that had been in the BLM pipeline during the Biden administration that appears to still be viable at the agency under President Donald Trump, who regularly denounces wind energy as a scam.” [E&E News, 3/17/26, link to article]

Good news corner

SEIA: Solar energy added most capacity to the grid in 2025 “The U.S. solar industry installed 43 gigawatts (GW) of new capacity in 2025, making it the dominant source of new capacity added to the grid for the fifth straight year. Overall, solar and energy storage represent 79 percent of new capacity installed last year, according to the U.S. Solar Market Insight 2025 Year in Review report from the Solar Energy Industries Association (SEIA) and Wood Mackenzie. The report found 11 states set new annual installation records in 2025, and 12 states added over 1 GW of new solar capacity. Texas led all states with 11 GW of new installations. Deployment in Indiana and Utah has skyrocketed, with Indiana deploying nearly 3 GW, up from 1.6 GW in 2024. More than two-thirds of all solar capacity installed in 2025 was built in Texas, Indiana, Florida, Arizona, Ohio, Utah, and Arkansas, all of which are in the top 10 in solar capacity.” [Daily Energy Insider, 3/12/26, link to article]

How a tiny Texas town is using wind energy to help out senior citizens “A wonky tax deal with wind companies means more food and medical resources for older residents of ​’The Biggest Little Town in the World.’” [Canary Media, 3/17/26, link to article]

Weekly Clips 3.9-3.13

To:

staf@greenlightamerica.org

Subject

Weekly Big Picture News and Trends  3.9-3.13

Clean energy trends and big picture news

Setting the stage for US energy storage: A pv magazine feature by Phoebe Skok examines how sweeping import tariffs and the One Big Beautiful Bill Act reshaped the U.S. energy storage industry in 2025 and what developers are navigating heading into 2026. Trump's policy changes have made domestic manufacturing a necessity rather than a preference, forcing developers to absorb new political and financial risks. "We are no longer in an era of cheap imported batteries," said Zora Chung, CFO at battery optimization platform ReJoule, noting that heavy tariff implementation and tightening tax credit eligibility have reversed the long trend of falling foreign cell prices. "The cost of having a compliant battery system is significantly higher than before." Jon M. Williams, CEO of lithium-ion battery manufacturer Viridi Parente, said higher costs and tighter margins are pushing developers to weigh total lifecycle value over upfront capital cost, with permitting delays and insurance premiums hitting harder as a result. Eligibility for the 48E Investment Tax Credit — worth a 30% ITC — now hinges on strict limits for foreign-supplied components, generating significant demand for supply chain traceability. [PV Magazine, Mar 11 2026, link to article]

150+ Local Governments Have Moved to Block Battery Storage Development, New Database Reveals: At least 150 local governments across 17 states have enacted moratoriums, bans, or restrictive ordinances targeting battery energy storage systems, according to the first publicly available nationwide database of such restrictions, compiled by Delaware-based developer Carina Energy. New York leads the country with 98 moratoriums — 65% of the national total — concentrated in the Hudson Valley, Capital Region, and Long Island. States including Indiana, Michigan, Iowa, and Maine are seeing a rising wave of new restrictions as utility-scale battery projects expand into new markets. The firm’s BESS Moratorium Monitor tracks 17 states through weekly review of municipal agendas and ordinances and also tracks seven states that have enacted legal pathways allowing developers to bypass local moratoriums under certain conditions. “The moratorium problem is growing faster than the industry realizes,” said Malaquias Encarnacion, Managing Director of Carina Energy. “Finding 150 tells you that local opposition to battery storage is becoming systemic. This isn’t a handful of outliers anymore — it’s a new regulatory reality.” [GlobeNewswire / Carina Energy, Mar 10 2026, link to article]

How Will the War in Iran Affect Your Utility Bills?: Energy experts fear the Trump administration’s decision to attack Iran could set off a utility bill spiral similar to the one triggered by Russia’s invasion of Ukraine in 2022. Qatar has halted production at the world’s largest liquefied natural gas facility after Iranian drone strikes, and shipments through the Strait of Hormuz — through which 15% of the global oil supply and 20% of global LNG normally flow — have been cut off. The disruption is “wreaking havoc with global gas and LNG markets, even more so than oil,” according to Wood Mackenzie analysts. The situation compounds pre-existing rate pressure: U.S. electricity rates rose 5% nationwide in 2025, and in some states like Pennsylvania, nearly 9%. The article traces how growing LNG exports have tethered domestic gas prices to global market swings: “It’s U.S. consumers who are bidding against global consumers for the same gas,” said Clark Williams-Derry of the Institute for Energy Economics and Financial Analysis. LNG export facilities, by contrast, stand to profit. “It’s a gold rush,” Williams-Derry said. [Inside Climate News, Mar 5 2026, link to article]

Why Electricity Bills Are So High—and How the Blowback Could Hit Trump: A detailed Inside Climate News investigation finds that U.S. residential electricity rates rose an average of 5% in 2025, with consumers increasingly frustrated and politicians across the spectrum taking notice. The single most significant driver, found in every state, is utilities’ surge in infrastructure spending — long-lived equipment investment roughly doubled from $108.6 billion in 2015 to a forecasted $207.9 billion in 2025, with the largest one-year jump between 2024 and 2025. State-level renewable energy mandates play a modest role in the Northeast and Mid-Atlantic but are barely detectable elsewhere. Data centers absorb a disproportionate share of public blame, but researchers say the evidence is more complicated and varies substantially by state and utility. The deeper structural problem is utility companies’ financial incentive to maximize capital spending, since regulated returns are tied to infrastructure investment. [Inside Climate News, Mar 2 2026, link to article]

Good News Corner

Solar Power Surging in Michigan, Midwest as Renewables Rapidly Gain Ground: A new report from the Institute for Energy Economics and Financial Analysis finds that despite Trump administration efforts to boost coal and natural gas, renewables are still gaining market share at a “blistering” pace across the Midwest Independent System Operator region — and Michigan in particular is poised for major growth. Solar provided barely 1% of MISO’s power as recently as 2023 but had grown to 4.5% by 2025, driven largely by improvements in battery storage technology that allow utilities to dispatch renewable power on demand. Report coauthor Dennis Wamsted said solar’s trajectory in MISO mirrors that of Texas a decade ago. Michigan has more than 3,600 MW of new solar in the pipeline scheduled to come online by 2028 — the largest of any state in MISO. “It’s like a secret sauce,” Wamsted said of solar paired with storage. “You put them together and you have a competitor for any kind of coal or natural gas plant, because you now have dispatchable renewable power.” [Michigan Public, Mar 9 2026, link to article]

Weekly Clips 3.2-3.6

To:

staf@greenlightamerica.org

Subject

Weekly big picture news and trends 03/02-03/06

Clean energy trends and big picture news

BlackRock investor led consortium buying AES in $10.7 billion cash deal “AES Corporation is being sold for $10.7 billion cash in a deal that will potentially see the world’s largest commercial and industrial clean energy supplier enter private ownership. Blackrock-owned Global Infrastructure Partners leads the consortium that is acquiring AES’s global business, which includes US electric utilities in Indiana and Ohio, plus a major global renewable generation portfolio.” [PV Magazine, 3.3.2026, link to article]

Interior jump-starts solar energy permitting “The Interior Department could be softening its hard-line stance on solar energy. Interior is reviewing at least 20 commercial-scale projects that have languished in the permitting pipeline since President Donald Trump took office in January 2025, according to three agency career officials familiar with the process.” [E&E News, 2.26.2026, link to article]

Why bigger batteries can make sense for facilities: “Much of the upfront cost of installing a battery energy storage system is the same whether you put in a four-hour battery or an eight-hour battery. So if you envision using a BESS system for time-of-use energy arbitrage as well as for backup power, it might make sense to go with a bigger battery, a battery executive says…U.S. battery demand for stationary storage systems jumped by 29% in 2025, according to data from the Solar Energy Industries Association, or SEIA.” [FacilitiesDive.com, 03.04.2026, link to article]

Solar and wind PPA prices up 9% in 2025, set to continue rising with energy demand: “Prices for wind and solar power purchase agreements in North America each rose nearly 9% in 2025 over the previous year, according to data from LevelTen Energy’s marketplace released in January…However, LevelTen found the results of a November survey of developers with project pipelines totaling more than 230 GW “encouraging”: of projects expected to achieve operation before 2029, more than three-quarters were expected to retain access to tax credits, it said.”  [FacilitiesDive.com, 03.04.2026, link to article]

Good news corner

EIA: Some 11 gigawatts of coal, natural gas plants set to retire this year: [Daily energy Insider, 02.26.2026, link to article]

600+ GWh of US energy storage expected by 2030: Benchmark/SEIA: “By 2030, the consultancy expects more than 600 GWh of energy storage on the U.S. grid…Data centers could account for 83% of behind-the-meter commercial and industrial storage deployments by 2030, Benchmark said. Meanwhile, the market for residential storage grew 51% in 2025, according to Benchmark. It attributes that growth, in part, to the Section 25D tax credit’s expiration at the end of 2025, which pulled demand forward as customers raced to take advantage of the expiring incentives.” [UtilityDive.com, 03/03/2026, link to article]

Weekly Clips 2.23 - 2.27

To:

staf@greenlightamerica.org

Subject

Weekly big picture news and trends 2.23 - 2.27

Clean energy trends and big picture news

Wind, solar power face a common foe — creative local governments “USA TODAY’s analysis found by the end of 2025, 24% of counties nationwide had some impediment to new utility-scale wind and solar energy ‒ up from as few as 15% two years earlier. That calculation of local rules included outright bans, zoning restrictions, land-use rules or political stonewalls. It also required USA TODAY to set a standard about which regulations were restrictive enough to count as an impediment.” [USA Today, 02/21/26, link to article]

Clean energy bans skyrocket under Trump, USA TODAY analysis finds “USA TODAY reporting in 2023 found that at least 15% of U.S. counties had limited the construction of new wind or solar projects. Now, at least 24% of counties ‒ nearly one in four ‒ have restricted construction of these energy sources… President Donald Trump has led the charge, calling wind farms "ugly" and moving the country away from renewable sources of energy and back toward coal and oil.” [USA Today, 02/21/26, link to article]

New U.S. electric generating capacity expected to reach a record high in 2026 “U.S. power plant developers and operators plan to add 86 gigawatts (GW) of new utility-scale electric generating capacity to the U.S. power grid in 202... Solar power makes up 51% of the planned 2026 capacity additions, followed by battery storage at 28% and wind at 14%.” [EIA, 2/20/26, link to article]

AI is reshaping U.S. electricity demand faster than the grid can keep up “Data centers accounted for at least 60% of last year’s increase in U.S. electricity demand, straining an aged grid that struggles to keep up despite investments made in upgrades… Last year, electricity demand from data centers alone grew 21% compared with 2024, 150% in the past five years and 400% over the past decade.” [DailyEnergyInsider, 02/18/26, link to article]

Good news corner

Chart: Surprise — batteries got cheaper again last year “Yes, batteries got cheaper again last year… In 2025, the average price of a lithium-ion battery pack was $108 per kilowatt-hour, down 8% from the year prior... It’s the continuation of a long-standing trend: With the exception of 2022, battery prices have declined every single year since 2010, when BNEF began looking at the data.” [Canary Media, 02/06/2026, link to article]

Weekly Clips 2/17 - 2/20

To:

staf@greenlightamerica.org

Subject

Weekly Big Picture News & Trends 2/17 - 2/20

Clean Energy Trends and Big Picture News

Trump EPA repeals Endangerment Finding, stripping legal bedrock for solar and clean energy: “In a move described as the “single largest deregulatory action in U.S. history,” the EPA has rescinded the 2009 finding that greenhouse gases threaten public health, a decision that could dismantle the legal justification for federal solar incentives and emissions standards.” [PV Magazine, 2/12/26, link to article]

Navigating the OBBBA cliff for solar tax credits: The One Big Beautiful Bill Act (passed July 2025) moved up deadlines for utility-scale solar to keep full IRA tax credits — construction must begin by July 4, 2026 and be placed in service by Dec. 31, 2027 — while replacing the 5% Cost Safe Harbor with a stricter Physical Work Test for projects over 1.5 MW. “At a time when we need energy abundance, these rules create new federal red tape,” said Heather O’Neill, president and CEO of Advanced Energy United. Developers now face heavier documentation requirements including geotagged photos and unredacted contracts. [pv magazine USA, Feb 18 2026, link to article]

Fear of farmland loss slows solar and wind development: Rural opposition over loss of agricultural land is slowing both solar and wind projects across the country, even though agrivoltaics can give farmers extra income (one Texas rancher now runs 3,000 sheep under panels on 8,000 acres). Experts point out that urban sprawl actually threatens far more farmland than renewables ever will, but Trump administration cuts to USDA funding and REAP grants have stalled progress. [Canary Media, Feb 18 2026, link to article]

States push climate superfund bills despite Trump’s opposition: Maine, New Jersey, Illinois, Oregon, Rhode Island and Connecticut are advancing “climate superfund” legislation that would force oil and gas companies to pay for climate damages. “Climate superfund laws are based on the principle that if you make a mess, you clean it up,” said Jamie Flynn of the Conservation Law Foundation. Trump has called the bills “extortion” and issued an executive order opposing them. [Canary Media, Feb 17 2026, link to article]

The EPA’s biggest emissions-fighting tool is dead — at least for now: The Trump administration formally revoked the 2009 EPA endangerment finding that greenhouse gases endanger public health and welfare — the legal foundation for nearly all federal climate rules since 2007. “Most people have never heard of this safeguard — the ‘endangerment’ finding — but repealing it sends a clear message: this government doesn’t care,” said David Widawsky of the World Resources Institute. Court battles are expected. [Canary Media, Feb 13 2026, link to article]

Southern California air board rejected pollution rules after AI-generated flood of comments: An AI-powered campaign using the platform CiviClick generated more than 20,000 form emails that helped kill a South Coast AQMD proposal to phase out gas furnaces and water heaters. The rules would have cut nitrogen oxide emissions and prevented nearly 2,500 premature deaths and over 10,000 asthma cases. Experts called it “the next step in digital astroturfing.” [Los Angeles Times, Feb 17 2026, link to article]

Good News Corner

How battery deployment in the Eastern U.S. could scale as in Texas and California: Texas has already deployed nearly ten times more grid-scale battery storage than all four Eastern grid regions combined, proving batteries can handle rising peak demand. A new GridLab study outlines 15 concrete policy fixes to unlock the 140 GW of battery projects already sitting in Eastern interconnection queues. “Texas and California… have proved that grid-scale battery storage can effectively tackle rising peak demand,” said GridLab Executive Director Ric O’Connell. [pv magazine USA, Feb 18 2026, link to article]

With AI demands, can renewable energy be the economic future of rural Kansas?: Surging electricity needs from AI data centers (projected 25% jump in U.S. demand by 2030) are opening the door for wind and solar to bring tax revenue, construction jobs, permanent maintenance roles and landowner lease payments to declining rural counties. Kansas already gets nearly half its electricity from renewables. “We have excellent wind and we have excellent solar. More days of sunshine than San Diego, California,” said Lona DuVall, Finney County Economic Development director. [KCUR, Feb 17 2026, link to article]

Weekly Clips 2/9 - 2/13

To:

staff@greenlightamerica.org

Subject

Weekly big picture news and trends Feb. 9-13

Clean energy trends and big picture news

Energy Secretary Chris Wright pushes back on utilities’ call for more wind and solar “Utility companies looking to add every possible electron to the electric grid will not have the full backing of the Trump administration, as Energy Secretary Chris Wright said the United States needs reliable energy, not wind and solar power. Utility trade groups, large tech firms, and major manufacturers, as well as Republican and Democratic lawmakers, have called for the Trump administration to adopt an ‘all-of-the-above’ energy strategy, which would include renewable energy sources such as wind and solar, to meet the growing demand brought on by artificial intelligence and address soaring electricity prices.” [Washington Examiner, 2/6/26, link to article]

Doubling the pace of adding solar and storage in the PJM region could save $178 billion by 2035 “Adding 78 GW more solar and 54 GW more battery storage in the PJM grid region than projected in a status quo scenario would drive a 20% reduction in system costs and improve reliability through 2035, says a study. The study’s sponsor called for enabling policies to improve interconnection and permitting, with roles for both PJM and states.” [PV Magazine, 2/11/26, link to article]

Good news corner

Trump administration is now 0-5 in latest effort to halt offshore wind “All five offshore wind farms that had their operations suspended by a stop work order from the Department of the Interior in December have been cleared by federal courts to resume construction, with the fifth wind farm — Ørsted’s 924-MW Sunrise Wind — receiving a preliminary injunction” [Utility Dive, 2/3/26, link to article]

First Solar releases poll showing support for solar among GOP voters “When asked if all forms of electricity generation, including utility‑scale solar, should be allowed to compete fairly in the marketplace without political interference, 79% of GOP voters agreed. A slight majority of right-leaning voters — 51% — surveyed in a poll commissioned by utility-scale solar manufacturer First Solar said they favor the use of utility solar energy, with 30% opposing it and 19% declining to take a position.” [Utility Dive, 2/5/26, link to article]

Offshore wind showed up big during the East Coast’s brutal cold “Bone-chilling cold and Arctic winds gripped the northeastern U.S. over the past few weeks, straining electricity systems and raising power prices as people cranked up their heat. Now, as the region finally starts to thaw, early data shows how America’s offshore wind farms helped keep electricity flowing during the extreme-weather stretch.” [Canary Media, 2/12/26, link to article]
